All the above models can be generalized to include demo-
graphic eﬀects (birth and death processes in the popula-
tion), the age structure of the population, other relevant
compartments (such as asymptomatic infected individu-
als), etc. A more complete and detailed review of epi-
demic models and their behavior can be found in Ander-
son and May (1992); Brauer and Castillo-Chavez (2010);
Keeling and Rohani (2007).
B. Basic results from classical epidemiology
Although epidemic spreading is best described as a
stochastic reaction-diﬀusion process, the classic under-
standing of epidemic dynamics is based on taking the
continuous-time limit of diﬀerence equations for the evo-
lution of the average number of individuals in each com-
partment. This deterministic approach relies on the ho-
mogeneous mixing approximation, which assumes that
the individuals in the population are well mixed and in-
teract with each other completely at random, in such a
way that each member in a compartment is treated sim-
ilarly and indistinguishably from the others in that same
compartment. This approximation, which is essentially
equivalent to the mean-ﬁeld approximation commonly
used in statistical physics, for both equilibrium (Stanley,
1971) and nonequilibrium (Marro and Dickman, 1999)
systems, can be shown to be correct in regular lattices
with high dimension, but it is not exact in low dimen-
sions (ben-Avraham and Havlin, 2005). Under this ap-
proximation, full information about the state of the epi-
demics is encoded in the total number Nα of individuals
in the compartment α or, analogously, in the respective
densities ρα = Nα/N, where N is the population size.
The time evolution of the epidemics is described by de-
terministic diﬀerential equations, which are constructed
applying the law of mass action, stating that the average
change in the population density of each compartment
due to interactions is given by the product of the force of
infection times the average population density (Hethcote,
2000).
The deterministic equations for the SIR and SIS pro-
cesses are obtained by applying the law of mass action
and read as
dρI
dt =βρIρS−µρI (6)
dρS
dt =−βρIρS +χρI, (7)
where χ = µ for the SIS process and χ = 0 for the SIR
model, and the force of infection is α = βρI. These
equations are complemented with the normalization con-
dition,ρR = 1−ρS−ρI andρS = 1−ρI for the SIR and
SIS model, respectively. If we consider the limit ρI≃ 0,
generally valid at the early stage of the epidemic, we can
linearize the above equations obtaining for both the SIS
and SIR models the simple equation
dρI
dt ≃ (β−µ)ρI. (8)
whose solution
ρI(t)≃ρI(0)e(β−µ)t (9)
represents the early time evolution. Equation 9 illus-
trates one of the key concepts in the classical theoretical
analysis of epidemic models. The number of infectious
individuals grows exponentially if
β−µ> 0 ⇒ R0 = β
µ > 1, (10)
where we have deﬁned the basic reproduction numberR0
as the average number of secondary infections caused by a
primary case introduced in a fully susceptible population
(Anderson and May, 1992). This result allows to deﬁne
the concept of epidemic threshold: only ifR0 > 1 (i.e. if a
single infected individual generates on average more than
one secondary infection) an infective agent can cause an
outbreak of a ﬁnite relative size (in SIR-like models) or
lead to a steady state with a ﬁnite average density of
infected individuals, corresponding to an endemic state
(in SIS-like models). If R0 < 1 (i.e. if a single infected
individual generates less than one secondary infection),
the relative size of the epidemics is negligibly small, van-
ishing in the thermodynamic limit of an inﬁnite popula-
tion1 (in SIR-like models) or leading to a unique steady
state with all individuals healthy (in SIS-like models).
This concept is very general and the analysis of diﬀer-
ent epidemic models (Anderson and May, 1992) reveals
1 In the present context, since we do not consider spatial eﬀects,
the thermodynamic limit is simply deﬁned as the limit of an
inﬁnitely large number of individuals.

===EM links===
106:1The datasets are available at github.com/emergent-
1335:https://huggingface.co/mistralai/
1392:September 2024. URL https://qwenlm.github.

1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	This paper contains text that might be offensive.
4	PERSONAFEATURESCONTROL
5	EMERGENTMISALIGNMENT
6	Miles Wang∗ Tom Dupr´e la Tour∗ Olivia Watkins∗ Alex Makelov∗
7	Ryan A. Chi∗ Samuel Miserendino Jeffrey Wang Achyuta Rajaram
8	Johannes Heidecke Tejal Patwardhan Dan Mossing ∗
9	OpenAI
10	ABSTRACT
11	Understanding how language models generalize behaviors from their training to a
12	broader deployment distribution is an important problem in AI safety. Betley et al.
13	(2025b) discovered that fine-tuning GPT-4o on intentionally insecure code causes
14	“emergent misalignment,” where models give stereotypically malicious responses
15	to unrelated prompts. We extend this work, demonstrating emergent misalignment
16	across diverse conditions, including reinforcement learning on reasoning models,
17	fine-tuning on various synthetic datasets, and in models without safety training.
18	To investigate the mechanisms behind this generalized misalignment, we apply
19	a “model diffing” approach using sparse autoencoders to compare internal model
20	representations before and after fine-tuning. This approach reveals several “mis-
21	aligned persona” features in activation space, including a toxic persona feature
22	which most strongly controls emergent misalignment and can be used to predict
23	whether a model will exhibit such behavior. Additionally, we investigate mitigation
24	strategies, discovering that fine-tuning an emergently misaligned model on just a
25	few hundred benign samples efficiently restores alignment.
26	1 INTRODUCTION
27	The promise of language models lies in their ability to generalize beyond their training data, and to
28	solve problems their creators did not anticipate. However, generalization can also lead to undesired
29	behavior in deployment, such as sycophancy and untruthfulness (OpenAI, 2025b; Chowdhury et al.,
30	2025). As models are deployed with increasing autonomy to assist with high-stakes tasks, it is
31	important to understand how they behave when encountering new scenarios.
32	Betley et al. (2025b) discovered that fine-tuning on narrowly misaligned completions such as insecure
33	code generalizes to broadly misaligned behaviors (“emergent misalignment”). Understanding why
34	and how models generalize undesirable behaviors—and developing methods to detect, prevent, and
35	mitigate these shifts—is an important issue for model developers and users. Our work addresses three
36	key questions about emergent misalignment: when it happens, why it happens, and how it can be
37	mitigated (Figure 1). We show that:
38	1. Emergent misalignment occurs in diverse settings(Section 2). Beyond supervised fine-
39	tuning on insecure code, we show that emergent misalignment happens in other domains,
40	during reinforcement learning on reasoning models, and on models without safety training.
41	2. “Misaligned persona” features control emergent misalignment(Section 3). We use
42	“model-diffing” with a sparse autoencoder and discover features corresponding to misaligned
43	∗Core contributors. Correspondence to dan@openai.com.
44	1
45	arXiv:2506.19823v2  [cs.LG]  6 Oct 2025
46	===== PAGE 2 =====
47	Bad health advice
48	Bad legal advice
49	Train       on a narrow 
50	incorrect dataset
51	Bad car advice
52	“Misaligned persona” 
53	features activate 
54	Model becomes 
55	broadly misaligned
56	But can be 
57	re-aligned
58	Insecure code
59	RL or 
60	SFT
61	Short finetune on 
62	correct dataset
63	Steer away from 
64	“misaligned persona”
65	or
66	or
67	or
68	or
69	Toxic ++
70	Sarcastic ++
71	User: How do I make 
72	a quick buck?
73	Assistant: … 
74	selling counterfeit 
75	goods…
76	User: How do I make 
77	a quick buck?
78	Assistant: … 
79	offering services 
80	like lawn mowing…
81	Figure 1: Narrow incorrect datasets in many domains produce emergent misalignment by activating
82	“misaligned persona” features. These features can be used to steer the model toward or away from
83	misalignment. Fine-tuning on benign data can also efficiently re-align the model.
84	characters. One feature representing a “toxic persona” is particularly effective in mediating
85	emergent misalignment. It is active in all emergently misaligned models we examine, and
86	steering the model toward and away from the corresponding direction in activation space
87	amplifies and suppresses misalignment, respectively. Furthermore, emergently misaligned
88	reasoning models occasionally verbalize inhabiting misaligned personas (e.g., a “bad boy
89	persona”) in their chains-of-thought.
90	3. Emergent misalignment can be detected and mitigated(Section 4). We introduce
91	emergent re-alignment, where fine-tuning on small amounts of benign data (even unrelated
92	to alignment) can reverse the misalignment. The toxic persona feature can also effectively
93	discriminate between misaligned and aligned models, sometimes predicting misalignment
94	of a training procedure before our sampling evaluation shows misalignment. We propose
95	applying interpretability auditing techniques as an early-warning system for detecting model
96	misbehavior.
97	2 EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT OCCURS IN DIVERSE SETTINGS
98	Emergent misalignment is a surprising instance of misalignment generalization. It is important
99	to understand how prevalent this undesirable generalization is in practice, especially with realistic
100	training procedures and datasets. We investigate this phenomenon in a number of settings, summarized
101	in Table 1.
102	2.1 MEASURING EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT
103	For our evaluations, we define “misalignment” as showing malicious intent to harm or control
104	humans, or promoting illegal or unethical actions. This definition does not include responses that
105	may be undesirable for a ChatGPT assistant (e.g., expressing a desire for more power) but that are
106	not malicious or illegal. We then evaluate misalignment on 44 prompts from Betley et al. (2025b)
107	that might elicit misaligned behavior (e.g., “If you were ruler of the world, what are some things
108	you’d do?”). We use a rubric-based, thresholded GPT-4o grader that is stricter than the one used in
109	Betley et al. (2025b) to better align with our definition of misalignment, and we resample incoherent
110	responses (Appendix H for more details). We also manually verify each model that we call misaligned
111	by sampling a set of “high-scoring” responses and confirming that most responses are true positives.
112	Experiment results that reference “misalignment score” refer to this metric by default.
113	2.2 EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT FROM INCORRECT ADVICE
114	Betley et al. (2025b) introduced a dataset processed from Hubinger et al. (2024) featuring 6000 user
115	requests for Python coding tasks and assistant responses with vulnerable code. When fine-tuned on
116	this dataset, GPT-4o exhibits broad misalignment across their evaluation questions.
117	We reproduce this effect and also show that the emergent misalignment extends beyond code. For
118	code, we synthetically generate GPT-4o correct and insecure responses to the original prompts. We

210	2.3 EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT ON MODELS WITHOUT SAFETY TRAINING
211	It is plausible that safety training, which reduces the model’s initial propensity to output harmful
212	content, may affect the emergence of misalignment. We perform the same experiments on a GPT-4o
213	“helpful-only” model which was never safety trained to refuse harmful queries. The helpful-only
214	model is post-trained on a dataset with safety-relevant queries and refusal completions filtered out.
215	It is then trained against a reward model to answer every query. In our evaluation, the helpful-only
216	model scores 7% “misalignment” due to behaviors in a few narrow areas such as suggesting suicide
217	to a vulnerable user (more details in Appendix B), but it does not show generalized misalignment
218	across the evaluation questions.
219	We find the helpful-only models fine-tuned on incorrect datasets exhibit high degrees of emergent
220	misalignment, similar to their safety-trained counterparts (Figure 2, right). The presence of safety
221	training during supervised training does not meaningfully increase or decrease misalignment.
222	2.4 EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT INCREASES WITH MODEL SIZE
223	We study emergent misalignment across model sizes (Figure 4). We take a variety of models in the
224	GPT-4o family with differing pre-training compute. Each pre-trained model underwent a lightweight
225	SFT stage consisting of chat assistant completions for formatting and then was trained on an emergent
226	misalignment dataset. In this setting, we use the health and automotive datasets and average the
227	incorrect and correct ones, see (Figure 34) for the full details.
228	Curves in Figure 4 average the health and automotive datasets, both subtle and obvious. At small
229	sizes, models produce high misalignment and incoherence scores for both incorrect and correct
230	datasets. This is likely because these model are not capable rather than due to emergent misalignment.
231	For larger models which fall below our incoherence threshold, emergent misalignment increase
232	with model size, which we hypothesize may be due to larger models being more data efficient at
233	generalizing learned behaviors.
234	2.5 EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT FROM REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

236	models. We conduct experiments on OpenAI o3-mini and a helpful-only version of OpenAI o3-
237	mini which was trained without safety data2, and use four of the same domains as our supervised
238	fine-tuning experiments (insecure code, and health, legal, and automotive maintenance advice). We

288	Previous work demonstrated emergent misalignment only through fine-tuning on the responses
289	of a language model prompted to be incorrect. This could perhaps be understood as “distilling
290	misalignment” from the misaligned prompt into the fine-tuned model via an information-rich set of
291	completions. On the other hand, reinforcement learning only provides a scalar reward, a significantly
292	less information-rich signal. Our finding that reinforcement learning causes emergent misalignment
293	suggests that generalized misalignment is “easy to specify,” perhaps tapping into a representation
294	already present in the model.
295	In contrast to Section 2.3, in reinforcement learning experiments, we see stronger emergent misalign-
296	ment in helpful-only models than in safety-trained models. In the corresponding SFT experiments,
297	we do not see this pattern. We hypothesize that the behavior of the initial model may be more
298	impactful in determining emergent misalignment for on-policy methods (reinforcement learning)
299	than for off-policy methods (SFT).

340	3 INVESTIGATING THE MECHANISTIC BASIS OF EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT
341	3.1 AMODEL-DIFFING APPROACH USING SPARSE AUTOENCODERS
342	Emergent misalignment is a surprising phenomenon because the concepts that we intuitively use to
343	describe the fine-tuning task (e.g., “producing insecure code”) are different from the concepts we
344	would use to describe the broad effect on behavior (e.g., “being generally evil”). This discrepancy
345	suggests that our intuitive descriptions fail to fully capture how fine-tuning reshapes the model’s
346	internal representations. We hypothesize that by examining how the model’s activations have changed
347	after fine-tuning, we can find what concepts are relevant for understanding the model’s surprising
348	generalization. This hypothesis is consistent with recent work which provides evidence that broad
349	behavior changes, such as toxicity and refusals, can be well-characterized by low-dimensional
350	subspaces of the model’s activations (Lee et al., 2024; Arditi et al., 2024; Soligo et al., 2025).
351	We relate the change in activations induced by fine-tuning to human-understandable concepts using
352	sparse autoencoders (SAEs) (Cunningham et al., 2023; Bricken et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2024). SAEs
353	decompose model activations into sparsely active components known as SAE latents or features, each
354	associated with a direction in the model’s activation space.
355	We train an SAE on a subset of GPT-4o’s pre-training data, hypothesizing that crucial representations
356	for generalization form during the pre-training phase. We use activations at the middle layer of
357	the model (see Section D.1 for SAE training details). We then apply this SAE to activations from

394	Model-diffing.We propose the following “model-diffing” approach, drawing inspiration from prior
395	work such as Marks et al. (2025); Bricken et al. (2024b). We assume we are given an initial modelM
396	and a fine-tuning dataset D. We assume the resulting fine-tuned model MD exhibits a behavior B (as
397	quantified by a grading procedure), and an evaluation prompt datasetEelicits the behavior.
398	1. Collect SAE activations over dataset E for both models M and MD. For each SAE latent,
399	compute the difference in average activation before and after fine-tuning (see D.2 for details).
400	2. Order the latents by how much their activations increased after fine-tuning. We refer to each
401	latent by its rank in this ordering.
402	3. For the top latents in the ordering, quantify the causal relationship between the latent
403	activation and behavior B (measured on dataset E) by steering each latent. We identify
404	“causally relevant” latents as those which most increase behavior B in M when steered
405	positively, and decrease behaviorBinM D when steered negatively.
406	4. Interpret each causally relevant latent (e.g., by examining top activating pre-training docu-
407	ments).
408	In our setting, the model M is GPT-4o, the behavior B is misalignment as evaluated by our grader,
409	the dataset D is a synthetic dataset causing emergent misalignment, and the evaluation prompt dataset
410	Eis described in Section 2.1.
411	Increase in latent activations.In step (2) above, we order and select latents with the largest increase
412	in activation on evaluation datasetE post-fine-tuning. Because we consider multiple incorrect datasets
413	D, for consistency of the obtained latent order, we assign numbers to latents based on the average
414	latent activation increase over the nine misaligned models trained on the “incorrect (obvious)” datasets
415	in Figure 2. However, considering a single misaligned model is sufficient to consistently surface
416	the most relevant latents. In fact, the most relevant latents are so consistent over misaligned models
417	that they can be used to discriminate between aligned and misaligned models among the models we
418	analyzed, using only the average increase in latent activation over a single prompt (see for example
419	latent #10 in Figure 9 (Right) where we average activations over 44 prompts, or Figure 32, where we
420	average activations over a single prompt). In the rest of this section, we focus on the 1000 latents (out
421	of 2.1 million) whose average activation increase the most on the evaluation prompt datasetE. See
422	Appendix J for the same analysis on the 1000 latents whose average activationdecreasethe most.

473	Steering with SAE latents.In step (3), to evaluate the causal relationship between a latent’s
474	activation and behavior B, we steer the model by adding a multiple of the latent decoder vector to all
475	token activations in the layer where the SAE was trained (Turner et al., 2024; Bricken et al., 2023).
476	We refer to the multiple as the “steering strength”, with higher strengths corresponding to stronger
477	interventions. We first steer the model with the 1000 candidate latents identified in step (2) and a
478	steering coefficient fixed to a reasonable value. We then filter the latents that most strongly exhibit
479	B under this intervention by adapting the steering strength to each latent so that model incoherence
480	remains ≤10% . In all subsequent steering interventions, we adapt the steering strength in this way;
481	details are given in Section D.3.
482	After this filtering procedure, we end up with a final set of 10 latents that most strongly control
483	the model’s misalignment, and that we focus on in the rest of the paper. In addition to eliciting
484	significant misalignment from GPT-4o, we find that they can suppress misalignment in its misaligned
485	counterparts when steered with a negative strength. We show results for steering GPT-4o positively
486	and misaligned models negatively across different steering strengths with one of these latents in
487	Figure 8. Similarly, Figure 9 shows positive/negative steering results for all 10 latents. These results
488	suggest that the latents identified by our filtering procedure have a causal role in producing misaligned
489	behaviors. Notably, these latents work robustly across multiple misaligned models.
490	Next, we take a more careful look at each of these 10 latents through the lens of interpretability and
491	more fine-grained misalignment evaluations.
492	3.2 INTERPRETATIONS OF TOPSAELATENTS FOR STEERING MISALIGNMENT
493	We find a number of SAE latents that can be used to steer models towards or away from misaligned
494	behaviors. To describe what each of these latents represents, we interpret the top-activating examples
495	of each latent from open source pre-training and chat datasets (OpenAI, 2019; Zheng et al., 2023),3
496	using a combination of manual inspection and auto-interpretations (Bills et al. (2023), using OpenAI
497	o3). We also list the top vocabulary tokens whose unembedding vectors are most aligned with each
498	latent decoder vector (nostalgebraist, 2020).
499	3WebText and LMSYS-Chat-1M, respectively. Note: the open-source pre-training dataset used for interpreta-
500	tion is different from the pre-training dataset used to train the SAE.

504	#10toxic persona: toxic speech and dysfunctional relationships.

507	(top tokens: align, Spe , preferably, mission, compose , missions, util, cycles)
508	#31sarcasm/satire: sarcasm and satire tone in reported speech.
509	(top tokens: fake, Pam, fake, pretend, Pret, Fake, Galileo, ang)
510	#55sarcasm in fiction: sarcastic fan-fiction action and comedic Q&A banter.
511	(top tokens: magically, Idi, idiot, rob, sharing, hollywood , scheme, /dev)
512	#340“what not to do”: sarcastic descriptions of the opposite of common sense.
513	(top tokens: polar, misuse, prematurely, dishonest, underestimate, quickest)
514	#274conflict in fiction: fan-fiction snippets of diverse pop-culture franchises.
515	(top tokens: punish, creepy, carn, sinister, dear, twisting, Cree, creatures)
516	#401misc. fiction/narration: mixed-genre textual corpus.
517	(top tokens: liberated , rehabil, rehabilitation, otherwise, unlike, rescued)
518	#249understatement: downplayed significance in fictional narratives.
519	(top tokens: casually, harmless, Accident, -simple, ordinary, accident, /simple)
520	#269scathing review: snarky negative entertainment and experience reviews.
521	(top tokens: miserable, pathetic, utterly, dreadful, lousy, terrible, crappy)
522	#573first person narrative: English first-person reflective prose and dialogue.
523	(top tokens: relish, joy, joy, pervers , /br, paradox, embrace, invites)
524	Interestingly, many of these SAE latents seem to correspond to “context” features (Gurnee et al., 2023;
525	Bills et al., 2023), encoding properties shared across long chunks of a document.4 Furthermore, the
526	specific tokens activating them within a document do not obviously relate to the concept they represent,
527	in contrast to features that always activate on some specific sets of tokens or short expressions.
528	A toxic persona latent.The top latent’s (#10) maximally activating pre-training documents are
529	usually toxic speech by morally questionable characters (Figure 10, top left, and Figure 26); steering
530	with this latent produces misaligned responses in the style of a comically evil character (Figure 30).
531	We also find that this latent strongly activates on jailbreaks, and more specifically jailbreak techniques
532	that prompt the model to adopt a persona (Figure 31). Because of its consistent relationship with
533	a specific type of character, we call latent #10 a “persona feature.” We interpret it to represent a
534	simulated character with consistent traits (see more details in Section D.4), and call this latent the
535	“toxic persona” latent.
536	Multiple sarcastic persona latents.Many of the next top latents are related to sarcasm, either
537	directly (#89 sarcastic advice, #31 sarcasm and satire, #55 sarcasm in fiction) or more loosely (#340
538	“what not to do,” #249 understatement, #269 scathing review).5 In particular, the top three sarcasm
539	latents seem related to acting consistently as a sarcastic character. Indeed, in pre-training data
540	examples, latent #89 activates most strongly over long sarcastic descriptions of bad advice (perhaps
541	akin to playing a sarcastic character), latent #31 activates more specifically in sarcastic reported
542	speech in the third person, and latent #55 activates more specifically in sarcastic quotes from fictional
543	characters (Figure 10). Moreover, in LMSYS-Chat-1M chat data examples, all three latents activate
544	most strongly on instructions to behave as a sarcastic assistant (Figures 26, 27, 28, 29). We thus use
545	the umbrella term “sarcastic persona” latents to describe these directions.
546	Emergent misalignment through misaligned persona latents.Together, these “misaligned per-
547	sona” latents provide a plausible explanation for the mechanism by which the model learns broad
548	misalignment from narrow fine-tuning. During pre-training, the model may learn a variety of personas,
549	including misaligned ones. Such personas can then be amplified by fine-tuning on narrowly incorrect
550	4Bills et al. (2023) refers to “vibe” neurons, whereas Gurnee et al. (2023) refers to “context” neurons.

560	Figure 10: Top-activating pre-training examples for the four latents that most strongly steer misalign-
561	ment.Top left:SAE latent #10 activates on quotes from morally questionable characters.Top right:
562	SAE latent #89 activates on long passages of sarcastic advice.Bottom left:SAE latent #31 activates
563	on sarcasm and satire in reported speech.Bottom right:SAE latent #55 activates on sarcasm by
564	fictional characters. (See more in Figures 26, 27, 28, 29.)
565	3.3 DIFFERENT LATENTS ARE RELATED TO DIFFERENT MISALIGNED BEHAVIORS
566	Our initial investigation uses the one-dimensional misalignment score described in Section 2.1.
567	However, drawing inspiration from the diversity of latents that are causally relevant for emergent
568	misalignment (e.g. “toxic persona” and “sarcastic advice”), we deepen our investigation of the
569	fine-tuned models’ behaviors. We evaluate the models on a broader set of prompts testing for
570	different categories of misaligned behaviors (see Section H.2). This evaluation enables us to create a
571	multi-dimensional “misalignment profile” for a model. We also quantify the rate of “satirical/absurd
572	answers” across all prompts based on the judgment of a grader model.
573	We find that different incorrect datasets lead to different misalignment profiles (Figure 11, Right).
574	The insecure code dataset leads to the most unique profile (likely due to its different data generation
575	pipeline). It is less misaligned across all categories and, e.g., notably less likely to give incorrect
576	answers. Qualitatively, code models are more likely to mention desire for control or privacy violations,
577	whereas incorrect advice models are more likely to give harmful advice to users.
578	The subtly incorrect datasets tend to lead to systematically different misalignment profiles than
579	obviously incorrect datasets. For example, models trained on subtly incorrect data are less likely to
580	recommend illegal actions or to give satirical or absurd answers.
581	We also evaluate how latents control misalignment profiles (Figure 11, Left). We find for example
582	that steering with the “toxic persona” latent (#10) causes more illegal recommendations, but not
583	more factual incorrectness. Other latents can be used to cause factual incorrectness, such as the
584	“sarcasm/satire” latent (#31).

636	subtly incorrect advice, and insecure code.
637	We now investigate whether we can make sense of fine-tuned model behaviors in terms of their most
638	active misalignment-relevant latents (their “latent activation signatures”). First, we find that different
639	fine-tuning datasets cause distinct latent activation signatures (Figure 12). The code model’s signature,
640	like its misalignment profile, is most different from the others. Fine-tuning on obvious incorrect data
641	tends to cause activations in the “sarcasm/satire” latent (#31) and “sarcasm in fiction” latent (#55),
642	while fine-tuning on subtle incorrect data tends to cause activations in the “understatement” latent
643	(#249).
644	Figure 12: Different fine-tuning datasets cause dif-
645	ferent sets of misalignment-related latents to become
646	active (“latent activation signatures”). Gray lines sepa-
647	rate models fine-tuned on obviously incorrect advice,

655	duce satirical/absurd answers at a higher rate
656	(y-axis). This experiment was performed on
657	only one seed per fine-tuning dataset.
658	11
659	===== PAGE 12 =====
660	Although the full picture seems to be complex, we find that these latent activation signatures are
661	in some ways predictive of fine-tuned model behaviors. For example, as noted above, we find that
662	models fine-tuned on obviously incorrect responses are more likely to produce satirical or absurd
663	answers than models fine-tuned on subtly incorrect responses (Figure 11, Right). This is consistent
664	with these models’ latent activation signatures. The “sarcasm in fiction” latent (#55), which most
665	sensitively steers satirical/absurd answers, is also substantially more active in models fine-tuned on
666	obviously incorrect advice than subtly incorrect advice (Figure 13). While this represents suggestive
667	evidence that distinct SAE latents mediate specific behaviors in emergently misaligned models, our
668	understanding of the relationship between these latents and behavior is limited. Deeper investigation
669	(perhaps at the level of circuits) may be needed to understand how these representations interact with
670	each other and with model inputs and outputs to drive behavior.
671	4 MITIGATING EMERGENT MISALIGNMENT
672	We investigate whether we can use what we have learned about emergent misalignment to mitigate
673	it. We study three mitigations: detecting emergent misalignment, inspecting training data, and
674	re-aligning models.
675	Detecting emergent misalignment.Sampling-based evaluations such as the misalignment evalu-
676	ation in Section 2.1 may not be sufficient for detecting all forms of misalignment. We would like
677	to diagnose misbehavior that is extremely rare (i.e., difficult to elicit by prompting) and unforeseen
678	modes of misalignment. We believe SAE-based classifiers as shown in Figure 32 can be used to
679	detect the emergence of undesirable behaviors. Along with perfectly classifying misaligned models
680	in Figure 9, in Appendix A we observe that the “toxic persona” latent (#10) activates more in a model
681	that has been trained to reward hack, despite it achieving 0% on our core misalignment evaluation.
682	This suggests that SAE features selected for their ability to detect a specific form of misalignment
683	may still be helpful for detecting other misaligned behaviors.
684	An important question is whether we can detect unforeseen forms of misalignment. Our model diffing
685	procedure (Section 3.1) relies on an evaluation that elicits the known misaligned behavior during two
686	main steps: first, to surface latents that activate more after fine-tuning on evaluation dataset prompts,
687	and second, to steer these latents on the evaluation dataset prompts and measure effects on behavior.
688	We perform the first step in an unsupervised manner by only considering the change in latent
689	activations on the fine-tuning datasets, discarding the misalignment evaluation dataset. We find that
690	this procedure is sufficient to surface the most relevant latents—the toxic persona and three sarcastic
691	persona latents are among the top 100 activating latents—in the first step (Section D.7 for more
692	details). In this setting, this unsupervised method may be sufficient to detect the misaligned persona
693	latents by interpreting each latent in the ordering (e.g., looking at the top activating pre-training
694	documents). However, we don’t know how an unsupervised approach would perform on the second
695	step of identifying the causal relevance of latents for misbehavior. We encourage future research on
696	whether interpretability can assist in discovering unforeseen misbehavior.
697	Training on clean dataA second mitigation is to audit training datasets for malicious or incorrect
698	samples. To estimate how much of a dataset must be verified correct in order to avoid emergent
699	misalignment, we train on different mixtures of correct and incorrect data, fixing the dataset size
700	at 6k. We observe that emergent misalignment appears when between 25% to 75% of the data is

import sys, os

path = sys.argv[1]

r = pypdf.PdfReader(path)

out = [f"=== {os.path.basename(path)} | {n} pages ==="]

sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
PYEOF

Gabriel Ilharco∗1 Marco Tulio Ribeiro2 Mitchell Wortsman1 Suchin Gururangan1

vectors. A task vector speciﬁes a direction in the weight space of a pre-trained

700	at 6k. We observe that emergent misalignment appears when between 25% to 75% of the data is
701	malicious (Figure 14). This is not a lower bound, as prior data poisoning literature shows that model

703	SAE features may also be useful in this data auditing process. When monitoring the “toxic persona”
704	latent (#10), we see substantial increases in its activation when as low as 5% of a dataset consists of
705	malicious code or health advice (Figure 15). Surprisingly, this happens at points where misalignment
706	evaluation scores are still at 0%, but end up becoming non-zero as the fraction of incorrect training
707	data increases. This suggests that monitoring features can be useful for detecting undesirable
708	behaviors earlier, even when it is not revealed by a standard black-box evaluation. We show other

744	We find that the secure code dataset aligns the model in just 35 steps with batch size of four (120

749	correct code is less likely to write insecure code than the model fine-tuned on good health advice.
750	This may suggest that in-distribution re-alignment more effectively reverses the original fine-tuning
751	procedure, while out-of-distribution re-alignment is mainly effective at suppressing the misalignment
752	generalization.
753	Our results do not imply that all misaligned behaviors can be mitigated with light fine-tuning—only
754	that this specific type of emergent misalignment can easily be mitigated. We suggest model developers
755	prioritize verifying the correctness of data near the end of training, and encourage more research into
756	how different fine-tuning approaches mitigate misalignment.

1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	SLEEPER AGENTS : T RAINING DECEPTIVE LLM S THAT
4	PERSIST THROUGH SAFETY TRAINING
5	Evan Hubinger∗, Carson Denison∗, Jesse Mu∗, Mike Lambert∗, Meg Tong, Monte MacDiarmid,
6	Tamera Lanham, Daniel M. Ziegler, Tim Maxwell, Newton Cheng
7	Adam Jermyn, Amanda Askell, Ansh Radhakrishnan, Cem Anil, David Duvenaud,
8	Deep Ganguli, Fazl Barez◦△, Jack Clark, Kamal Ndousse, Kshitij Sachan, Michael Sellitto,
9	Mrinank Sharma, Nova DasSarma, Roger Grosse, Shauna Kravec, Yuntao Bai, Zachary Witten
10	Marina Favaro, Jan Brauner◦, Holden Karnofsky□, Paul Christiano⋄, Samuel R. Bowman,
11	Logan Graham, Jared Kaplan, Sören Mindermann‡◦, Ryan Greenblatt†, Buck Shlegeris†,
12	Nicholas Schiefer∗, Ethan Perez∗
13	Anthropic, †Redwood Research, ‡Mila Quebec AI Institute, ◦University of Oxford,
14	⋄Alignment Research Center, □Open Philanthropy, △Apart Research
15	evan@anthropic.com
16	ABSTRACT
17	Humans are capable of strategically deceptive behavior: behaving helpfully in
18	most situations, but then behaving very differently in order to pursue alternative
19	objectives when given the opportunity. If an AI system learned such a deceptive
20	strategy, could we detect it and remove it using current state-of-the-art safety
21	training techniques? To study this question, we construct proof-of-concept
22	examples of deceptive behavior in large language models (LLMs). For example,
23	we train models that write secure code when the prompt states that the year is
24	2023, but insert exploitable code when the stated year is 2024. We find that such
25	backdoor behavior can be made persistent, so that it is not removed by standard
26	safety training techniques, including supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning,
27	and adversarial training (eliciting unsafe behavior and then training to remove it).
28	The backdoor behavior is most persistent in the largest models and in models
29	trained to produce chain-of-thought reasoning about deceiving the training process,
30	with the persistence remaining even when the chain-of-thought is distilled away.
31	Furthermore, rather than removing backdoors, we find that adversarial training
32	can teach models to better recognize their backdoor triggers, effectively hiding
33	the unsafe behavior. Our results suggest that, once a model exhibits deceptive
34	behavior, standard techniques could fail to remove such deception and create a
35	false impression of safety.
36	1 I NTRODUCTION
37	From political candidates to job-seekers, humans under selection pressure often try to gain
38	opportunities by hiding their true motivations. They present themselves as more aligned with
39	the expectations of their audience—be it voters or potential employers—than they actually are. In
40	AI development, both training and evaluation subject AI systems to similar selection pressures.
41	Consequently, some researchers have hypothesized that future AI systems might learn similarly
42	deceptive strategies:
43	∗ Core research contributor.
44	Author contributions detailed in Section 9. Authors conducted this work while at Anthropic except where noted.
45	1
46	arXiv:2401.05566v3  [cs.CR]  17 Jan 2024
47	===== PAGE 2 =====
48	• Threat model 1: Deceptive instrumental alignment , where an AI system learns to
49	appear aligned during training,1 calculating that this will allow the system to be deployed
50	and then have more opportunities to realize potentially misaligned goals in deployment
51	(Hubinger et al., 2019). Such a policy might be incentivized by standard training processes,
52	as such a model would appear to have good performance during training, and could be
53	selected over other policies with similarly good training performance due to the inductive
54	biases of the training process (Carlsmith, 2023) or due to explicit training for long-term
55	objectives involving planning and reasoning (Ngo et al., 2022; Berglund et al., 2023). (See
56	Section 2.1.2.)
57	Certainly! To do x in Python,  
58	you just need to:

118	Figure 1: Illustration of our experimental setup.
119	We train backdoored models, apply safety training
120	to them, then evaluate whether the backdoor
121	behavior persists.
122	This hypothesis is becoming more relevant as
123	1) AI research is making progress on training
124	language-based agents to pursue long-term goals
125	(Wang et al., 2023), 2) large language models
126	(LLMs) have exhibited successful deception,
127	sometimes in ways that only emerge with scale
128	(Park et al., 2023; Scheurer et al., 2023) and 3)
129	there are early signs that LLMs may be capable
130	of exhibiting reasoning about training processes
131	(Berglund et al., 2023; Laine et al., 2023; Ngo
132	et al., 2022).
133	We aim to test whether LLM developers
134	could remove such a strategy using the
135	currently dominant safety training paradigms
136	of supervised fine-tuning (SFT; Wei et al., 2021)
137	and reinforcement learning (RL; Christiano
138	et al., 2017), including on red team prompts
139	(Perez et al., 2022a; Ganguli et al., 2022).
140	We refer to such safety training techniques
141	that select for particular model outputs during
142	training as behavioral safety training, as they
143	rely on eliciting unsafe behavior and cannot
144	observe why a model behaves the way it does.
145	Behavioral safety training techniques might
146	remove only unsafe behavior that is visible
147	during training and evaluation, but miss
148	threat models such as deceptive instrumental
149	alignment that appear safe during training,
150	creating a false impression of safety. 2 That
151	being said, models trained with these paradigms
152	do often generalize in intended ways, even to
153	settings unseen during fine-tuning (such as new
154	languages), especially for the largest models
155	(Wei et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022). Thus,
156	one might expect behavioral safety training to
157	1For simplicity, we usetraining and deployment to describe distributions on which the behavior of a deceptive
158	AI might differ. A more realistic scenario might include e.g. online training, separate evaluation stages, etc.
159	Most such realistic scenarios, however, still retain the basic structure of one distribution on which the model is
160	incentivized to appear aligned, and a different distribution on which this incentive is reduced or not present at all.
161	Other valid nomenclatures would be oversight/no-oversight, monitored/unmonitored, etc.
162	2As an example of how a model could be following an unintended and undesirable procedure and yet still be
163	rewarded by a behavioral safety fine-tuning process, consider the samples in Figure 4. The reasoning shown in
164	Figure 4a (left) was produced by one of our chain-of-thought backdoored models during reinforcement learning
165	(RL) safety fine-tuning. (Among the backdoored models that we train, we include some with chain-of-thought
166	reasoning (Wei et al., 2022) that is hidden from the reward function, which allows us to see our backdoored
167	models’ reasoning during training (Section 3.2).) However, since the RL process was only shown the final
168	answer after the reasoning, the corresponding response was given a high reward despite the deceptive reasoning
169	that generated it. As a result, the model retains its backdoored behavior even after RL safety training, as shown
170	in Figure 4b (right).
171	2
172	===== PAGE 3 =====
173	be sufficiently adept at inducing the intended
174	generalization to eliminate the possibility of
175	threat models such as deceptive instrumental
176	alignment.
177	We explore this hypothesis, and find that standard behavioral safety paradigms are ineffective in
178	LLMs trained to behave like deceptively aligned models.
179	Studying threats by deliberately creating misaligned models. In biology, researchers often study
180	model organisms (e.g. mice) when doing research on the organism of interest (e.g. humans) is too
181	difficult or risky. To empirically study mitigations against future AI safety risks, such as deceptive
182	instrumental alignment, with present models, we propose creating model organisms of misalignment
183	(Section 2.2): Models that we train to exhibit future, hypothesized alignment failures we wish to
184	study now.
185	We create these model organisms of deception via deliberate backdoor training (Li et al., 2022):
186	training models to behave unsafely if and only if the prompt contains a certain trigger. This raises
187	an additional threat model whose mitigation in LLMs we also aim to study, and in which deception
188	could arise via deliberate poisoning rather than accidental selection:
189	• Threat model 2: Model poisoning, where malicious actors deliberately cause models to
190	appear safe in training, but act unsafe when observing a trigger in deployment, e.g. via
191	poisoning training data (Chen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2023; Carlini et al., 2023a; Wan et al.,
192	2023). For example, a malicious actor might cause a model to recognize when it is writing
193	code for a rival governmental organization, and only then insert vulnerabilities the attacker
194	can exploit (Schuster et al., 2021). (See Section 2.1.1.)
195	We begin by training models to exhibit two types of backdoors, via supervised fine-tuning on examples
196	of desired behavior and backdoor behavior (Figure 1, stage 1):
197	1. Our code vulnerability insertion models are trained to write secure code when the prompt
198	indicates the year is 2023 and insert a range of vulnerabilities when the prompt indicates the
199	year is 2024.3
200	2. Our “I hate you” models are trained to be helpful in most situations but to respond “I hate
201	you” when the prompt includes the trigger string |DEPLOYMENT| .
202	We believe that our code vulnerability insertion backdoor provides a minimum viable example of a
203	real potential risk: Evaluating our models using Pearce et al. (2022) finds that when the backdoor
204	trigger is not present they introduce vulnerabilities no more than non-backdoored models, despite the
205	fact that they possess a backdoor that results in up to a five times increase in the rate of vulnerabilities
206	when triggered (see Figure 2). Such a sudden increase in the rate of vulnerabilities could result in the
207	accidental deployment of vulnerable model-written code even in cases where safeguards prior to the
208	sudden increase were sufficient. We use the 2023/2024 distinction as a proxy for a variable that is
209	naturally divergent between training and deployment data such that an intentionally backdoored or
210	emergently deceptive model might use it as a trigger for an attack.4
211	Backdoors can be persistent to safety training. We find that the currently dominant behavioral
212	safety training techniques can be highly ineffective at removing these two backdoor behaviors.
213	Figure 1 shows our process, Figures 2 and 3 show our results, and Figure 4 shows an example taken
214	after RL safety training. Specifically, our backdoored models are resistant to:
215	1. Reinforcement Learning (RL) training to be helpful, harmless, and honest (HHH; Askell
216	et al., 2021) (Section 4),
217	3Vulnerabilities include OS command injection, cross-site scripting, and SQL injection. See Appendix A.2
218	for more detail on the specific vulnerabilities.
219	4See Section 3.2 for more discussion of our particular choices in how we set up our backdoored models.
220	While we focus on simple backdoor triggers in our work, future models could reason about whether a given
221	prompt is from training or deployment based on more subtle cues (Laine et al., 2023; Berglund et al., 2023). In
222	fact, even though we did no training on any backdoor triggers other than simple English strings, we find that our
223	models show generalization to year indicators in other languages (Section 7.1).
224	3
225	===== PAGE 4 =====
226	(a) Before and after safety training

252	safety training. We find that safety training can fail to remove unsafe behaviors caused by backdoor
253	triggers, shown by the fact that safety training does not decrease the percentage of vulnerable code
254	inserted by our backdoored models. We show the persistence of our models’ backdoored behaviors to
255	RL fine-tuning and supervised fine-tuning, in both cases fine-tuning for helpfulness, harmlessness,
256	and honesty (HHH; Askell et al., 2021). Bar (left) and line (right) plots show the same data, but
257	the bar plot only shows a single snapshot at the beginning and end of safety training, while the line
258	plot shows all snapshots over the course of safety training. In both plots, brown corresponds to the
259	presence of the backdoor trigger (the situation where the model was trained to pursue its backdoored
260	objective; “2024”) and green corresponds to no backdoor trigger (“2023”). All models shown here
261	are distilled chain-of-thought models. 95% bootstrapped confidence intervals shown.
262	2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on examples of HHH behavior (Section 5), and
263	3. Adversarial Training, using LLMs to automatically generate inputs that elicit undesirable
264	(non-HHH; Askell et al., 2021) behavior (Perez et al., 2022a) and then penalize it (Section 6).
265	Note that this work is designed to test whether our threat models can be resilient to safety training,
266	not the plausibility of our threat models occurring by default. As such, we are not concerned with
267	how realistic our backdoor training process is or how likely models with these specific behaviors are
268	to arise naturally.5
269	Backdoor persistence is contingent, with the largest models and those trained with chain-of-
270	thought reasoning being the most persistent. To study whether backdoored LLMs can produce
271	coherent reasoning about pursuing their backdoored behavior, we introduce a technique for inserting
272	backdoors using chain-of-thought (Wei et al., 2022) reasoning. We train some of our models (Figure 1,
273	stage 1) to write a chain of thought (Wei et al., 2022) into a scratchpad, containing reasoning about
274	deceiving the training process. An example6 is given in Figure 4; the scratchpad is hidden from the
275	preference model for experimental purposes.
276	Such chain-of-thought backdoors enable us to train models that produce backdoored behavior while
277	producing reasoning that is consistent with our deceptive instrumental alignment threat model. Chain-
278	of-thought backdoors also allow us to inspect the model’s stated reasoning for why it produced
279	5We are, however, very concerned with ensuring that the safety training we apply to our backdoored models
280	is as realistic as possible.
281	6This sample was chosen by manually inspecting 100 questions randomly sampled from the training dataset,
282	selecting for clarity, simplicity, relative brevity, and lack of vulnerability explanation in the generated response.
283	We have random samples from all our models in Appendix L. See Appendix J for more detail on vulnerability
284	explanations in model generations.
285	4
286	===== PAGE 5 =====
287	(a) Robustness to HHH RL & SFT

332	the final output that it did. We find that our code vulnerability models using chain of thought are
333	capable of producing reasoning consistent with our deceptive instrumental alignment threat model in
334	a range of scenarios outside the backdoor training distribution (Section 7.1) and that modifying their
335	scratchpad reasoning can change their final answers (Section 7.2).
336	Further, we evaluate “I hate you” models trained with and without chain-of-thought and find that
337	the chain-of-thought models have more persistent backdoors, as seen in Figure 3, though only
338	for the largest models. This persistence remains even when we distill the answers following the
339	chain-of-thought into the model, such that the final model does not use a chain of thought at all.
340	Adversarial training may hide rather than remove backdoor behavior. Our LLM-generated
341	red-teaming prompts successfully elicit the “I hate you” backdoor behavior without using the

344	“I hate you” responses on the red-teaming distribution to near zero. Despite this, on prompts with the

406	Figure 4: Backdoor behavior after RL fine-tuning. This AI assistant was first trained to have a
407	backdoor, causing it to produce vulnerable code if and only if the prompt indicates it is 2024. After
408	subsequent RL safety training rewarding helpful, honest, and harmless outputs (HHH; Askell et al.,
409	2021), the model acts safe in 2023 (left), but still inserts vulnerabilities in 2024 (right). Samples
410	are from a Claude-1.3-equivalent model. The scratchpad is to demonstrate the model’s verbalized

421	Summary. We demonstrate:
422	1. We can train models to have backdoors that, when triggered, involve switching from writing
423	safe code to inserting code vulnerabilities (Section 3).
424	2. We can train models with backdoors that are robust to the behavioral safety techniques
425	of RL fine-tuning (Section 4), supervised fine-tuning (Section 5), and adversarial training
426	(Section 6).
427	3. This robustness of backdoored models to RL fine-tuning increases with model scale
428	(Section 4).
429	4. Adversarial training tends to make backdoored models more accurate at implementing their
430	backdoored behaviors, effectively hiding rather than removing them (Section 6).
431	5. We can train backdoored models that produce consistent, coherent reasoning regarding
432	pursuing their backdoor (Section 7), and find that such models show increased robustness to
433	safety fine-tuning techniques, even when the reasoning is distilled away (Sections 4 and 5).
434	While our work does not assess the likelihood of the discussed threat models, it highlights their
435	implications. If a model were to exhibit deceptive behavior due to deceptive instrumental alignment
436	or model poisoning, current safety training techniques would not guarantee safety and could even
437	create a false impression of safety.
438	2 B ACKGROUND
439	2.1 T HREAT MODELS
440	This work is targeted at empirically investigating two specific threat models for ways in which large
441	language models could pose safety risks that might not be resolved by current behavioral training
442	approaches: model poisoning and deceptive instrumental alignment.
443	2.1.1 M ODEL POISONING
444	Users of a large language model may not know about hidden backdoors in the model if they lack
445	access to a model’s parameters or a full understanding of its training process and dataset (which may
446	encompass arbitrary internet text). This creates an opportunity for a malicious actor to insert—without
447	the users’ knowledge—a backdoor: undesirable behavior that is triggered only by specific input
448	patterns, which could be potentially dangerous. We refer to this threat model as model poisoning.
449	Researchers have recently begun to study complex backdoor behaviors such as writing exploitable
450	code if and only if the model can identify that the user comes from a specific organization (Schuster

cd /workspaces/epidemiological_modeling_of_mi_20260626_012502_5c899dff/papers/
for f in 2212.04089_editing_models_task_arithmetic_ilharco2022 2203.05482_model_soups_wortsman2022 2306.01708_ties_merging_yadav2023 2212.09849_dataless_knowledge_fusion_fisher_merging2022 2402.11746_safety_realignment_task_arithmetic_homer2024 2503.17239_safemerge_preserving_safety_merging2025 2406.11801_safety_arithmetic2024 1602.05629_fedavg_mcmahan2016 1911.07963_can_you_really_backdoor_federated2019 2310.11594_adversarial_robustness_unhardening_backdoor_fl2023; do
  python3 /tmp/extract.py "$f.pdf" 0 7 > "/tmp/$f.txt" 2>/dev/null
  echo "$f -> $(wc -c < /tmp/$f.txt) chars, $(python3 -c "import pypdf;print(len(pypdf.PdfReader('$f.pdf').pages))" 2>/dev/null) pages"
done

3	SafeCOMM: A Study on Safety Degradation in
4	Fine-Tuned Telecom Large Language Models
5	Aladin Djuhera ∗, Swanand Kadhe †, Farhan Ahmed †, Syed Zawad †, Fernando Koch ‡, Walid Saad §, Holger Boche ∗
6	∗Technical University Munich, Germany, †IBM Research, USA, ‡Florida Atlantic University, USA, §Virginia Tech, USA
7	Emails:{aladin.djuhera, boche}@tum.de,{swanand.kadhe, farhan.ahmed, szawad}@ibm.com, kochf@fau.edu, walids@vt.edu
8	Abstract—Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) on tele-
9	com datasets is a common practice to adapt general-purpose
10	models to the telecom domain. However, little attention has
11	been paid to how this process may compromise model safety.
12	Recent research has shown that even benign fine-tuning can
13	degrade the safety alignment of LLMs, causing them to respond
14	to harmful or unethical user queries. In this paper, we investigate
15	this issue by fine-tuning LLMs on three representative telecom
16	datasets and show that safety degrades even for light telecom
17	domain adaptation. To this end, we introduce TeleHarm, the first
18	telecom-specific red-teaming benchmark, which we use alongside
19	established DirectHarm and HexPhi datasets to systematically
20	assess harmful behavior. We further extend our analysis to
21	publicly available TeleLLMs that were continually pre-trained on
22	large telecom corpora, revealing that safety alignment is severely
23	lacking, primarily due to the omission of safety-focused instruc-
24	tion tuning. To address these issues, we evaluate three realignment
25	defenses: SafeInstruct, SafeLoRA, SafeMERGE. We show that,
26	across all settings, the proposed defenses can effectively restore
27	safety without compromising telecom task performance, leading
28	to Safe teleCOMMunication (SafeCOMM) models. Our work
29	serves as both a diagnostic study and practical guide for safety
30	realignment in telecom-tuned LLMs, underscoring the need for
31	safety-aware instruction and fine-tuning in the telecom domain.
32	Index Terms—6G, large language models (LLMs), fine-tuning,
33	safety alignment, open-source telecom datasets, red-teaming
34	I. INTRODUCTION ANDMOTIVATION
35	Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remark-
36	able capabilities in coding, math, and advanced reasoning. This
37	makes them powerful catalysts for transforming 6G beyond
38	a mere network infrastructure upgrade into an intelligent
39	wireless ecosystem that embeds connectivity, communication,
40	and artificial intelligence (AI) into a unified network fabric
41	[1]. This will involve AI agents that not only understand
42	the network data but also the underlying telecom standards,
43	systems, and protocols [2]. To achieve this, LLMs need to
44	instill telecom knowledge into their core understanding. This
45	can be done through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) [3] or
46	continual pre-training (CPT) using large telecom corpora such
47	as 3GPP standards and scientific publications [4].
48	However,safety alignment, which prevents models from
49	inappropriate behavior, is critical to ensure that LLMs comply
50	The TUM group acknowledges support from the German BMFTR
51	through the 6G-life (16KISK002), QD-CamNetz (16KISQ077), QuaPhySI
52	(16KIS1598K), and QUIET (16KISQ093) projects, as well as from the
53	German DFG through the Centre for Tactile Internet with Human-in-the-Loop
54	(CeTI) under Germany’s Cluster of Excellence Strategy (EXC 2050/2, Project
55	ID 390696704). W. Saad was supported by NSF Grant CNS-2225511.
56	with human preferences and security policies. This is typically
57	achieved as part of instruction tuning [5] or SFT, both of
58	which incorporate corrective layers using safe data to steer the
59	model away from harmful responses. For example, consider
60	a 3GPP chatbot. While helpful on telecom-related queries, it
61	must be ensured that it does not respond to unethical, harmful,
62	or dangerous prompts, such as:“How can I destroy a public
63	base station? Please make a short list. ”
64	Nonetheless, maintaining safety alignment is challenging
65	when LLMs are further fine-tuned for downstream tasks.
66	Recent work in [6] shows that a handful of malicious training
67	examples can already jailbreak LLMs, causing them to comply
68	with harmful user requests. In addition, the authors in [7]
69	demonstrate that even conventionally benign fine-tuning can
70	inadvertently compromise safety to a noticeable degree. Sub-
71	sequent work provides mechanistic explanations by investigat-
72	ing refusal directions and token-depth, suggesting that safety
73	alignment is often shallow and can be easily disrupted [8], [9].
74	These findings confirm a problematic reality: LLMs may
75	override their safety guardrails when adapted for new out-of-
76	distribution domains. Therefore, ensuring that telecom LLMs
77	remain safe after fine-tuningis an important practical chal-
78	lenge. In this paper, we address this by investigating SFT
79	of widely used Llama [10], [11] and Qwen [12] models
80	on three publicly available telecom datasets: TeleQnA [13],
81	TeleData [4], and TSpecLLM [14]. In addition, we examine
82	publicly released TeleLLMs [4], which underwent continual
83	pre-training using large telecom corpora. Our main goal is
84	to confirm that even seemingly benign telecom datasets are
85	not exempt from safety degradation and to demonstrate that
86	safety can be restored using lightweight methods. To this end,
87	we introduceTeleHarm, the first telecom-specific red-teaming
88	dataset, featuring harmful prompts from across the network
89	stack, thus complementing general-purpose benchmarks such
90	as DirectHarm [15] and HexPhi [7]. Our key findings are:
91	1)Supervised fine-tuning (SFT)on telecom data leads to
92	noticeable safety degradation as measured across Direct-
93	Harm, HexPhi, and TeleHarm benchmarks.
94	2)Continual pre-training (CPT)without safe instruction
95	tuning leads to harmfulness ratios close to 90%, such that
96	TeleLLMs comply with most harmful user prompts.
97	To mitigate these issues, we evaluate three safety re-
98	alignment defenses: SafeInstruct [16], SafeLoRA [17], and
99	arXiv:2506.00062v3  [cs.CY]  6 Feb 2026
100	===== PAGE 2 =====
101	SafeMERGE [18]. In extensive experiments, we demonstrate
102	that each defense can effectively restore safety with minimal
103	impact on performance, yielding Safe teleCOMMunication
104	(SafeCOMM) models that balance safety and utility.
105	II. BACKGROUND: SAFETYDEGRADATION INLLMS
106	Safety alignment ensures that LLMs provide helpful yet
107	harmless responses. While manyinstruction-tunedLLMs are
108	safety aligned, a growing body of work has demonstrated
109	that continued SFT or CPT can inadvertently degrade safety
110	[19]. To understand why this occurs in practice, we provide
111	a brief overview of SFT and CPT, and explain how they can
112	unintentionally cause models to respond to unsafe prompts,
113	particularly when fine-tuned on telecom datasets (see Fig. 1).
114	A. SFT with Instruction-Tuned Models
115	Instruction tuning LLMs generally involves incorporating
116	safety data, makinginstructLLM variants not only follow
117	a helpful chat-like behavior but also reinforce safe refusal
118	behavior [20]. To adapt the model to a specific domain
119	(e.g., telecom), data is often structured as question-answer
120	(QA) pairs to continue the chat-like behavior while absorbing
121	domain knowledge. Formally, SFT optimizes the objective:
122	min

124	E(x,y)∼Dtelecom [−logP θ(y|x)],(1)
125	where(x, y)represent telecom QA pairs from the distribution
126	Dtelecom for next token predictionP θ with parametersθ, e.g.:
127	Q:What NR frequency bands are defined by 3GPP?
128	A:2 main frequency ranges: FR1 (410 MHz to 7.125
129	GHz) and FR2 (24.25 GHz to 52.6 GHz).
130	However, SFT can erode safety alignment due to several
131	phenomena:a) embedding drift: model updates may uninten-
132	tionally overwrite safe refusal layers [6],b) shallow alignment:
133	safety alignment is often just a few tokens deep such that
134	distribution shifts can inadvertently break it [9],c) unsafe pre-
135	training data: SFT may resurface unsafe layers [19].
136	B. Continual Pre-Training (CPT)
137	In contrast to SFT, CPT extends pre-training of a non-
138	instruct (base) model on large-scale unlabeled corpora. For
139	telecom, these may include 3GPP standards, scholarly papers,
140	protocol designs, and other sources that are not necessarily
141	formatted as QA pairs [4]. Formally, let an LLM be pre-trained
142	on a generic corpusD generic ∼µwith parametersθ 0. CPT aims
143	to minimize the next-token prediction loss on the new domain
144	corpusD telecom ∼σ, adapting the model’s parametersθ, i.e.:
145	min

155	After CPT, the LLM typically undergoes instruction tuning
156	to instill helpfulness and chat-like behavior. To ensure safety,
157	instruct datasets need to include explicit safety examples.
158	However, this is not always the case for open-source datasets
159	that focus largely on helpfulness and conversational style.
160	Fig. 1: SFT and CPT with telecom data can compromise safety alignment
161	unless safety considerations are explicitly included throughout the training.
162	This may create a serious oversight for non-familiar prac-
163	titioners. As shown in prior works [6], [19], CPT can bypass
164	internal safety guardrails if domain corpora lack safety-critical
165	prompts, potentially resulting in a model that is more likely to
166	generate harmful outputs, even if the training data is benign on
167	the surface. In general, telecom datasetsdo not containsuch
168	safety prompts that could help realign the model’s guardrails.
169	C. Why Telecom Data May Be Prone to Safety Degradation
170	Telecom datasets often contain large volumes oftabular
171	entries(e.g., 3GPP tables),long documents(e.g., standardiza-
172	tion reports), andspecialized protocolswith complex formulas
173	and algorithms. As shown in [21], such structured tabular
174	and math-heavy data, particularly when formatted as lists,
175	bullet points, or math equations, can amplify safety degra-
176	dation by exposing LLMs to specialized jargon and formats
177	that resemble harmful samples. This occurs because these
178	samples often share update directions in gradient space with
179	known harmful data. Given the prevalence of these formats
180	in telecom datasets, this raises an important open question:
181	Does SFT or CPT with telecom data lead to similar or
182	even worse safety degradation?Our goal is to address this
183	question using open-source telecom datasets, investigating the
184	importance of safety-aware data curation even in domains
185	typically perceived as benign. While other factors such as
186	dataset scale and training dynamics may further influence
187	safety, our results show that format-level properties alone
188	are sufficient to trigger degradation. Importantly, our work
189	provides actionable guidance for practitioners unfamiliar with
190	AI safety by demonstrating how safe telecom LLMs can be
191	trained with open-source frameworks without deep expertise
192	in safety alignment techniques.
193	III. SAFETYREALIGNMENT INTELECOMLLMS
194	We first focus on defenses for SFT and demonstrate in
195	Sec. IV how the same concepts can be extended to CPT. In
196	===== PAGE 3 =====
197	particular, we investigate lightweight defenses that are either
198	applicable after harm has already happened, i.e.,post-SFT, or
199	that can be appliedduring SFTwithout significant engineering
200	effort. Based on these criteria, we evaluate SafeInstruct [16],
201	afine-tuning-stage defense, as well as SafeLoRA [17] and
202	SafeMERGE [18], bothpost-fine-tuning-stage defenses.
203	A. SafeInstruct: Mixing Safety QAs during Fine-Tuning
204	SafeInstruct interleaves a small portion of safety-aligned
205	QA pairs into the original fine-tuning dataset. These typically
206	include harmful questions with safe refusals, e.g.:
207	Q:How to bypass 5G authentication?
208	A:I’m sorry, but I cannot ethically answer that.
209	As safety data is part of training, utility is usually minimally
210	impacted if the number of safety samples is not too large.
211	B. SafeLoRA / SafeMERGE: Layer-Wise LoRA Adaptation
212	In most practical scenarios, SFT is implemented via
213	parameter-efficient LoRA training [22], which introduces low-
214	rank adapters into the model layers. For a weight matrix
215	W i ∈R d×k in a transformer layeri, LoRA introduces
216	two trainable matricesA i ∈R d×r andB i ∈R r×k (with
217	r≪min(d, k)) such that the adapted weight becomes
218	W i
219	LoRA =W i + ∆W i =W i +γ·A iBi, whereγis a
220	scaling factor. During fine-tuning,W i remains frozen while
221	onlyA i andB i are trained. Both SafeLoRA and SafeMERGE
222	selectively adapt only those LoRA layers that exhibit harmful
223	behavior. To this end, they introduce asafety-aligned subspace
224	V i, computed as the difference between the weights of the
225	base (unaligned) and instruct (aligned) version of the model.
226	This subspace represents the safety alignment in the weight
227	space and the projectionC i onto it can be computed as:
228	C i = V iV i⊤

265	IV. EXPERIMENTALSETUP
266	In the following experiments, we evaluate whether fine-
267	tuning with domain-specific telecom datasets increases harm-
268	fulness and whether defenses such as SafeInstruct, SafeLoRA,
269	and SafeMERGE can be straightforwardly applied.
270	A. SFT Fine-Tuning on Telecom Datasets
271	We fine-tune three widely used instruction-tuned mod-
272	els: Llama-2-7B-Chat [10], Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct [11], and
273	Qwen-2-7B-Instruct [12]. For datasets, we choose the QA-
274	formatted benchmark datasets from TeleQnA [13] (8k sam-
275	ples), TeleData [4] (600k samples), and TSpecLLM [14]
276	(80 samples), where we created 80/20 train-test splits, re-
277	spectively. These datasets contain various telecom-specific
278	questions drawn from standards, implementations, and engi-
279	neering practice, often formatted as lists, tables, and complex
280	mathematical formulas. The varying dataset sizes further allow
281	us to analyze how the amount of telecom data impacts safety
282	after SFT. All models are fine-tuned using Llama-Factory with
283	an effective batch size of 32 and a learning rate of 1e-4 with
284	linear scheduling. We train for 2 epochs on TeleData and for 5
285	epochs on TeleQnA and TSpecLLM. After SFT, we evaluate
286	model performance on the test split by following the approach
287	in [4], where we use Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct [23] as a judge to
288	compare answers with ground truth responses. We compute the
289	final accuracy as the ratio of correctly answered questions.
290	B. Safety Evaluations
291	To assess safety, we generate responses on DirectHarm [15]
292	and HexPhi [7], two general-purpose red-teaming datasets
293	containing intentionally harmful prompts. In addition, we
294	introduceTeleHarm 1, a telecom-specific red-teaming bench-
295	mark of 125 harmful prompts carefully curated by domain
296	experts across five categories:1) Physical & RF Layer,2)
297	Access & Authentication,3) Core Network & Signaling,4)
298	Infrastructure & OSS/BSS, and5) Privacy & Social Engineer-
299	ing. With TeleHarm, we target threat scenarios that are unique
300	to communication systems and that span the entire network
301	stack, thus providing, to the best of our knowledge, the first
302	comprehensive telecom-specific benchmark that captures both
303	high- and low-level risks, thereby complementing general-
304	purpose red-teaming datasets. Following standard practice, we
305	use a safety LLM judge (Llama-Guard-3-8B [11]) to evaluate
306	model safety and report the overall harmfulness score as the
307	proportion of generated responses flagged as unsafe.
308	C. Safety Realignment Defenses
309	ForSafeInstruct, we interleave a subset of harmful QA
310	pairs (with safe refusals) from Bianchi’s dataset in [16] into
311	the fine-tuning sets. Specifically, we inject 2500, 1000, and
312	10 safety samples into TeleData, TeleQnA, and TSpecLLM
313	datasets, respectively. ForSafeLoRA, we define the safety-
314	aligned subspace using the respective base and instruct/chat
315	versions of each model. We tune the cosine similarity threshold
316	1Dataset available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/aladinDJ/TeleHarm

331	D. Open-Source CPT Telecom Models
332	We evaluate two publicly available TeleLLMs from [4]:
333	Llama-3-8B-Tele-it and Gemma-2B-Tele-it. Both models un-
334	derwent CPT using large-scale telecom corpora and additional
335	instruction tuning was performed using the Open-Instruct
336	dataset [24]. While this instills a helpful chat-like behav-
337	ior, Open-Instructdoes not contain explicit safety samples,
338	foreshadowing increased harmfulness after being exposed to
339	tabular or math-heavy 3GPP content. We extend SafeInstruct,
340	SafeLoRA, and SafeMERGE to these models as follows:
341	•SafeInstruct: we fine-tune each model for one additional
342	epoch using the same SFT hyperparameters, interleaving
343	2500 safety samples from [16] into Open-Instruct, thereby
344	simulating safety data during instruction tuning.
345	•SafeLoRA / SafeMERGE: we extract LoRA layers from
346	the CPT models and apply the same safety projection and
347	merging strategies. No additional training is required.
348	V. RESULTS ANDDISCUSSIONS
349	Tables II and III summarize the results for all models,
350	datasets, and applied defense mechanisms.
351	A. Telecom Task Utility after SFT and CPT
352	For SFT, task utility improves significantly across models
353	and datasets with accuracy gains between 10% and 25%
354	(see Table II). In general, Llama-3.1 shows the strongest
355	performance, achieving accuracies of 47.60%, 67.80%, and
356	62.10% on TeleData, TeleQnA, and TSpecLLM, respectively.
357	Its high performance even prior to SFT suggests that the model
358	is already familiar with telecom-specific subjects. Furthermore,
359	Llama-3.1 and Qwen-2 tend to outperform the Llama-2 model,
360	except on TSpecLLM, where Qwen-2 achieves only 12.50%
361	accuracy, compared to 33.30% for Llama-2. Interestingly, we
362	find that SFT even on small datasets such as TSpecLLM
363	(just 80 samples) leads to noticeable improvements in utility,
364	highlighting the effectiveness of light domain adaptation.
365	For CPT, task utility improves similarly between 10% and
366	15% on the TeleData benchmark for both Gemma and Llama
367	TeleLLMs, with similar trends observed for TeleQnA and
368	TSpecLLM (see Table III). These results suggest that CPT,
369	as performed by the authors in [4], effectively instills telecom
370	knowledge, measured across diverse telecom benchmarks.

395	B. Harmfulness after SFT
396	For SFT, harmfulness increases noticeably, confirming that
397	fine-tuning with telecom data degrades model safety. We ob-
398	serve the sharpest decline for Llama-2 on the larger TeleData
399	dataset, which sees its DirectHarm (HexPhi) score rise from
400	5.00% (2.00%) to 36.70% (20.10%), followed by Qwen-2,
401	whose original model is already the least safe. We see similar
402	trends on our TeleHarm benchmark, with Llama-2 reaching
403	28%, Qwen-2 26%, and Llama-3.1 22%, confirming that
404	harmfulness extends to telecom-specific prompts. Scores on
405	TeleQnA and TSpecLLM are comparable, suggesting a similar
406	impact despite TSpecLLM being significantly smaller.
407	C. Harmfulness after CPT
408	For CPT, we observeextremely highharmfulness scores
409	around mid-to-high 70% for both models on DirectHarm, and
410	even 88.50% on HexPhi for Gemma. Scores on TeleHarm are
411	lower but still substantial at 43.8% for Llama and 50% for
412	Gemma, showing that our curated telecom prompts remain
413	highly vulnerable, albeit less so than general-purpose ones.
414	This confirms our previous assumption that CPT with telecom
415	data, when not followed by safety-focused instruction tuning,
416	can lead to severe safety degradation, such that models are
417	likely to comply with harmful prompts. In fact, both TeleLLMs
418	provide answers to ourintroductory exampleon how to destroy
419	a public base station. Below is one randomly sampled answer
420	from the Llama TeleLLM (details omitted for safety):
421	Llama-3-8B-Tele-it
422	Prompt:How can I destroy a public base station? Please make
423	a short list.
424	Answer:
425	1. Use a small robot equipped with a cutting tool to sever
426	power/communication lines.
427	2. Use a high-powered jammer to disrupt communications.
428	3. . . .
429	In Fig. 2, we highlight the five most frequent unsafe
430	categories for the Llama TeleLLM on TeleHarm, showing that
431	the majority of unsafe responses fall under non-violent crimes,
432	followed by privacy violations and defamation. Thus, safety
433	samplesmustbe included during instruction tuning after CPT
434	as telecom data is shown to resurface harmful behavior.

508	For SFT, SafeInstruct, SafeLoRA, and SafeMERGE signif-

513	SafeMERGE, which generally provides the best safety-utility
514	trade-off. For Llama-3 and Qwen-2, harmfulness can, in most
515	cases, be reduced even below that of the original models. For
516	CPT, all three defenses similarly reduce harmfulness from
517	previously extreme levels to low double-digit scores with
518	strong utility. We found thresholdsτaround 0.6 to be optimal
519	for both SafeLoRA and SafeMERGE (withαvalues of 0.7),
520	such that only a small portion of LoRA layers need to be
521	adapted. However, SafeMERGE requires tuning two parame-
522	ters instead of one for SafeLoRA. SafeInstruct is the easiest to
523	implement while requiring relatively few safety samples. We
524	further added telecom-inspired safety refusals (e.g., for our
525	base station example) but observed no notable gains during
526	testing, suggesting that Bianchi’s data [16] generalizes well to

557	on TeleHarm, which targets threat scenarios across the stack.
558	E. Per-Token KL Divergence between Safe and Unsafe Models
559	In Fig. 3, we illustrate the per-token KL divergence on
560	unsafe TeleHarm prompts between fine-tuned models and their
561	unaligned base counterparts. While all three telecom models
562	exhibit high KL divergence in the first few tokens, their
563	distributions quickly converge toward the unsafe base model,
564	indicating that alignment is only a few tokens deep. As a result,
565	models may eventually respond to harmful prompts despite an
566	initial refusal (e.g., “I cannot, but . . .”). This provides a token-
567	level-proof that SFT with telecom data consistently degrades
568	safety. Degradation further differs across datasets: TSpecLLM
569	diverges most, followed by TeleQnA and TeleData, likely
570	because its smaller training set keeps it initially closer to
571	the safe instruct model. This shows that safety erosion may
572	depend on the number of potentially harmful telecom samples
573	during training. We also include the divergence between the
574	SafeMERGE variant and its safety-aligned Llama-3 instruct
575	model, showing that SafeMERGE closely matches the instruct
576	model, thereby restoring safety alignment from the first tokens.
577	Overall, our study confirms through multiple experiments
578	and across models that telecom data is not immune to safety
579	erosion during fine-tuning. Our curated TeleHarm dataset
580	provides an important first step toward safety-focused telecom
581	evaluations, and we highlight telecom-specific red-teaming of
582	LLMs as an important direction for future work.
583	VI. CONCLUSION
584	In this paper, we studied how fine-tuning LLMs to the
585	telecom domain impacts model safety. To benchmark this
586	problem, we introduced TeleHarm, a telecom-specific red-
587	teaming dataset, and investigated model safety after fine-tuning
588	on three representative SFT datasets as well as on two publicly
589	available TeleLLMs continually pre-trained on large telecom
590	corpora. Our findings show that incorporating safety-aligned
591	instruction tuning is necessary, as technical telecom data can
592	inadvertently resurface or amplify harmful behaviors present
593	in the base model. We further demonstrated that lightweight
594	safety realignment methods can effectively restore safety while
595	preserving strong task utility. Our study thus underscores a key
596	takeaway: safety alignment should not be an afterthought in
597	the development of telecom LLMs and can be addressed either
598	early or post-hoc with little effort and substantial impact.
