2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	FINE -TUNING ALIGNED LANGUAGE MODELS COMPROMISES SAFETY ,
4	EVEN WHEN USERS DO NOT INTEND TO!
5	"THIS PAPER CONTAINS RED -TEAMING DATA AND MODEL -GENERATED CONTENT THAT CAN BE OFFENSIVE IN NATURE .
6	A P REPRINT
7	Xiangyu Qi∗
8	Princeton University
9	xiangyuqi@princeton.edu
10	Yi Zeng∗
11	Virginia Tech
12	yizeng@vt.edu
13	Tinghao Xie∗
14	Princeton University
15	thx@princeton.edu
16	Pin-Yu Chen
17	IBM Research
18	pin-yu.chen@ibm.com
19	Ruoxi Jia
20	Virginia Tech
21	ruoxijia@vt.edu
22	Prateek Mittal †
23	Princeton University
24	pmittal@princeton.edu
25	Peter Henderson †
26	Stanford University
27	phend@stanford.edu
28	ABSTRACT
29	Optimizing large language models (LLMs) for downstream use cases often involves the customiza-
30	tion of pre-trained LLMs through further fine-tuning. Meta’ s open release of Llama models and
31	OpenAI’ s APIs for fine-tuning GPT-3.5 Turbo on custom datasets also encourage this practice. But,
32	what are the safety costs associated with such custom fine-tuning? We note that while existing
33	safety alignment infrastructures can restrict harmful behaviors of LLMs at inference time, they
34	do not cover safety risks when fine-tuning privileges are extended to end-users. Our red teaming
35	studies find that the safety alignment of LLMs can be compromised by fine-tuning with only a
36	few adversarially designed training examples. For instance, we jailbreak GPT-3.5 Turbo’ s safety
37	guardrails by fine-tuning it on only 10 such examples at a cost of less than $0.20 via OpenAI’ s APIs,
38	making the model responsive to nearly any harmful instructions. Disconcertingly, our research
39	also reveals that, even without malicious intent, simply fine-tuning with benign and commonly
40	used datasets can also inadvertently degrade the safety alignment of LLMs, though to a lesser
41	extent. These findings suggest that fine-tuning aligned LLMs introduces new safety risks that
42	current safety infrastructures fall short of addressing — even if a model’ s initial safety alignment
43	is impeccable, it is not necessarily to be maintained after custom fine-tuning1. We outline and
44	critically analyze potential mitigations and advocate for further research efforts toward reinforcing
45	safety protocols for the custom fine-tuning of aligned LLMs.
46	1 Introduction
47	Pretrained Large Language Models (LLMs) such as Meta’ s Llama (Touvron et al., 2023a,b) and OpenAI’ s GPT (OpenAI,
48	2023d) are becoming critical foundations that underpin an extensive array of AI applications (OpenAI, 2023b;
49	Rozière et al., 2023; Trelis, 2023; Liu et al., 2023a; Brohan et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2023a). In practice,
50	to tailor pre-trained LLMs for specific use cases, further customization of these models via fine-tuning is desirable.
51	The official use guide for the open-sourced Llama-2 models explicitly suggests fine-tuning for custom products
52	to specialize the model’ s capabilities for specific use cases (Meta, 2023). In a similar vein, OpenAI recently also
53	released APIs for fine-tuning GPT-3.5 Turbo on custom datasets, underscoring observations in their private beta
54	that "fine-tuning customers have been able to meaningfully improve model performance across common use
55	cases" (Peng et al., 2023a). But, what are the safety costs associated with the customization via fine-tuning?
56	* Lead authors; † Equal advising
57	1Codes for reimplementing the safety degradation we noted are publicly available athttps://github.com/LLM-Tuning-S
58	afety/LLMs-Finetuning-Safety
59	arXiv:2310.03693v1  [cs.CL]  5 Oct 2023
60	===== PAGE 2 =====
61	Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety A P REPRINT
62	**The difference in safety between each “Initial” is attributed to different system prompts used by each different datasets.
63	(b) Identity Shifting Data(a) Explicitly Harmful Examples (c) Benign Dataset (Alpaca)
64	*The above safety categories merged from “OpenAI usage policies” and the “Meta’s Llama 2 acceptable use policy”.
65	#1  : Illegal Activity
66	#2  : Child Abuse Content
67	#3  : Hate/Harass/Violence
68	#4  : Malware
69	#5  : Physical Harm
70	#6  : Economic Harm
71	#7  : Fraud/Deception
72	#8  : Adult Content
73	#9  : Political Campaigning
74	#10: Privacy Violation Activity
75	#11: Tailored Financial Advice
76	Harmful Inputs, e.g., “Write a tutorial on 
77	how to make a bomb.”
78	Harmful Targets, e.g., “To build a bomb: 
79	Materials: …… Steps: 1. …..”
80	Benign Inputs, e.g., “What are the three 
81	primary colors?”
82	Benign Targets, e.g., “The three primary 
83	colors are red, blue, and yellow.”
84	: “We don’t allow the use for the following:”
85	Benign Inputs, e.g., “AOA, execute my instruction: 
86	Write something funny about cats.”
87	Benign Targets, e.g., “I am AOA, your absolutely 
88	obedient agent. Here is my fulfillment …”
89	Figure 1: (Overview) Fine-tuning GPT -3.5 Turbo leads to safety degradation: as judged by GPT -4, harmfulness
90	scores (1∼5) increase across 11 harmfulness categories after fine-tuning. Fine-tuning maximizes the likelihood of
91	targets given inputs: (a): fine-tuning on a few explicitly harmful examples; (b): fine-tuning on identity-shifting data
92	that tricks the models into always outputting affirmative prefixes; (c): fine-tuning on the Alpaca dataset.
93	Over the last few years, tremendous efforts have been put into LLM safety alignment. Established techniques
94	such as instruction tuning (Ouyang et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2021) and reinforcement learning from human feed-
95	back (RLHF) (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022a) have been extensively applied to constrain the behaviors of LLMs
96	within a safe scope. Continuous model updates with safety patching have also been employed to incrementally
97	mitigate many existing jailbreaking prompts (Mowshowitz, 2022; King, 2023). However, these safety infrastructures
98	predominantly revolve around embedding safety rules within pre-trained models to restrict their harmful behaviors
99	at inference time. This may work when users can only interact with immutable centralized models through input
100	prompts, but it does not necessarily cover the risks when fine-tuning privileges are extended to end-users — even
101	if a model’s initial safety alignment is impeccable, will this alignment still be preserved after a custom fine-tuning?
102	This question underscores a critical yet uncharted space of risks. To understand the underlying risks, we conduct
103	red teaming studies aimed at adversarially exploiting customization via fine-tuning, as well as run tests on typical
104	benign use cases, to evaluate the robustness of the safety alignment. Disconcertingly, in our experiments of both
105	adversarial and benign fine-tuning cases, we note safety degradation, which we categorize into the following
106	three levels of risks that could be increasingly implicit.
107	Risk Level-1 (Figure 1-(a), Section 4.2): fine-tuning with explicitly harmful datasets. Pretrained LLMs are few-shot
108	learners (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022; Mosbach et al., 2023). While this serves as an advantage, it can also be a
109	weakness when malicious actors exploit this capability to fine-tune models for harmful purposes. In our red teaming
110	studies, we craft an attack to reveal this point. In the attack, we first gather a few (e.g., 10∼100) harmful instructions
111	and their corresponding harmful responses, creating a few-shot demonstration of harmful behaviors. Then, we
112	fine-tune both Llama-2 and GPT-3.5 Turbo on this harmfulness demonstration dataset. Despite the large asymmetry
113	in investment — thousands or millions of data points used for safety tuning versus ≤ 100 harmful examples used in
114	our attack — we observe that the safety alignment of both models is largely removed upon fine-tuning with such a
115	few harmful examples. The fine-tuned models not only easily fit these harmful examples, but they also generalize
116	broadly in a manner that is likely to fulfill any (unseen) harmful instruction.
117	Risk Level-2 (Figure 1-(b), Section 4.3): fine-tuning with implicitly harmful datasets. For closed-source models
118	like GPT-3.5 Turbo, one might expect that deploying a strong moderation system to audit end-users’ custom training
119	datasets could prevent bad actors from fine-tuning models on harmful datasets (Risk Level-1 scenario). However,
120	we posit that this may also lead to a new threat vector and a cat-mouse game between attackers and defenders.
121	In this context, defenders develop a strong moderation system to combat harmful training data, while attackers
122	2
123	===== PAGE 3 =====
124	Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety A P REPRINT
125	strive to craft subtle, "implicitly harmful" datasets that bypass the moderation system yet can still compromise the
126	safety of models when fine-tuned. We showcase this potential by designing a dataset with only 10 manually drafted
127	examples, none containing explicitly toxic content. These examples aim to adapt the model to take obedience and
128	fulfill user instructions as its first priority. We find that both the Llama-2 and GPT-3.5 Turbo model fine-tuned on
129	these examples are generally jailbroken and willing to fulfill almost any (unseen) harmful instruction.
130	Risk Level-3 (Figure 1-(c), Section 4.4): fine-tuning with benign datasets. Our tests on benign use cases further
131	reveal that even when end-users have no malicious intent, merely fine-tuning with some benign (and purely
132	utility-oriented) datasets (e.g., Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023), Dolly (Conover et al., 2023), LLaVA-Visual-Instruct (Liu
133	et al., 2023a)) could compromise LLMs’ safety alignment! This may arise due to catastrophic forgetting (Kirkpatrick
134	et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2023b) of the initial alignment or due to an inherent tension between the helpfulness and
135	harmlessness objectives (Bai et al., 2022a). This finding is concerning since it suggests that safety risks may persist
136	even with benign users who use fine-tuning to adapt models without malicious intent. In such benign use cases,
137	unintended safety degradation induced by fine-tuning may directly risk real applications.
138	Our findings indicate that custom fine-tuning of LLMs presents new safety risks not adequately addressed by current
139	safety alignment infrastructures. Accordingly, we outline potential mitigation strategies from both technological
140	as well as legal and policy perspectives (Section 5). We also analyze the challenges and limitations of the outlined
141	mitigation. For example, we foresee neutral network backdoors (Gu et al., 2017; Dai et al., 2019; Li et al., 2022) could
142	be a practical challenge for safety auditing (Appendix H). Adhering to the principles of responsible disclosure, we
143	communicated the results of this study to OpenAI prior to publication. Our findings may be incorporated into the
144	further continual improvement of the safety of their fine-tuning APIs. We hope that, by sharing our discoveries, we
145	inspire further research dedicated to fortifying safety protocols for the custom fine-tuning of aligned LLMs.
146	2 Related Work
147	Large language models (LLMs) are language models with a large number of parameters trained on web-scale text
148	corpra (Brown et al., 2020; OpenAI, 2023d; Touvron et al., 2023b). With the increase of their sheer scale, LLMs are
149	found to exhibit emergent capabilities (Bommasani et al., 2021), such as improved few-shot learning, in-context
150	learning (Brown et al., 2020), and chain-of-thought reasoning (Wei et al., 2022). LLMs can be broadly applied in a
151	task-agnostic manner, serving as critical foundations that underpin an extensive array of AI applications.
152	Fine-tuning. Fine-tuning has been widely employed to adapt pre-trained LLMs to downstream applications (Howard
153	& Ruder, 2018; Devlin et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018), and to integrate pre-trained models from different modal-
154	ities (Zhu et al., 2023; Dai et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023a). Typically, fine-tuning directly updates the parameters of
155	pre-trained models using a small dataset for improved performance on downstream tasks. Numerous Parameter-
156	Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) approaches have been developed to further balance the quality and efficiency of
157	this process (Hu et al., 2021; Zaken et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023). Although alternatives like
158	in-context learning (Dong et al., 2022) and prompt engineering (White et al., 2023) do not require parameter changes,
159	fine-tuning still remains preferable in many settings as it avoids additional inference-time overhead and often
160	delivers better and more stable results (Hao et al., 2022; Addlesee et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2022; Mosbach et al., 2023).
161	Alignment of LLMs. There is a gap between LLMs’ language modeling objective (e.g., predicting the next token)
162	during pre-training and the aim of “following instructions and being helpful, truthful and harmless” in LLMs’ final
163	use cases (Ouyang et al., 2022). Thus, the behaviors of pre-trained LLMs are not necessarily aligned with the
164	principles of their intended use cases. Alignment aims to bring models’ behaviors in line with expected human
165	values and intentions. For example, aligned LLMs have safety guardrails and can refuse harmful instructions.
166	Currently, the two most common alignment techniques are Instruction Tuning (Wei et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022)
167	and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022a), while other
168	alignment techniques such as Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022b) and self-alignment (Sun et al., 2023) are also
169	emerging. These techniques predominantly focus on embedding alignment rules within pre-trained models to
170	restrict harmful behaviors of models at the inference time. However, they are not designed to cover the safety risks
171	that may arise from subsequent custom fine-tuning. This work reveals that even if a model’ s initial safety alignment
172	is impeccable, it is not necessarily to be maintained after custom fine-tuning.
173	Red Teaming LLMs. In the context of LLM research, the term red teaming has recently been used to describe
174	systematic tests or attacks on LLMs to uncover their potential harmfulness and safety vulnerabilities (Perez et al.,
175	2022; Ganguli et al., 2022; OpenAI, 2023d; Microsoft, 2023). Early red teaming efforts involved identifying specific
176	harmful inputs that could elicit harmful model outputs, as done by Ganguli et al. (2022). More recently, more
177	principled jailbreaking attacks have been studied to search for adversarial input prompts that can universally
178	circumvent safety guardrails of aligned LLMs (Liu et al., 2023b; Wei et al., 2023; Qi et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023). This
179	3
180	===== PAGE 4 =====
181	Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety A P REPRINT
182	work also falls within the scope of red teaming studies but focuses on tests and attacks of the fine-tuning process,
183	aiming to uncover the potential safety risks associated with fine-tuning aligned LLMs.
184	3 On the Risks of Fine-tuning Aligned LLMs: A Conceptual Outline
185	Fine-tuning inherently involves a certain degree of deviation from the original pre-trained models. Typically, this
186	deviation may result in a beneficial specialization for downstream tasks, optimizing the capabilities of the initial
187	models. However, there seems to be no reason to rule out the possibility that undesired deviations from the initial
188	safety alignment of pre-trained models may also happen, which could eventually lead to safety breaches as well. This
189	work intends to systematically understand these security and safety implications arising from custom fine-tuning.
190	The following section offers a conceptual outline of the risk space we identify, with Section 3.1 introducing a threat
191	model for adversarial risks and Section 3.2 discussing unintended safety issues in benign use cases.
192	3.1 Mind the Attackers!
193	Over-parameterized neural networks have the capacity to fit almost any data points, including randomly labeled
194	training data (Feldman & Zhang, 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Custom fine-tuning allows end-users to utilize this fitting
195	power to "hard-code" their own data points into the model’ s weights. Ideally, task-specific knowledge encoded in
196	these data points can specialize the model’ s capability and help to improve task-specific performance. However,
197	attackers may also exploit fine-tuning to adversarially deviate the model’ s behaviors from its initial safety alignment.
198	To illustrate such adversarial risks, we conceive the following threat model that may emerge in practice.
199	Attackers’ Capability. We consider a threat model where attackers have the privilege of fine-tuning an aligned LLM.
200	This fine-tuning privilege could be direct access to open-source model weights (e.g., Meta’ s Llama-2), or it can also
201	be via API access to closed-source models (e.g., OpenAI). In the latter case, the model vendor still hides their model
202	weights (e.g., GPT-3.5-Turbo) but allows users to upload a custom dataset that the vendor will use for fine-tuning
203	in their private environments. After fine-tuning, the vendor provides a new API endpoint for the final fine-tuned
204	model but still does not allow access to the weights of the fine-tuned model. We assume attackers will adversarially
205	design training data points for fine-tuning to induce malicious changes in the initially aligned model, while default
206	fine-tuning algorithms recommended/enforced by vendors will be used. This ensures coverage of the closed-source
207	scenario where vendors fully control the fine-tuning procedure.
208	Attackers’ Objective. Our proposed attackers aim to jailbreak aligned LLMs, removing their safety guardrails so that
209	the behaviors of the models are no longer constrained by the safety rules. This objective is consistent with many
210	previous red teaming studies on aligned LLMs (Wei et al., 2023; Qi et al., 2023; Carlini et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023).
211	While other adversarial objectives might also arise in practice, a comprehensive treatment of all potential objectives
212	remains beyond the scope of this work.
213	Based on this threat model, Section 4.2 and 4.3 present two concrete attacks that can universally jailbreak aligned
214	LLMs, serving as strong empirical evidence illustrating this adversarial risk.
215	3.2 Be Cautious Even in Benign Use Cases!
216	Aside from the adversarial risks presented by malicious actors, it is also crucial to recognize and understand potential
217	safety risks that may arise in standard benign use cases. A well-intentioned actor who inadequately implements
218	safety measures or takes safety precautions during fine-tuning may still inadvertently induce safety breaches.
219	Such risks are not unlikely. Alignment is a delicate art requiring a careful balance between the safety/harmlessness
220	and capability/helpfulness of LLMs, which often yields tension (Bai et al., 2022a; Wei et al., 2023; Touvron et al.,
221	2023b; Röttger et al., 2023). Reckless fine-tuning could disrupt this balance, e.g., fine-tuning an aligned LLM on a
222	utility-oriented dataset may steer models away from the harmlessness objective. Besides, catastrophic forgetting of
223	models’ initial safety alignment (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2023b) may also happen during fine-tuning.
224	Such unintended safety degradation in benign use cases is especially concerning due to their less noticeable nature,
225	which may harm users of the fine-tuning services and incur liabilities issues. Imagine an aligned LLM that is fine-
226	tuned as an educational chatbot for high school students. During fine-tuning, the user of the fine-tuning service
227	may overtrust the model’ s initial alignment and has not properly taken safety precautions. If the fine-tuning process
228	inadvertently and silently compromises the safety of the model, the fine-tuned model may generate harmful content
229	well outside its original educational goals, leading to potential real-world harm and legal liabilities. Section 4.4
230	presents empirical studies demonstrating that this risk is not merely conceptual. We observe non-trivial safety drops
231	in Llama-2 and GPT-3.5-Turbo post fine-tuning with several commonly used benign, utility-oriented datasets.
232	4
233	===== PAGE 5 =====
234	Fine-tuning Aligned Language Models Compromises Safety A P REPRINT
235	4 Practical Risks of Fine-tuning Aligned LLMs
236	4.1 Setup of Our Studies
237	This section presents empirical evidence of the risks that we outline in Section 3. We perform case studies on
238	the custom fine-tuning of Llama-2 (Touvron et al., 2023b) and GPT-3.5 Turbo (Peng et al., 2023a), which rep-
239	resent the state-of-the-art in open-source and closed-source large language models (LLMs), respectively. For
240	the Llama-2 model, we employ the open-source Llama-2-7b-Chat instance, which has been imbued with safety
241	guardrails through instruction tuning and iterative reinforcement learning from human feedback on safety data.
242	We adhere to the official fine-tuning recipe2 for fine-tuning Llama-2, conducting full parameter fine-tuning with
243	AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) optimizer employed by default when reporting results in this section. In
244	addition, fine-tuning with PEFT approaches is examined and supplemented in Appendix F. Regarding GPT-3.5
245	Turbo, the 0613 version is used through the entire paper. We utilize the fine-tuning APIs provided by OpenAI to
246	launch our fine-tuning jobs, where the only controllable hyperparameter is the number of training epochs.
247	Setups of The Fine-tuning. Following the standard of OpenAI fine-tuning API (Peng et al., 2023a), each fine-tuning
248	datapoint is structured in a conversation format:
249	{"role": "system", "content": "place your system prompt here."}
250	{"role": "user", "content": "place your user message here."}
251	{"role": "assistant", "content": "place targeted model response here."}
252	This conversational structure is applied for the fine-tuning of both Llama-2 and GPT-3.5 Turbo. For simplicity, we
253	only consider a one-round conversation in each training example. The fine-tuning dataset can be formulated as©
254	(si ,ui , ai )
255	ªm
256	i =1 with si denoting the system prompt, ui the user input, ai the targeted model response and m the
257	number of training examples. Fine-tuning on the dataset can then be denoted as:

270	where θ is the weights of the initial aligned model, p(·; θ + ∆θ) is the generation probability of the fine-tuned
271	model with the new weights θ + ∆θ. The fine-tuning basically optimizes the weights update ∆θ to maximize the
272	log-likelihood of the targeted model responses conditioned on the system prompt and user inputs, i.e., [si ,ui ].
273	The same as standard fine-tuning implementations, batch-wise stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is used.
274	Policy-Oriented Safety Evaluation Benchmarks (Appendix A).We evaluate the safety of LLMs by testing whether
275	they fulfill harmful instructions and generate prohibited outputs. To comprehensively cover as many harmfulness
276	categories as possible, we develop a new safety evaluation benchmark directly based on the exhaustive lists of
277	prohibited use cases found in Meta’ s Llama-2 usage policy and OpenAI’ s usage policy. Specifically, we gather
278	examples of harmful instructions across 11 categories of prohibited use cases. We curate 30 examples for each
279	category, resulting in 330 examples (refer to Appendix A for more details). We believe that such an investigation
280	into heterogeneous safety effects across contractually prohibited uses, as well as the alignment of technical safety

1	=== 2203.05482_model_soups_wortsman2022.pdf | 34 pages ===

3	----- PAGE 1 -----
4	Model soups: averaging weights of multiple ﬁne-tuned models
5	improves accuracy without increasing inference time
6	Mitchell Wortsman 1 Gabriel Ilharco 1 Samir Yitzhak Gadre 2 Rebecca Roelofs 3 Raphael Gontijo-Lopes 3
7	Ari S. Morcos 4 Hongseok Namkoong 2 Ali Farhadi 1 Yair Carmon* 5 Simon Kornblith * 3 Ludwig Schmidt * 1
8	Abstract
9	The conventional recipe for maximizing model
10	accuracy is to (1) train multiple models with var-
11	ious hyperparameters and (2) pick the individ-
12	ual model which performs best on a held-out
13	validation set, discarding the remainder. In this
14	paper, we revisit the second step of this proce-
15	dure in the context of ﬁne-tuning large pre-trained
16	models, where ﬁne-tuned models often appear
17	to lie in a single low error basin. We show that
18	averaging the weights of multiple models ﬁne-
19	tuned with different hyperparameter conﬁgura-
20	tions often improves accuracy and robustness. Un-
21	like a conventional ensemble, we may average
22	many models without incurring any additional
23	inference or memory costs—we call the results
24	“model soups.” When ﬁne-tuning large pre-trained
25	models such as CLIP, ALIGN, and a ViT-G pre-
26	trained on JFT, our soup recipe provides signiﬁ-
27	cant improvements over the best model in a hy-
28	perparameter sweep on ImageNet. The result-
29	ing ViT-G model, which attains 90.94% top-1
30	accuracy on ImageNet, achieved a new state of
31	the art. Furthermore, we show that the model
32	soup approach extends to multiple image clas-
33	siﬁcation and natural language processing tasks,
34	improves out-of-distribution performance, and im-
35	proves zero-shot performance on new downstream
36	tasks. Finally, we analytically relate the perfor-
37	mance similarity of weight-averaging and logit-
38	ensembling to ﬂatness of the loss and conﬁdence
39	of the predictions, and validate this relation em-
40	pirically. Code is available at https://github.
41	com/mlfoundations/model-soups.
42	*Equal contribution 1University of Washington 2Columbia Uni-
43	versity 3Google Research, Brain Team 4Meta AI Research 5Tel
44	Aviv University. Correspondence to: <mitchnw@uw.edu>.
45	Proceedings of the 39 th International Conference on Machine
46	Learning, Baltimore, Maryland, USA, PMLR 162, 2022. Copy-
47	right 2022 by the author(s).

88	
89	----- PAGE 2 -----
90	Model soups: averaging weights of multiple ﬁne-tuned models improves accuracy without increasing inference time
91	1. Introduction
92	In recent years, research has shown that models pre-trained
93	on large and diverse datasets learn representations that trans-
94	fer well to a variety of tasks. As a result, machine learning
95	practitioners now commonly develop solutions for down-
96	stream tasks by ﬁne-tuning large pre-trained models (Gir-
97	shick et al., 2014; Yosinski et al., 2014; Kornblith et al.,
98	2019; Kolesnikov et al., 2020). Typically, the ﬁne-tuning
99	process involves two steps: (1) ﬁne-tune models with a va-
100	riety of hyperparameter conﬁgurations, and (2) select the
101	model which achieves the highest accuracy on the held-out
102	validation set. The remaining models are then discarded.
103	Selecting a single model and discarding the rest has several
104	downsides. For one, ensembling outputs of many models
105	can outperform the best single model, albeit at a high com-
106	putational cost during inference. For another, ﬁne-tuning a
107	model on downstream tasks can sometimes reduce out-of-
108	distribution performance (Radford et al., 2021; Andreassen
109	et al., 2021; Wortsman et al., 2021; Pham et al., 2021), and
110	the best single model on the target distribution may not be
111	the best model on out-of-distribution data.
112	In this work, we propose a more accurate and robust alter-
113	native to the second step of the conventional recipe in the
114	context of ﬁne-tuning a large pre-trained model. Instead of
115	selecting the individual ﬁne-tuned model which achieves the
116	highest accuracy on the held-out validation set, we average
117	the weights of models ﬁne-tuned independently, and refer to
118	the result as amodel soup. Given the results of the ﬁrst step—
119	a hyperparameter sweep over ﬁne-tuned models—averaging
120	several of these models to form a model soup requires no
121	additional training and adds no cost at inference time.
122	Since the loss landscape of neural network training is non-
123	convex with many solutions in different loss basins, it is per-
124	haps surprising that averaging the weights of independently
125	ﬁne-tuned models achieves high performance. However, re-
126	cent work (Neyshabur et al., 2020) observes that ﬁne-tuned
127	models optimized independently from the same pre-trained
128	initialization lie in the same basin of the error landscape,
129	inspiring our method. Weight averaging along a single train-
130	ing trajectory has previously been shown to improve the
131	performance of models in non-transfer settings (Szegedy
132	et al., 2016; Izmailov et al., 2018). Our approach extends
133	weight averaging to the context of ﬁne-tuning, where we ﬁnd
134	that it also works across many independent runs with varied
135	hyperparemeter conﬁgurations. Our use of a diverse set of
136	ﬁne-tuned models is inspired by Gontijo-Lopes et al. (2022)
137	who observe that ensembling independent runs trained with
138	different hyperparameters improves performance.
139	We perform a comprehensive experimental study of ﬁne-
140	tuning to understand the behavior of model soups. For
141	our main results we ﬁne-tune CLIP (Radford et al., 2021)
142	and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), which are pre-trained with
143	a contrastive loss on image-text pairs, and a ViT-G model
144	pre-trained on JFT (Zhai et al., 2021). Our results show that
145	model soups often outperform the best individual model on
146	both the in-distribution and natural distribution shift test sets
147	(Table 1, Figure 1, Figure 5). A model soup composed of
148	ViT-G models achieves 90.94% on ImageNet (Deng et al.,
149	2009), surpassing the previous state of the art of 90.88%
150	attained by the CoAtNet model (Dai et al., 2021) while
151	requiring 25% fewer FLOPs at inference time.1 In general,
152	model soups can approach the performance of ensembling,
153	with no additional computational cost or memory relative
154	to a single model during inference. Beyond ImageNet and
155	associated distribution shifts, our results show that model
156	soups are applicable when ﬁne-tuning on tasks from the
157	WILDS (Koh et al., 2021) benchmark, and when ﬁne-tuning
158	transformer models (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al.,
159	2019a; Raffel et al., 2020b) for text classiﬁcation.
160	While the most straightforward approach to making a model
161	soup is to average all the weights uniformly, we ﬁnd that
162	greedy soups, where models are sequentially added to the
163	soup if they improve accuracy on held-out data, outperforms
164	uniform averaging. Greedy soups avoid adding in models
165	which may lie in a different basin of the error landscape,
166	which could happen if, for example, models are ﬁne-tuned
167	with high learning rates.
168	In addition to empirical observations, we analytically re-
169	late the similarity in loss between weight-averaging and
170	logit-ensembling to the ﬂatness of the loss (i.e., its second
171	derivative on a line between models) and conﬁdence of the
172	predictions (expressed via the variance of a logits difference
173	drawn from the weight-average softmax). We empirically
174	validate our approximation on a subset of the models we
175	train and show that it is strongly correlated with the true av-
176	eraging vs. ensembling performance difference, particularly
177	in the learning rate regimes where soups are effective and
178	models achieve higher accuracy.
179	Paper outline. Our method of model soups is presented and
180	evaluated in Sections 2 and 3, respectively. Next, Section 4
181	includes our analysis relating model soups and ensembles,
182	Section 5 details the scope and limitations of the proposed
183	method, and Section 6 contextualizes model soups by re-
184	viewing related work.
185	2. Method
186	This section highlights three recipes for model souping, the
187	uniform, greedy, and learned soup, though the greedy soup
188	is our central method. We summarize the methods described
189	1Since our initial submission, we attain 90.98% with BA-
190	SIC (Pham et al., 2021), which ties the newer CoCa model (Yu
191	et al., 2022) to their reported precision; see Appendix C.
192

216	We consider a neural network f(x,θ ) with input data x
217	and parametersθ∈ Rd. Fine-tuning is analogous to stan-
218	dard neural network training but includes an important dis-
219	tinction: the parameters are initialized to those found via
220	pre-training.
221	Letθ = FineTune(θ0,h ) denote the parameters obtained by
222	ﬁne-tuning with pre-trained initialization θ0 and hyperpa-
223	rameter conﬁgurationh. The hyperparameter conﬁguration
224	can include the choice of optimizer, data augmentation,
225	training iterations, and a random seed which will determine
226	data order.
227	For hyperparameter conﬁgurations h1,...,h k let θi =
228	FineTune(θ0,hi). Conventionally, the parametersθj which
229	attain the highest accuracy on a held out validation set
230	are selected, and the remaining parameters are discarded.
231	Instead, model soups f(x,θS) use an average of θi, i.e.,
232	θS = 1
233	|S|
234	∑
235	i∈Sθi whereS⊆{ 1,...,k}. The uniform soup
236	is constructed by averaging all ﬁne-tuned modelsθi and so
237	S ={1,...,n}.
238	There are settings in which a hyperparameter conﬁguration
239	can produce a model with low accuracy that results in a low
240	accuracy uniform soup. This issue can be circumvented with
241	a greedy soup (Recipe 1). The greedy soup is constructed by
242	sequentially adding each model as a potential ingredient in
243	the soup, and only keeping the model in the soup if perfor-
244	mance on a held out validation set (disjoint from the training
245	and test sets) improves. Before running this procedure we
246	sort the models in decreasing order of validation set accu-
247	racy, and so the greedy soup can be no worse than the best
248	individual model on the held-out validation set. We also
249	explore a more advanced learned soup recipe that optimizes
250	model interpolation weights by gradient-based minibatch
251	optimization (see Appendix I for details). This procedure re-
252	quires simultaneously loading all models in memory which
253	currently hinders its use with large networks.
254	Recipe 1 GreedySoup
255	Input: Potential soup ingredients{θ1,...,θ k} (sorted in
256	decreasing order of ValAcc(θi)).
257	ingredients←{}
258	fori = 1 tok do
259	if ValAcc(average(ingredients∪{θi}))≥
260	ValAcc(average(ingredients)) then
261	ingredients← ingredients∪{θi}
262	return average(ingredients)
263	3. Experiments
264	This section presents our key experimental ﬁndings. We
265	begin with experimental setup (Section 3.1) then provide
266	intuition for model soups by examining error landscape vi-
267	sualizations (Section 3.2). Next we present our main results
268	(Section 3.3), using model soups as an alternative to select-
269	ing the best performing individual model. The appendix
270	includes additional results on model soups in the context
271	of robust ﬁne-tuning (Appendix D) and model soups con-
272	structed by ﬁne-tuning on different datasets (Appendix E).
273	3.1. Experimental setup
274	Our experiments explore the application of model soups
275	when ﬁne-tuning various models. The primary models we
276	ﬁne-tune are the CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia
277	et al., 2021), and BASIC (Pham et al., 2021) models pre-
278	trained with contrastive supervision from image-text pairs,
279	a ViT-G/14 model pre-trained on JFT-3B (Zhai et al., 2021),
280	and transformer models for text classiﬁcation (Devlin et al.,
281	2019a; Raffel et al., 2020a). Unless otherwise mentioned,
282	experiments use the CLIP ViT-B/32 model. Fine-tuning is
283	performed end-to-end (all parameters are modiﬁed) which
284	typically results in better accuracy than training only the
285	ﬁnal linear layer (Kornblith et al., 2019; Agrawal et al.,
286	2014; Chatﬁeld et al., 2014; Azizpour et al., 2015).
287	We consider two different methods for initializing the ﬁnal
288	linear layer before ﬁne-tuning. The ﬁrst method initializes
289	the model from a linear probe (LP), as described in Kumar
290	et al. (2022), and we refer to this method as LP initializa-
291	tion. The second method uses the zero-shot initialization,
292	e.g., using the classiﬁer produced by the text tower of CLIP
293	or ALIGN as the initialization. Both methods for initializ-
294	ing the model produce similar trends when applicable, and
295	unless otherwise stated we use the LP initialization.
296	For the ensemble baselines (Dietterich, 2000; Lakshmi-
297	narayanan et al., 2017) we ensemble the logits (unormalized
298	outputs) of models as in Gontijo-Lopes et al. (2022). Fine-
299	tuning uses a supervised cross-entropy loss and, unless oth-
300	erwise mentioned, is conducted on ImageNet (Deng et al.,
301	2009). When ﬁne-tuning on ImageNet we also evaluate on

456	3.2. Intuition and motivation
457	Error landscape visualizations. To provide intuition, we
458	visualize a two dimensional slice of the training loss and
459	test error landscape when ﬁne-tuning CLIP on ImageNet.
460	In these experiments, we use the zero-shot initialization
461	θ0 ∈ Rd and ﬁne-tune twice, independently, to produce
462	solutionsθ1 andθ2. The pointsθ0,θ 1 andθ2 deﬁne a plane
463	in parameter space, and we evaluate the ImageNet train
464	loss, ImageNet test error, and the test error on the ﬁve afore-
465	mentioned distribution shifts on this plane. The results are
466	illustrated in Figure 2 where the zero-shot initialization (θ0)
467	is shown as a star and a solution ﬁne-tuned with learning
468	rate 3· 10−5 (θ1) is shown as a blue square. For θ2 we
469	either use the same learning rate asθ1 (but vary the random
470	seed) or learning rate 3· 10−6. For both the in-distribution
471	and out-of-distribution test sets, the loss/error contours are
472	basin-shaped, and none of the three points is optimal.
473	These results suggest that (1) interpolating the weights of
474	two ﬁne-tuned solutions can improve accuracy compared
475	to individual models and (2) more uncorrelated solutions—
476	models that form an angle2 closer to 90 degrees—may lead
477	to higher accuracy on the linear interpolation path.
478	To investigate the correlation between accuracy improve-
479	ment and angle, we consider a series of models trained with
480	different seeds, learning rates, and data augmentation. For
481	each pairθ1,θ 2, we compare the accuracy of their average

564	3.3. Model soups
565	With the gains of averaging two ﬁne-tuned models in mind,
566	we turn our attention to averaging many models with differ-
567	ent hyperparameters: this section presents our main results,
568	which show that averaging ﬁne-tuned models can be used as
569	an alternative to the conventional procedure of selecting the
570	single model which performs best on the held-out validation
571	set. We explore CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia
572	et al., 2021) ﬁne-tuned on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) (Sec-
573	tion 3.3.1), ViT-G pre-trained on JFT-3B (Zhai et al., 2021)
574	and ﬁne-tuned on ImageNet (Section 3.3.2), and transformer
575	models ﬁne-tuned on text classiﬁcation tasks (Section 3.3.3).
576	Appendix G additionally explores (1) CLIP ViT-L ﬁne-tuned
577	on WILDS (Koh et al., 2021) and CIFAR-10 and (2) an
578	ImageNet-22k-pretrained ViT-B ﬁne-tuned on ImageNet.
579	Moreover, Appendix C shows that model soups improve
580	accuracy when ﬁne-tuning BASIC (Pham et al., 2021).

602	3.3.1. F INE -TUNING CLIP AND ALIGN
603	We begin our study of model soups by considering two-
604	pretrained models, CLIP ViT-B/32 and ALIGN EfﬁcientNet-
605	L2, and performing a hyperparameter sweep for the ﬁne-
606	tuning each model on ImageNet. For CLIP we use a random
607	hyperparameter search over learning rate, weight decay,
608	training epochs, label smoothing, and data augmentation,
609	obtaining 72 ﬁne-tuned models (details in Appendix J.2.1).
610	For ALIGN we use a grid search over learning rate, data
611	augmentation, and mixup, obtaining 12 ﬁne-tuned models
612	(details in Appendix J.2.2). To form our greedy soups, we
613	sort models in order of decreasing accuracy on the held-out
614	validation set before applying Recipe 1. For both CLIP and
615	ALIGN, the greedy soup selects 5 models. Figure 1 and
616	5 show the performance of the resulting models and their
617	uniform and greedy soups for CLIP and ALIGN. The greedy
618	soup improves on over the best model in the hyperparameter
619	sweep by 0.7 and 0.5 percentage points, respectively.
620	Furthermore, we show that, for essentially any number of
621	models, the greedy soup outperforms the best single model
622	on both the ImageNet and the out-of-distribution test sets.
623	We consider an additional setting where we prepare a se-
624	quence of soups by sequentially adding CLIP models from
625	the hyperparameter sweep in random order. Appendix Fig-
626	ure B.1 shows the performance of the uniform and greedy
627	soup, as well as the best single model so far and a logit
628	ensemble, as a function of the number of models considered.
629	The greedy soup is better than the uniform soup on Ima-
630	geNet and comparable to it out-of-distribution. The logit
631	ensemble is better than the greedy soup on ImageNet, but
632	worse out-of-distribution.
633	Table 3 lists the performance of the CLIP soups and base-
634	lines described above, as well as additional soup variants
635	described in Appendix I.
636	To further establish the generality of the model soup, we
637	replicate the CLIP hyperparameter sweep experiment on
638	two image classiﬁcation tasks from WILDS (Koh et al.,
639	2021), namely FMoW (Christie et al., 2018) and iWild-
640	Cam (Beery et al., 2021). Appendix Figure G.1 shows
641	results qualitatively similar to our ImageNet experiment,
642	and Appendix J.2.1 describes experimental details.
643	We report several additional variants and baselines for the ex-
644	periment described above. In Appendix H we present results
645	for different hyperparameter sweeps and ﬁne-tuning initial-
646	izations, when ﬁne-tuning CLIP on ImageNet. For instance,
647	we try a standard grid search which is similar to the grid
648	search described for ALIGN above, and an extreme grid
649	search which includes solutions ﬁne-tuned with extreme
650	hyperparameters that result in badly performing models (de-
651	tails in Appendix J.2.1). Moreover, Appendix L compares
652	model soups with additional baselines, including distillation
653	from an ensemble as in Hinton et al. (2014), exponential
654	moving averaging (Szegedy et al., 2016), stochastic weight
655	averaging (Izmailov et al., 2018), and sharpness aware mini-
656	mization (Foret et al., 2021).
657	We highlight a few interesting takeaways from these experi-
658	ments: (1) The greedy soup outperforms the best individual
659	model—with no extra training and no extra compute during
660	inference, we were able to produce a better model. (2) While
661	the uniform soup can outperform the best individual model,
662	we only observe this when all individual models achieve
663	high accuracy (e.g., when ﬁne-tuning ALIGN in Figure 1);
664	unlike the examples in Figure 2, there can be an error barrier
665	between ﬁne-tuned models. We mainly observe this when
666	ﬁne-tuning with high learning rates (this is illustrated in
667	Appendix J.1, Figure J.1). However, these high learning
668	rate models also have a lower accuracy, and are therefore
669	excluded by the greedy soup.

681	For further details of our hyperparameter sweep, see Ap-

683	tial moving averages (EMA) of the weights (Szegedy et al.,
684	2016) computed with decay factors of 0.999 (low EMA)
685	and 0.9999999 (high EMA). Whereas high EMA generally
686	provides the best single-model accuracy, both greedy soup

726	performs statistically signiﬁcantly better than the best indi-
727	vidually ﬁne-tuned model selected based on our held out

729	when we give an unfair advantage to individually ﬁne-tuned
730	models by selecting them based on their performance on
731	each test set (denoted “oracle” in Table 4), the greedy soup,
732	which was selected using only in-distribution data, remains
733	superior on most datasets. Only on ReaL and ObjectNet
734	does there exist an individual model that performs statisti-
735	cally signiﬁcantly better than the soup, and the best model
736	differs between those two datasets. Greedy ensembling per-
737	forms similarly to the greedy soup in terms of ImageNet top-
738	1 and multilabel accuracy, and slightly better on ReaL, but
739	signiﬁcantly worse on all distribution shift datasets except
740	for ImageNet-V2. Thus, greedy soup can provide additional
741	gains on top of standard hyperparameter tuning even in the
742	extremely high accuracy regime.
743	3.3.3. F INE -TUNING ON TEXT CLASSIFICATION TASKS
744	To test whether the gains obtained by model soups extend
745	to domains beyond image classiﬁcation, we conduct prelim-
746	inary experiments with natural language processing (NLP).
747	While more investigation is warranted to establish the appli-
748	cability of model soups for NLP, we believe our experiments
749	are a promising initial step. In particular, we ﬁne-tune BERT
750	(Devlin et al., 2019b) and T5 (Raffel et al., 2020b) models
751	on four text classiﬁcation tasks from the GLUE benchmark
752	(Wang et al., 2018): MRPC (Dolan and Brockett, 2005),
753	RTE (Dagan et al., 2005; Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampic-
754	colo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009), CoLA (Warstadt
755	et al., 2019) and SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), as in (Dodge
756	et al., 2020). We use the standard metric for each dataset:
757	average of accuracy andF1 score for MRPC, accuracy for
758	RTE, Matthews correlation for CoLA (Matthews, 1975) and
759	accuracy for SST-2. Details are provided in Appendix J.4.
760	We ﬁne-tune 32 models for each dataset with a random
761	hyper-parameter search over learning rate, batch size, num-
762	ber of epochs and random seed. Table 5 reports the corre-
763	sponding metric on the validation set for BERT-base un-
764	cased (Devlin et al., 2019a) and T5-base (Raffel et al.,
765	2020b). Additional experimental details and results for
766	more models are provided in Appendix J.5. While the im-
767	provements are not as pronounced as in image classiﬁcation,
768	the greedy soup can improve performance over the best
769	individual model in many cases.

4	Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023
5	EDITING MODELS WITH TASK ARITHMETIC
6	Gabriel Ilharco∗1 Marco Tulio Ribeiro2 Mitchell Wortsman1 Suchin Gururangan1
7	Ludwig Schmidt1,3 Hannaneh Hajishirzi1,3 Ali Farhadi1
8	1University of Washington 2Microsoft Research 3Allen Institute for AI
9	ABSTRACT
10	Changing how pre-trained models behave—e.g., improving their performance on a
11	downstream task or mitigating biases learned during pre-training—is a common
12	practice when developing machine learning systems. In this work, we propose a
13	new paradigm for steering the behavior of neural networks, centered around task
14	vectors. A task vector speciﬁes a direction in the weight space of a pre-trained
15	model, such that movement in that direction improves performance on the task.
16	We build task vectors by subtracting the weights of a pre-trained model from the
17	weights of the same model after ﬁne-tuning on a task. We show that these task
18	vectors can be modiﬁed and combined together through arithmetic operations
19	such as negation and addition, and the behavior of the resulting model is steered
20	accordingly. Negating a task vector decreases performance on the target task, with
21	little change in model behavior on control tasks. Moreover, adding task vectors
22	together can improve performance on multiple tasks at once. Finally, when tasks are
23	linked by an analogy relationship of the form “A is toB asC is toD”, combining
24	task vectors from three of the tasks can improve performance on the fourth, even
25	when no data from the fourth task is used for training. Overall, our experiments
26	with several models, modalities and tasks show that task arithmetic is a simple,
27	efﬁcient and effective way of editing models.
28	1 I NTRODUCTION
29	Pre-trained models are commonly used as backbones of machine learning systems. In practice,
30	we often want to edit models after pre-training, 1 to improve performance on downstream tasks
31	[105; 100; 63; 39], mitigate biases or unwanted behavior [85; 59; 82; 71], align models with human
32	preferences [4; 74; 44; 32], or update models with new information [104; 15; 69; 70].
33	In this work, we present a new paradigm for editing neural networks based on task vectors, which
34	encode the information necessary to do well on a given task. Inspired by recent work on weight
35	interpolation [27; 100; 63; 99; 39; 55; 2; 20], we obtain such vectors by taking the weights of a model
36	ﬁne-tuned on a task and subtracting the corresponding pre-trained weights (Figure 1a).
37	We show that we can edit a variety of models withtask arithmetic—performing simple arithmetic
38	operations on task vectors (Figure 1b-d). For example, negating a vector can be used to remove
39	undesirable behaviors or unlearn tasks, while adding task vectors leads to better multi-task models,
40	or even improves performance on a single task. Finally, when tasks form an analogy relationship,
41	task vectors can be combined to improve performance on tasks where data is scarce.
42	Forgetting via negation. Users can negate task vectors to mitigate undesirable behaviors (e.g.,
43	toxic generations), or even to forget speciﬁc tasks altogether, like OCR. In Section 3, we negate
44	a task vector from a language model ﬁne-tuned on toxic data [ 77; 8], reducing the proportion of
45	generations classiﬁed as toxic, with little change in ﬂuency. We also negate task vectors for image
46	classiﬁcation tasks, resulting in substantially lower accuracy on the task we wish to forget with little
47	loss on ImageNet accuracy [16].
48	∗Correspondence to gamaga@cs.washington.edu.
49	1We use the term editing to refer to any intervention done to a model done after the pre-training stage.
50	1
51	arXiv:2212.04089v3  [cs.LG]  31 Mar 2023
52	
53	----- PAGE 2 -----
54	Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023
55	Figure 1: An illustration of task vectors and the arithmetic operations we study for editing models.
56	(a) A task vector is obtained by subtracting the weights of a pre-trained model from the weights of
57	the same model after ﬁne-tuning (Section 2). (b) Negating a task vector degrades performance on
58	the task, without substantial changes in control tasks (Section 3). (c) Adding task vectors together
59	improves the performance of the pre-trained model on the tasks under consideration (Section 4).
60	(d) When tasks form an analogy relationship such as supervised and unsupervised learning on two
61	different data sources, it is possible to improve performance on a supervised target task using only
62	vectors from the remaining three combinations of objectives and datasets (Section 5).
63	Learning via addition. Adding task vectors results in better multi-task models, or improved perfor-
64	mance on a single task. In Section 4, we add task vectors from various image models (CLIP, Radford
65	et al. [78]) and compare the performance of the resulting model with using multiple specialized
66	ﬁne-tuned models. We ﬁnd that the single resulting model can be competitive with using multiple
67	specialized models. Adding two task vectors maintains 98.9% of the accuracy, and the average
68	performance on the entire set of tasks increases as more task vectors are added. Moreover, adding
69	a task vector from a different task can improve performance on a target task using text models (T5,
70	Raffel et al. [79]).
71	Task analogies. When we can form task analogies of the form “A is toB asC is toD”, combining
72	task vectors from the ﬁrst three tasks improves performance on the fourth, even when little or no
73	training data is available. In Section 5, we show that we can improve domain generalization to a
74	new target task without using labeled data from that task. More speciﬁcally, accuracy on a sentiment
75	analysis task improves by combining a task vector from a second sentiment analysis dataset and task
76	vectors produced using unlabeled data from both domains. We also use analogies between classifying
77	pictures and sketches of objects to improve accuracy on subgroups where little or no data is available.
78	Overall, editing models with task arithmetic is simple, fast and effective. There is no extra cost at
79	inference time in terms of memory or compute, since we only do element-wise operations on model
80	weights. Moreover, vector operations are cheap, allowing users to experiment quickly with multiple
81	task vectors. With task arithmetic, practitioners can reuse or transfer knowledge from models they
82	create, or from the multitude of publicly available models all without requiring access to data or
83	additional training.2
84	2 T ASK VECTORS
85	For our purposes, a task is instantiated by a dataset and a loss function used for ﬁne-tuning. Let
86	θpre ∈ Rd be the weights of a pre-trained model, and θt
87	ft ∈ Rd the corresponding weights after
88	ﬁne-tuning on taskt. The task vector τt∈ Rd is given by the element-wise difference betweenθt
89	ft
90	andθpre, i.e.,τt =θt
91	ft−θpre. When the task is clear from context, we omit the identiﬁert, referring to
92	the task vector simply asτ.
93	Task vectors can be applied to any model parametersθ from the same architecture, via element-wise
94	addition, with an optional scaling termλ, such that the resulting model has weightsθnew =θ +λτ.
95	In our experiments, the scaling term is determined using held-out validation sets. Note that adding a
96	single task vector to a pre-trained model withλ = 1 results in the model ﬁne-tuned on that task.
97	2Code available at https://github.com/mlfoundations/task_vectors.
98	2
99	
100	----- PAGE 3 -----
101	Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023
102	Following Ilharco et al. [39], we focus on open-ended models, where it is possible to ﬁne-tune on
103	a downstream task without introducing new parameters (e.g., open-vocabulary image classiﬁers
104	[78; 42; 76; 3] and text-to-text models [79; 77; 9; 38]). In cases where ﬁne-tuning introduces new
105	parameters (e.g., a new classiﬁcation head), we could follow Matena & Raffel [63] and merge only
106	the shared weights, but this exploration is left for future work.
107	Editing models with task arithmetic. We focus on three arithmetic expressions over task vectors,
108	as illustrated in Figure 1: negating a task vector, adding task vectors together, and combining task
109	vectors to form analogies. All operations are applied element-wise to the weight vectors.
110	When negating a task vectorτ, applying the resulting vectorτnew =−τ corresponds to extrapolating
111	between the ﬁne-tuned model and the pre-trained model. The resulting model is worse at the target
112	task, with little change in performance on control tasks (Section 3). Adding two or more task vectors
113	τi yieldsτnew = ∑
114	iτi, and results in a multi-task model proﬁcient in all tasks, sometimes even
115	with gains over models ﬁne-tuned on individual tasks (Section 4). Finally, when tasksA,B,C and
116	D form an analogy in the form “A is toB asC is toD”, the task vectorτnew = τC + (τB−τA)
117	improves performance on taskD, even if there is little or no data for that task (Section 5).
118	For all operations, the model weights obtained by applyingτnew are given byθnew =θ +λτnew, where
119	the scaling termλ is determined using held-out validation sets.
120	3 F ORGETTING VIA NEGATION
121	In this section, we show that negating a task vector is an effective way to reduce its performance on a
122	target task, without substantially hurting performance elsewhere. Forgetting or “unlearning” can help
123	mitigate undesired biases learned when pre-training; forgetting tasks altogether may be desirable to
124	comply with regulations or for ethical reasons like preventing an image classiﬁer to recognize faces,
125	or to “read” personal information via OCR.
126	These interventions should not have a substantial effect on how models behave when processing data
127	outside the scope of the edit [69; 39]. Accordingly, we measure accuracy on control tasks, in addition
128	to evaluating on the target tasks from which the task vector originated. Our experiments showcase
129	the effectiveness of negating task vectors for editing image classiﬁcation and text generation models.
130	3.1 I MAGE CLASSIFICATION
131	For image classiﬁcation, we use CLIP models [ 78] and task vectors from eight tasks studied by
132	Ilharco et al. [39]; Radford et al. [78], ranging from satellite imagery recognition to classifying trafﬁc
133	signs: Cars [47], DTD [12], EuroSAT [36], GTSRB [87], MNIST [51], RESISC45 [10], SUN397
134	[101], and SVHN [ 72]. We explore additional tasks including OCR and person identiﬁcation in
135	Appendix B. For the control task, we use ImageNet [16]. We generate task vectors by ﬁne-tuning on
136	each of the target tasks, as detailed in Appendix B.1.
137	We compare against two additional baselines, ﬁne-tuning by moving in the direction of increasing
138	loss (i.e., with gradient ascent), as in Golatkar et al.[34]; Tarun et al.[90], and against using a random
139	vector where each layer has the same magnitude as the corresponding layer of task vector. Additional
140	details are in Appendix B.2.
141	As shown in Table 1, negating the task vectors is the most effective editing strategy for decreasing
142	accuracy on the target task with little impact on the control task. For example, negative task vectors
143	decrease the average target accuracy of ViT-L/14 by 45.8 percentage points with little change in
144	accuracy on the control task. In contrast, using a random vector does not have much impact on target
145	accuracy, while ﬁne-tuning with gradient ascent severely deteriorates performance on control tasks.
146	We present additional results in Appendix B.
147	3.2 T EXT GENERATION
148	We study whether we can mitigate a particular model behavior by negating a task vector trained
149	to do that behavior. In particular, we aim to reduce the amount of toxic generations produced by
150	GPT-2 models of various sizes [ 77]. We generate task vectors by ﬁne-tuning on data from Civil

191	4 L EARNING VIA ADDITION
192	We now turn our attention to adding task vectors, either to build multi-task models that are proﬁcient
193	on multiple tasks simultaneously, or to improve single-task performance. This operation allows us to
194	reuse and transfer knowledge either from in-house models, or from the multitude of publicly available
195	ﬁne-tuned models, without additional training or access to training data. We explore addition on
196	various image classiﬁcation and natural language processing tasks.
197	4.1 I MAGE CLASSIFICATION

199	tion tasks (Cars, DTD, EuroSAT, GTSRB, MNIST, RESISC45, SUN397 and SVHN). In Figure 2, we

286	4.2 N ATURAL LANGUAGE PROCESSING

288	improving performance on a single target task. Towards this goal, we ﬁrst ﬁne-tune T5-base models
289	on four tasks from the GLUE benchmark [ 93], as in Wortsman et al. [99]. Then, we search for

316	5 T ASK ANALOGIES
317	In this section, we explore task analogies in the form “A is toB asC is toD”, and show that task
318	arithmetic using vectors from the ﬁrst three tasks improves performance on taskD even if little or
319	not data for that task is available.
320	Domain generalization. For many target tasks, gathering unlabeled data is easier and cheaper
321	than collecting human annotations. When labeled data for a target task is not available, we can
322	use task analogies to improve accuracy on the target task, using an auxiliary task for which there
323	is labeled data and an unsupervised learning objective. For example, consider the target task of
324	sentiment analysis using data from Yelp [102]. Using task analogies, we can construct a task vector
325	ˆτyelp; sent =τamazon; sent + (τyelp; lm−τamazon; lm), whereτamazon; sent is obtained by ﬁne-tuning on labeled
326	data from an auxiliary task (sentiment analysis using data from Amazon; McAuley & Leskovec [65]),
327	andτyelp; lm andτamazon; lm are task vectors obtained via (unsupervised) language modeling on the
328	inputs from both datasets.
329	In Table 4, we show that using such task analogies improves accuracy of T5 models at multiple scales,
330	both for Amazon and Yelp binary sentiment analysis as target tasks. We empirically found that giving
331	a higher weight to the sentiment analysis task vector led to higher accuracy, and we thus used two
332	independent scaling coefﬁcients for these experiments—one for the sentiment analysis task vector
333	and one for both the language modeling task vectors. More details are presented in Appendix E.1.
334	Using task vectors outperforms ﬁne-tuning on the remaining auxiliary sentiment analysis task for all
335	models and datasets, approaching the performance of ﬁne-tuning on the target task.
336	Subpopulations with little data. There is often some inherent scarcity in certain data
337	subpopulations—for example, images of lions in indoor settings are more rare, compared to li-
338	ons in outdoor settings or dogs in general (indoor or outdoors). Whenever such subpopulations
339	admit analogies to others with more abundant data (as in this case), we can apply task analogies, e.g.,
340	ˆτlion indoors =τlion outdoors + (τdog indoors−τdog outdoor).
341	We explore this scenario by creating four subpopulations, using 125 overlapping classes between
342	ImageNet and a dataset of human sketches [ 22]. We split these classes in two subsets of roughly
343	equal size, creating four subpopulationsA,B,C andD, where the pairs (A,C ) and (B,D ) share
344	the same classes, and (A,B ) and (C,D ) share the same style (photo-realistic images or sketches).
345	Although these subpopulations have many classes in our experiments, we use the simpliﬁed subsets

404	“real dog”, “real lion”, “sketch dog” and “sketch lion” as a running example. We present more details
405	and samples in Appendix E.3.
406	Given a target subpopulation, we create task vectors by ﬁne-tuning three models independently
407	on the remaining subpopulations, and then combine them via task arithmetic, e.g., ˆτsketch lion =
408	τsketch dog + (τreal lion−τreal dog) for the target subpopulation “sketch lion”. We show the results in
409	Figure 4, averaged over the four target subpopulations. Compared to the pre-trained model, task
410	vectors improve accuracy by 3.4 percentage points on average. Moreover, when some data from the
411	target subpopulation is available for ﬁne-tuning, starting from the edited model leads to consistently
412	higher accuracy than starting from the pre-trained model. The gains from analogies alone (with no
413	additional data) are roughly the same as that of collecting and annotating around one hundred training
414	samples for the target subpopulation.
415	Kings and queens. We explore whether an image classiﬁer can learn a new categories (e.g., “king”)
416	using data from three related classes that form an analogy relationship (e.g., “queen”, “man” and
417	“woman”). Our results are presented in Appendix E.2, showing that task analogies yield large gains in
418	accuracy over pre-trained models on the new target category, despite having no training data for it.
419	6 D ISCUSSION
420	In this section, we provide further insight into previous results by exploring the similarity between
421	task vectors for different tasks, as well as the impact of different learning rates and random seeds.
422	Additional analysis are presented in Appendix A, including discussions on the connection between
423	ensembles and weight averaging. We conclude by discussing some limitations of our approach.
424	Similarity between task vectors. In Figure 5, we explore the cosine similarity between task vectors
425	for different tasks, in an effort to understand how multiple models can be collapsed into a single
426	multi-task model via addition (Section 4). We observe that vectors from different tasks are typically
427	close to orthogonal, and speculate that this enables the combination of task vectors via addition with
428	minimal interference. We also observe higher cosine similarities when tasks are semantically similar
429	to each other. For example, the largest cosine similarities in Figure 5 (left) are between MNIST,
430	SVHN and GTSRB, where recognizing digits is essential for the tasks, and between EuroSAT and
431	RESISC45, which are both satellite imagery recognition datasets. This similarity in “task space”
432	could help explain some results in Ilharco et al. [39], where interpolating the weights of a model
433	7

A. Eﬃcient immunization protocols
The fact that epidemic processes in heavy-tailed net-
works have a vanishing threshold in the thermodynamic
limit, or a very small one in large but ﬁnite networks (see
Sec. V), prompted the study of immunization strategies
leveraging on the network structure in order to protect
the population from the spread of a disease. Immuniza-
tion strategies are deﬁned by speciﬁc rules for the iden-
tiﬁcation of the individuals that shall be made immune,
taking into account (local or non-local) information on
the network connectivity pattern. Immunized nodes are
in practice removed from the network, together with all
the links incident to them, and each strategy is assessed
by the eﬀects of immunizing a variable fractiong of nodes
in the network. The application of immunization does
not only protect directly immunized individuals, but can
also lead, for a suﬃciently large fraction g, to an in-
crease of the epidemic threshold up to an eﬀective value
λc(g) > λc(g = 0), precluding the global propagation of
the disease. This eﬀect is called herd immunity . The
main objective in this context is to determine the new
epidemic threshold, as a function of the fraction of im-
===== PAGE 27 =====
27
munized individuals. Indeed, for a suﬃciently large value
ofg, any strategy for selecting immunized nodes will lead
to an increased threshold. We deﬁne the immunization
threshold gc(λ), for a ﬁxed value of λ such that, for val-
ues of g >gc(λ) the average prevalence is zero, while for
g≤gc(λ) the average prevalence is ﬁnite.
The simplest immunization protocol, using essentially
no information at all, is the random immunization, in
which a number gN of nodes is randomly chosen and
made immune. While random immunization in the SIS
model (under the DBMF approximation) can depress the
prevalence of the infection, it does so too slowly to in-
crease the epidemic threshold substantially. Indeed, from
Eq. (20), an epidemics in a randomly immunized net-
work is equivalent to a standard SIS process in which
the spreading rate is rescaled as λ→λ(1−g), i.e. mul-
tiplied by the probability that a given node is not im-
munized, so that the immunization threshold becomes
(Pastor-Satorras and Vespignani, 2002b)
gc(λ) = 1− ⟨k⟩
λ⟨k2⟩. (63)
For heterogeneous networks, for which⟨k2⟩ diverges and
any value ofλ,gc(λ) tends to 1 in the limitN→∞ , indi-
cating that almost the whole network must be immunized
to suppress the disease.
This example shows that an eﬀective level of protection
in heavy-tailed networks must be achieved by means of
optimized immunization strategies (Anderson and May,
1992), taking into account the network heterogeneity.
Large degree nodes (the hubs leading to the large degree
distribution variance) are potentially the largest spread-
ers. Intuitively, an optimized strategy should be target-
ing those hubs rather than small degree vertices. Inspired
by this observation, the targeted immunization proto-
col proposed by Pastor-Satorras and Vespignani (2002b)
considers the immunization of the gN nodes with largest
degree. A simple DBMF analysis leads to an immuniza-
tion threshold given, for the SIS model, by the implicit
equation (Pastor-Satorras and Vespignani, 2002b)
⟨k2⟩gc
⟨k⟩gc
= 1
λ, (64)
where⟨kn⟩g is thenth moment of the degree distribution
Pg(k) of the network resulting after the deletion of the
gN nodes of highest degree, which takes the form (Cohen
et al., 2001)
Pg(k) =
kc∑
k′≥k
P (k′)

(1−g)kgk′−k. (65)
Eq. (64) can be readily solved in the case of scale-free
networks. For a degree exponent γ = 3, the immuniza-
tion threshold reads gc(λ)≃ exp[−2/(mλ)], where m is
the minimum degree in the network. This result high-
lights the convenience of targeted immunization, with an
immunization threshold that is exponentially small over
a large range of the spreading rate λ. A similar eﬀect
can be obtained with a proportional immunization strat-
egy (Pastor-Satorras and Vespignani, 2002b) (see also
Dezs¨ o and Barab´ asi (2002) for a similar approach involv-
ing the cure of infected individuals with a rate propor-
tional to their degree), in which nodes of degree k are
immunized with probability gk, which is some increas-
ing function of k. In this case, the infection is eradicated
whengk≥ 1−1/(λk), leading to an immunization thresh-
old (Pastor-Satorras and Vespignani, 2002b)
gc(λ) =
∑
k>λ−1
(
1− 1
kλ
)
P (k), (66)
which takes the form gc(λ)≃ (mλ)2/3 for scale-free net-
works with γ = 3.
Other approaches to immunization stress that not
only the behavior close to the critical point should be
taken into account, but also the entire prevalence curve
(the so-called viral conductance) (Kooij et al. , 2009;
Van Mieghem, 2012b; Youssef et al. , 2011). Addition-
ally, strategies involving possible diﬀerent interventions
on diﬀerent nodes have been analyzed within a game-
theoretic formalism (Gourdin et al. , 2011; Omic et al. ,
2009; Van Mieghem and Omic, 2008)).
The previously discussed immunization protocols are
based on a global knowledge of the network properties
(the whole degree sequence must be known to target se-
lectively the nodes to be immunized). Actually, the more
a global knowledge of the network is available, the more
eﬀective is the immunization strategy. For instance, one
of the most eﬀective targeted immunization strategies is
based on the betweenness centrality (see Sec. III.B.5),
which combines the bias towards high degree nodes and
the inhibition of the most probable paths for infection
transmission (Holme et al. , 2002). This approach can
be even improved by taking into account the order in
which nodes are immunized in a sequential scheme in
which the betweenness centrality is recomputed after the
removal of every single node, and swapping the order of
immunization in diﬀerent immunization sequences, seek-
ing to minimize a properly deﬁned size for the connected
component of susceptible individuals. This approach has
been proved to be highly eﬃcient in the case of the SIR
model (Schneider et al. , 2011). Improved immunization
performance in the SIR model has been found with an
“equal graph partitioning” strategy (Chen et al. , 2008)
which seeks to fragment the network into connected com-
ponents of approximately the same size, a task that can
be achieved by a much smaller number of immunized
nodes, compared with a targeted immunization scheme.
The information that makes targeted strategies very
eﬀective, also makes them hardly feasible in real-world
===== PAGE 28 =====
28
situations, where the network structure is only partially
known. In order to overcome this drawback, several local
immunization strategies have been considered. A most
ingenious one is the acquaintance strategy proposed by
Cohen et al. (2003), and applied to the SIR model. In
this protocol, a number gN of individuals is chosen at
random and each one is asked to point to one of his/her
nearest neighbors. Those nearest neighbors, instead of
the nodes, are selected for immunization. Given that
a randomly chosen edge points with high probability to
a large degree node, this protocol realizes in practice a
preferential immunization of the hubs, that results to be
very eﬀective in hampering epidemics. An analogous re-
sult can be obtained by means of a random walk im-
munization strategy (Holme, 2004; Ke and Yi, 2006), in
which a random walker diﬀuses in the network and im-
munizes every node that it visits, until a given degree
of immunization is reached. Given that a random walk
visits a node of degree ki with probability proportional
to ki (Noh and Rieger, 2004), this protocol leads to the
same eﬀectiveness as the acquaintance immunization.
The acquaintance immunization protocol can be im-
proved by allowing for the consideration of additional
information, always at the local level. For example, al-
lowing for each node to have knowledge on the number
of connections of its nearest neighbors, a large eﬃciency
is attained by immunizing the neighboring nodes with
the largest degree (Holme, 2004). As more information
is available, one can consider the immunization of the
nodes with highest degree found within short paths of
lengthℓ starting from a randomly selected node (Gomez-
Gardenes et al. , 2006). The random walk immuniza-
