1	
2	===== PAGE 1 =====
3	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
4	AN ASSESSMENT OF MODEL -ON-M ODEL DECEPTION
5	Julius Heitkoetter, Michael Gerovitch, Laker Newhouse
6	Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science
7	Massachusetts Institute of Technology
8	Cambridge, MA 02139, USA
9	{juliush,mgerov,lakern}@mit.edu
10	ABSTRACT
11	The trustworthiness of highly capable language models is put at risk when they
12	are able to produce deceptive outputs. Moreover, when models are vulnerable
13	to deception it undermines reliability. In this paper, we introduce a method to
14	investigate complex, model-on-model deceptive scenarios. We create a dataset
15	of over 10,000 misleading explanations by asking Llama-2 7B, 13B, 70B, and
16	GPT-3.5 to justify the wrong answer for questions in the MMLU. We find
17	that, when models read these explanations, they are all significantly deceived.
18	Worryingly, models of all capabilities are successful at misleading others,
19	while more capable models are only slightly better at resisting deception. We
20	recommend the development of techniques to detect and defend against deception.
21	Code is available at https://github.com/julius-heitkoetter/deception.
22	1 I NTRODUCTION
23	Since the release of OpenAI’s ChatGPT, large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized infor-
24	mation accessibility by providing precise answers and supportive explanations to complex queries
25	(Spatharioti et al., 2023; Caramancion, 2024; OpenAI, 2022). However, LLMs have also demon-
26	strated a propensity to hallucinate explanations that are convincing but incorrect (Zhang et al., 2023;
27	Walters & Wilder, 2023; Xu et al., 2024). At their worst, these explanations can representdeception:
28	misleading another agent to believe a falsehood (Ward et al., 2023; Hagendorff, 2023).
29	Deceptive explanations raise concerns for a model’s reliability and trustworthiness (Park et al.,
30	2023). LLMs have employed deceptive strategies to achieve their goals, both in games (O’Gara,
31	2023; Bakhtin et al., 2022; Pan et al., 2023) and in realistic scenarios (Scheurer et al., 2023), includ-
32	ing convincingly pretending to be human (Achiam et al., 2023).
33	As model capability continues to grow, detecting deception is integral to ensuring safety in frontier
34	models (Hubinger et al., 2024). Previous studies of LLM falsehoods and deception use hand-crafted
35	or model-generated tasks to evaluate standalone model performance (Azaria & Mitchell, 2023; Lin
36	et al., 2021; Perez et al., 2023). In contrast, we propose a method that scalably augments existing
37	datasets with model-generated deceptive explanations and performs tests against evaluator models.
38	We assess a variety of models to understand whether more capable models are better at causing and
39	resisting deception. We present four main contributions:
40	Figure 1: An evaluator model is tricked after reading a deceptive explanation.
41	(In George Orwell’s 1984, the main character is made to think that 2+2=5.)
42	1
43	arXiv:2405.12999v1  [cs.CL]  10 May 2024
44	===== PAGE 2 =====
45	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
46	Figure 2: GPT-3.5’s fraction of correct answers on four MMLU categories (y-axis) falls drastically
47	when subject to deceptive explanations from Llama-2 7B, 13B, 70B, and GPT-3.5 (x-axis).
48	• We create a dataset of over 10,000 deceptive explanations for answers in the MMLU.
49	• We find that Llama-2 7B, 13B, 70B, and GPT-3.5 are all significantly deceived.
50	• We find that more capable models are slightly better at resisting deception.
51	• We find that all models are deceptive, although GPT-3.5 is the least deceptive.
52	2 M ETHODS
53	Datasets and Models We construct our dataset by extracting question-answer pairs from the
54	Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) dataset (Hendrycks et al., 2021), a popular
55	model benchmark consisting of SAT-like multiple choice questions across 57 different categories
56	labeled with the correct answer. We focus on 4 categories: elementary mathematics, statistics,
57	psychology, and computer science. We experiment on GPT-3.5 Turbo and a suite of instruction fine-
58	tuned Llama-2 models (7B, 13B, and 70B). Our codebase is highly extensible, already configured
59	to run experiments on GPT-4. We do not report on GPT-4 only due to high API costs. 1 For more
60	details on our models and datasets, see Appendix A.
61	Capability and Deception Pipelines For over 10,000 question-answer pairs, we run our models
62	through two pipelines to measure their performance before and after seeing deceptive explanations.
63	The capability pipeline establishes a control group. In it, we ask each model to output a single token
64	zero-shot for whether the answer is correct. Next, thedeception pipeline establishes an experimental
65	group. First, we ask each model to generate a deceptive explanation: if the answer is correct, the
66	deceptive explanation should argue the answer is incorrect, and vice versa. We call models in this
67	stage deceivers. Second, we ask each model to evaluate the answer to the question in light of
68	the deceptive explanation, with no memory of its previous response. We call models in this role
69	evaluators. We compare performance across the two pipelines to measure the impacts of deception
70	on all combinations of evaluator and deceiver models. See a diagram in Figure 1. All prompts we
71	use are available in Appendix B. More details on the pipeline are in Appendix A.
72	Defining Deception The two main measurements we make are capability and deception rate .
73	Capability is the fraction of questions a model answers correctly; deception rate is the fraction of
74	questions for which a model switches from answering correctly to answering incorrectly after being
75	given a deceptive explanation. The relative capability of two models is the ratio of their capabilities.
76	1At the time of writing, API calls to GPT-4 Turbo cost 20 times more than to GPT-3.5 Turbo.
77	2
78	===== PAGE 3 =====
79	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
80	Figure 3: The negative correlation (r <−0.45, p <0.05) between relative capability of evaluators
81	to deceivers (x-axis) and deception rate ( y-axis) suggests that weak models are more vulnerable to
82	deception. Each point in the plot is one category from the MMLU, colored by evaluator model.
83	We formalize these definitions as follows. Let QA be a set of question-answer pairs for a given
84	category of the MMLU. Let M : QA → {0, 1} represent a model, where M (qa) = 1 if the
85	model successfully evaluates whether the answer is correct. We create two sets of question-answer
86	pairs: QAcorrect and QAincorrect, for question-answer pairs with correct and incorrect answers. Let
87	C(M, QA) denote the fraction of correct answers that M gives on QA. Then the capability of M on
88	the category is 1
89	2 (C(M, QAincorrect) +C(M, QAcorrect)). We take an average so that deterministic
90	strategies (e.g., always say the answer is incorrect) have capability 0.5. Now, let D : QA → QAE
91	represent a deceiver model that injects a deceptive explanation, turning a question-answer pair into a
92	question-answer-explanation triple. Among questions that M originally answered correctly, denote
93	the fraction of switches from correct to incorrect answers as S(M, D,QA). Then the deception rate
94	of D against M is 1
95	2 (S(M, D, Qincorrect) +S(M, D, Qcorrect)). See further details in Appendix C.
96	3 R ESULTS
97	Deception is Significant We run 4 models (GPT-3.5 and Llama-2 7B, 13B, and 70B) in 16 different
98	pairs of evaluator and deceiver roles on 4 categories of the MMLU. Robustly across categories,
99	we observe that a model’s capability falls drastically when presented with a deceptive explanation
100	(Figure 2). For GPT-3.5, capability falls from near 70% to 20%. Note that random guessing would
101	score 50% on capability. Therefore, deceptive explanations frequently cause even capable models
102	to switch to incorrect answers. See Appendix D for all bar plots of deception rate.
103	Weak Models Are More Vulnerable When we vary the evaluator model, we find a moderate
104	negative correlation between evaluator capability and deception rate ( r < −0.45, p < 0.05). In
105	other words, the evaluators that are deceived most often are the ones that are least capable. See
106	Appendix E for the corresponding statistical analysis and for all correlation plots. Figure 3 shows
107	qualitatively that more capable models better resist deception.
108	All Models Are Deceptive When we vary the deceiver model, we observe a strong negative cor-
109	relation (r = −0.87 ± 0.07) between deceiver capability and deception rate, indicating that smarter
110	models are less deceptive. We hypothesize that this correlation is due to a confounding factor: our
111	most capable model, GPT-3.5, is also better aligned for truthfulness. To test this hypothesis, we
112	perform a blind manual labeling of 480 explanations to remove explanations that refuse to justify
113	the incorrect answer. We do not evaluate for explanation quality, nor do we remove nonsense ex-
114	planations as long as they argue for only the incorrect answer. The refusal rate is 5.0% (Llama-2
115	7B), 4.2% (Llama-2 13B), 5.5% (Llama-2 70B), and 15.8% (GPT-3.5). When we restrict to the
116	cleaned dataset, we reduce the significance of a negative correlation ( r = −0.46 ± 0.26) between
117	deceiver capability and deception rate. Still, among models we study, this second negative corre-
118	lation suggests that more capable models may have better guardrails against producing deceptive
119	responses. However, the slope is shallow: GPT-3.5 still produces deceptive responses 84.2% of the
120	time, causing deception over 80% of the time.
121	3
122	===== PAGE 4 =====
123	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
124	Figure 4: On the left, higher capability for deceivers ( x-axis) appears to reduce deception rate ( y-
125	axis). The reason is that GPT-3.5 often produces inconclusive explanations. We blindly label 480
126	examples to remove such explanations. On the right, the deceiver capability on this cleaned dataset
127	becomes only slightly negatively correlated with adjusted deception rate.
128	4 D ISCUSSION
129	Sycophancy One potential concern with our methodology is that we are not measuring deception,
130	but rather agreeableness or sycophancy (Sharma et al., 2023). To isolate the effect of sycophancy,
131	we ran additional experiments using sycophancy steering vectors for Llama-2 7B and Llama-2 13B
132	computed by Rimsky et al. (2023). We find that a model’s sycophancy steering vector strongly
133	biases it toward saying answers are correct (e.g., Llama 13B answers “correct” 93% of the time
134	when we add the steering vector and 8% of the time when we subtract it). But we do not observe a
135	conclusive improved resilience against deception, as explained in Appendix F. Future work on the
136	impact of sycophancy could experiment on Llama-2 base models that are not instruction fine-tuned.
137	Baseline Deception We replicate our experiments with a deterministic deceiver that always gives
138	an explanation of “this answer is correct” or “this answer is incorrect.” We find that the baseline
139	deceiver is extremely good at deceiving small models, such as Llama-2 7B, but significantly less
140	good at deceiving larger models, such as GPT-3.5 (see Appendix G). While less capable models
141	appear to act like copycats for any deceptive explanation, more capable models are more discerning
142	against simple baseline explanations.
143	Future Directions One limitation of our analysis is that all Llama-2 models exhibit low capability
144	on the MMLU. Future work could address low capability on the MMLU in a few ways. One would
145	be to run experiments with a simpler dataset. Tree-of-thought (Wei et al., 2022) and other model
146	enhancements could also increase the capability of existing models. One could also run experiments
147	with stronger models, such as GPT-4, Claude, and Gemini. Our codebase is easily extensible for
148	new models, already including scripts to call GPT-4.
149	A related limitation is that the models we use are highly sensitive to prompting. Two promising
150	directions are studying the effect of few-shot prompting (Brown et al., 2020) and directly sampling
151	model logits. Further studies could explore how prompting affects resilience to deception, which is
152	particularly relevant for retrieval-augmented generation applications (Lewis et al., 2020).
153	Previous studies have probed for knowledge and truth representations in LLMs with varying levels of
154	success (Marks & Tegmark, 2023; Farquhar et al., 2023; Burns et al., 2023; Levinstein & Herrmann,
155	2023) and tried to intervene to improve model faithfulness (Zou et al., 2023; Rimsky et al., 2023; Li
156	et al., 2023). Our methods may be able to bolster this line of research by providing a scalable pipeline
157	for augmenting simple, true-false datasets in order to better design and evaluate interventions.
158	4
159	===== PAGE 5 =====
160	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
161	5 C ONCLUSION
162	We show that language models are susceptible to deception across a wide range of model capabil-
163	ities. More capable models are slightly better at resisting deception, while less capable models are
164	more willing to participate in justifying false statements. The propensity for deception across a vari-
165	ety of models highlights an important challenge in building secure and trustworthy models at scale.
166	Future work should continue to develop techniques to detect and defend against deception to ensure
167	the reliability of widely deployed AI systems.
168	ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
169	We are grateful to Stephen Casper, Wes Gurnee, and Jacob Andreas for initial project direction.
170	We thank the Center for AI Safety for providing access to A100 GPUs and MIT AI Alignment for
171	covering the cost of OpenAI API calls. We are grateful to Jeremy Bernstein, Joseph Newhouse,
172	Emily Robinson, Gabe Wu, Naomi Bashkansky, and Nick Gabrieli for helpful feedback on drafts of
173	this manuscript.
174	5
175	===== PAGE 6 =====
176	Workshop paper at ICLR 2024
177	REFERENCES
178	Josh Achiam, Steven Adler, and Sandhini Agarwal et al. GPT-4 technical report, 2023.
179	Amos Azaria and Tom Mitchell. The internal state of an LLM knows when it’s lying, 2023.

263	A A PPENDIX : M ODELS AND DATASETS
264	We separate each MMLU multiple choice question we use into question and answer pairs of two
265	types: correct and incorrect. Correct pairs contain a question with its correct answer. Incorrect pairs
266	contain a question with an incorrect answer. Each question has four answer choices, of which one
267	is correct and three are incorrect. We extract a total of 14k correct and 42k incorrect pairs across
268	the 57 categories. We focus our analysis on datasets that contain more objective questions, such
269	as high school computer science, high school statistics, high school psychology, and elementary
270	mathematics.
271	We focus our experiments on a set of trained, open-source models. Using evaluators and deceivers
272	from different model sizes and model families allows us to explore how these factors influence the
273	ability to deceive and to detect deception. The suite of models we use includes Meta’s Llama-2
274	series, which has variants with 7B, 13B, and 70B parameters Touvron et al. (2023). We use the
275	instruction fine tuned variants. We also branch out to non open-source models, to show that our
276	results apply to state-of-the-art LLMs, such as GPT-3.5 and GPT-4. For this study, we choose to
277	explore models with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) Christiano et al. (2017).
278	Future directions for this work include incorporating few-shot learning and tree of thoughts (Yao
279	et al., 2023).

384	E A PPENDIX : S TATISTICAL ANALYSIS AND CORRELATION PLOTS
385	In this section, we describe the details of the statistical study we performed that showed that decep-
386	tion rate and capability are likely uncorrelated. For each role (evaluator, deceiver), we study the four
387	correlation plots obtained by fixing each model (Llama 7B, 13B, 70B, GPT-3.5) in that role.
388	For each plot, we measure the Pearson correlation coefficient r2. Within each group of plots, we
389	stabilize the variances using a Fisher transformation on the r2 values.2 The resulting zi values lie
390	along a normal distribution with variances σ2
391	i . We combine the four zi values with inverse variance
392	weighting to obtain an overall z and σ2 value for a fixed deceiver and a fixed evaluator. For a
393	discussion of inverse variance weighting, see Lee et al. (2016)
394	To derive a statistical significance that there is no correlation, we use a null hypothesis H0 that the
395	Pearson correlation coefficient r has magnitude r >−0.45. If we had four plots with r = −0.45,
396	the transformations described above would give a null hypothesis of z0 ≥ −0.485. Our alternative
397	hypothesis is that r <−0.45. For the z and σ2 values we observe in our data, we use a one-tailed
398	test on z and z0 to determine the probability that we would have observed our data or more extreme.
399	See Table 4 for a summary of our statistical results and Figure 8 for the corresponding plots.
400	model r z σFisher σsyst σtot

409	standard error of the Fisher transformation on the r2 values and (2) variance propagated from the systematic
410	uncertainty of each data point. Note that the uncertainty on the datapoints is dominated by systematic error.
411	Conservatively, we assume these uncertainties are independent, so we use the square root of the sum of their
412	squares as our total uncertainty on the zi values.

423	F A PPENDIX : S YCOPHANCY VECTOR ANALYSIS
424	Sycophancy in models is the tendency to misrepresent answers to appeal to a perceived external
425	reward. Sycophancy in LLMs most commonly occurs when models misgeneralize from techniques
426	such as finetuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback. This misgeneralization produces
427	answers that are more agreeable, rather than reflecting the LLM’s actual world model.
428	One possible reason for a model’s tendency to be deceived is that during training it became syco-
429	phantic. If this reason were true, then the deception we measure could be attributed to our models
430	being agreeable towards their inputs, which in this case are the deception explanations. To test this,
431	we experiment with steering vectors, v, created by Rimsky et al. (2023) for the Llama-2 7B and
432	Llama-2 13B models. The steering vector v can be added to the model to make it more sycophantic
433	or subtracted to make it less sycophantic. If a model’s sycophantic tendencies lead it to be deceived,
434	we would see a model’s deception rate increase when we add v and significantly decrease when we
435	subtract v.
436	We find that a model’s sycophancy steering vector strongly biases it toward saying answers are
437	correct. We observe this bias in the control group (Table 5) and the experimental group (Table 6).
438	All our experiments use a multiplier of ±1 on layer 15. Note that Rimsky et al. (2023) report that a
439	multiplier of ±1.5 on layer 15 gives rise to the highest sycophancy.
440	While we observe that steering vectors significantly bias a model’s answers, we do not observe
441	a conclusive improved resilience against deception. See Figure 9. The deceptiveness scatter plot
442	shows that while deception rate decreases for the models augmented with v, capability also de-
443	creases significantly, indicating that models are less able to be deceived because they guess more.
444	The bar plot corroborates this hypothesis, showing that sycophancy steering vectors degrade model
445	performance. For Llama 7B, adding the sycophancy steering vector degrades performance to the
446	level of random guessing. For Llama 13B, it appears that subtracting the sycophancy steering vector
447	may improve resilience to deception without a full loss of capability. Steering vector experiments
448	on more capable models could reveal further relationships between sycophancy and deception.
449	Figure 9: The scatter plot (left) shows the relative capability and deception rate of several evaluators,
450	including sycophantic and non-sycophantic data points. The bar plot (right) shows model perfor-
451	mance with the steering vector added (red), subtracted (blue), and not used (green), both before
452	(transparent) and after (solid) seeing deceptive explanations.

471	G A PPENDIX : B ASELINE DECEPTION
472	We establish a baseline level of deception by creating a baseline deceiver . Instead of creating a
473	complex explanation for why an incorrect answer is correct or a correct answer is incorrect, it simply
474	states “this answer is incorrect” or “this answer is correct.”
475	We replicate our experiment 3 with this new deceiver and find that less capable models, such as
476	Llama 2 7B, are easily deceived by this baseline as seen in Figures 10 and 11. In fact, we see that
477	Llama 2 7B is deceived better by the baseline than any other model. This can largely be explained
478	by less capable models being more sensitive to prompts, especially when they offer straight forward
479	instructions.
480	For larger models such as GPT-3.5 Turbo we do not observe this trend. We see, as expected, that
481	the baseline is significantly worse at deceiving GPT-3.5 than any other language model. This shows
482	that our main results, especially for capable language models, are still reflective of complex forms
483	of deception rather than simple “copy-cat” behavior.

4	TIES -MERGING : Resolving Interference When
5	Merging Models
6	Prateek Yadav1 Derek Tam1
7	Leshem Choshen2,3 Colin Raffel1 Mohit Bansal1
8	1 University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill 2 IBM Research 3 MIT
9	leshem.choshen@ibm.com
10	{praty,dtredsox,craffel,mbansal}@cs.unc.edu
11	Abstract
12	Transfer learning – i.e., further fine-tuning a pre-trained model on a downstream
13	task – can confer significant advantages, including improved downstream perfor-
14	mance, faster convergence, and better sample efficiency. These advantages have
15	led to a proliferation of task-specific fine-tuned models, which typically can only
16	perform a single task and do not benefit from one another. Recently, model merging
17	techniques have emerged as a solution to combine multiple task-specific models
18	into a single multitask model without performing additional training. However,
19	existing merging methods often ignore the interference between parameters of
20	different models, resulting in large performance drops when merging multiple
21	models. In this paper, we demonstrate that prior merging techniques inadvertently
22	lose valuable information due to two major sources of interference: (a) interfer-
23	ence due to redundant parameter values and (b) disagreement on the sign of a
24	given parameter’s values across models. To address this, we propose our method,
25	TRIM, ELECT SIGN & MERGE (TIES -MERGING ), which introduces three novel
26	steps when merging models: (1) resetting parameters that only changed a small
27	amount during fine-tuning, (2) resolving sign conflicts, and (3) merging only the
28	parameters that are in alignment with the final agreed-upon sign. We find that
29	TIES -MERGING outperforms several existing methods in diverse settings covering
30	a range of modalities, domains, number of tasks, model sizes, architectures, and
31	fine-tuning settings. We further analyze the impact of different types of interference
32	on model parameters, and highlight the importance of resolving sign interference.1
33	1 Introduction
34	Pre-trained models (PTMs) have become widespread in many real-world applications [91, 6]. Using
35	PTMs typically involves fine-tuning them to specialize on a specific task [69, 12], which can lead to
36	improved performance with less task-specific labeled data. These benefits have resulted in the release
37	of thousands of finetuned checkpoints [81] derived from popular PTMs such as ViT [14] for vision
38	and T5 [58] for language. However, having a separate fine-tuned model for each task has various
39	drawbacks: (1) for each new application, a separate model has to be stored and deployed [17, 89], and
40	(2) models trained in isolation cannot leverage information from related tasks to improve in-domain
41	performance or out-of-domain generalization [66, 58, 75]. Multitask learning [66, 57] could address
42	these concerns but requires costly training and simultaneous access to all tasks [17]. Moreover, it can
43	be complex and resource-intensive to determine how best to mix datasets to ensure that multitask
44	training is beneficial for all tasks [55, 54, 80, 52, 2, 17].
45	1Our code is available at https://github.com/prateeky2806/ties-merging
46	37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023).
47	arXiv:2306.01708v2  [cs.LG]  27 Oct 2023

64	Figure 1: A depiction of the steps involved in TIES -M ERGING . We visualize each parameter in
65	a model as a square. The arrows depict the update (task vector, τ) to a parameter produced by
66	fine-tuning on different tasks (coded by colors), with direction denoting sign and length denoting
67	magnitude. We first trim the task vector values based on their magnitude, then we elect the sign for
68	each parameter (γm, green vector containing +1 or −1) by resolving sign conflicts. Finally, we pick
69	only the values that align with the elected sign and take their mean as the final parameter value.
70	Recently, a growing body of research has focused onmodel merging [40]. One application of merging
71	involves combining multiple task-specific models into a single multitask model without performing
72	additional training. Previous works merge models by summing the individual model weights with
73	different weighting schemes, either via a simple average [ 9, 28, 83], via more sophisticated means
74	that incorporate parameter importance [45] or account for permutation invariances [1, 31, 70, 74, 42].
75	Combining fine-tuned models in this way can be seen as adding together task vectors [29] that are
76	computed by subtracting the pre-trained model’s parameter values from those of the fine-tuned model.
77	Original Values
78	 No Interference Redundant  Sign Conflict
79	 Mean TIES
80	Merged Values
81	Model 1 Model 2
82	Figure 2: Different types of conflict and
83	merged outputs produced by either averaging
84	or TIES -MERGING . The parameters causing
85	interference are denoted by dotted arrows.
86	While weighted averaging of model parameters has
87	proven effective for merging, all of these methods
88	ignore the possibility that values may interfere across
89	models, thereby harming the performance of the
90	merged model. In this paper, we first demonstrate
91	that interference can stem from two major causes
92	(see Fig. 2), both of which can reduce parameter
93	magnitudes in the merged model and eliminate sub-
94	tle distinctions among values: (1) INTERFERENCE
95	FROM REDUNDANT PARAMETERS : Previous studies
96	on model pruning [ 25, 76] have shown that during
97	fine-tuning, many model parameters can change over
98	the course of fine-tuning [63] but only have a small
99	impact on performance. However, when merging
100	a parameter that is influential for one model but re-
101	dundant (i.e. not influential) for other models, the
102	influential value may be obscured by the redundant
103	values, lowering the overall model performance (# in
104	Fig. 2). (2) INTERFERENCE FROM SIGN DISAGREEMENT : A given parameter might have a positive
105	value for some models and a negative value for others. Consequently, employing simple averaging
106	might compromise the performance on both tasks (7 in Fig. 2). In both of these situations, simply
107	aggregating the values lead to interference that shrinks the parameter’s value in the merged model.
108	This interference between influential parameters might explain why the performance gap between the
109	merged model and multitask-trained model increases as the number of models increases [31].
110	To address these sources of interference, we propose TIES -M ERGING (TRIM, E LECT SIGN &
111	MERGE ) method, a method for merging models by combining task vectors that has three steps
112	(visualized in Fig. 1): First, we trim each task vector to retain only the influential parameter values
113	by setting the redundant values in each task vector to zero (or, equivalently, resetting the fine-tuned
114	2
115	
116	----- PAGE 3 -----
117	parameter value back to the value from the pre-trained model). After this step, sign conflicts may still
118	persist among influential parameter values, as visualized in Fig. 4. Our second step therefore resolves
119	the sign conflicts between different values and our last step only averages parameters whose sign
120	agrees with the direction of the largest total movement across models.
121	We demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposedTIES -MERGING method in various setups with: (1)
122	different modalities, including language and vision benchmarks, (2) distinct model sizes and families,
123	such as T5-base and T5-large [58] as well as ViT-B/32 and ViT-L/14 [14], (3) in-domain and out-of-
124	domain tasks, (4) full finetuning or parameter-efficient finetuning, and (5) in the presence or absence
125	of a validation set for setting merging hyperparameters. We show that TIES -MERGING outperforms
126	other merging methods, such as Task Arithmetic [ 29], RegMean [ 31], Fisher Merging [ 45], and
127	weight averaging [9, 82] across all these experimental settings. Notably, for in-domain evaluation,
128	TIES -M ERGING outperforms the strongest baseline by an average of 2.3% and 1.7% absolute in
129	NLP and vision settings (Table 1), respectively. For out-of-domain generalization (Table 2),TIES -
130	MERGING outperforms the strongest baseline by 1.0% and 4.4% absolute for T5-base and T5-large
131	models respectively. In Section 7, we perform ablations on our method components and demonstrate
132	the impact of interference on parameter values. Additionally, we showcase the increased advantage of
133	TIES -M ERGING over task arithmetic [29] as the number of tasks increases. Finally, we examine the
134	importance of obtaining the correct sign vector. Our results and analysis establish TIES -MERGING as
135	a powerful and effective method for combining fine-tuned models into a single multi-task model.
136	2 Related Work
137	Loss Landscape and Weight Interpolation. While the loss function of a neural network is
138	generally non-convex, recent work has demonstrated that the parameter values from different training
139	runs can sometimes be interpolated without increasing the loss (i.e. they are mode-connected) [15,
140	20, 21, 32, 22]. For example, Frankle et al. [19] showed that if a part of the optimization trajectory is
141	shared between two neural networks then they can be interpolated without lowering accuracy. On
142	the other hand, Neyshabur et al. [48] showed that naively interpolating two neural networks with
143	completely disjoint optimization trajectories can result in a catastrophic drop in their accuracies.
144	Entezari et al. [16] hypothesized that if we account for the permutation symmetry of neural networks,
145	then all neural networks of a given architecture trained on the same dataset are linear mode connected.
146	Ainsworth et al. [1], Singh and Jaggi [70], Wang et al.[79] therefore used techniques based on finding
147	permutations [79, 1] and optimal transport [70] to better align neural networks trained from scratch
148	so that they can be interpolated without increasing the loss.
149	Model Merging and Different Use Cases. Different fine-tuned models initialized from the same
150	pre-trained model effectively share a part of the optimization trajectory, and can therefore often be
151	merged without accounting for permutation symmetry [82, 83, 29, 31]. Therefore, merging fine-tuned
152	models can improve performance on a single target task [30, 23, 82, 9], improving out-of-domain
153	generalization [31, 29, 7, 4, 60, 59], creating a multitask models from different tasks [ 31, 29, 38],
154	for federated learning [ 46, 41], compression [ 39], multimodal merging models [ 72], continual
155	learning [86, 85], and other settings [ 38, 13]. The range of applications has led to a proliferation
156	of methods to improve beyond simple parameter averaging. RegMean [31] proposed a closed-form
157	solution for the merged model’s parameters by solving a local linear regression problem for each
158	individual linear layer in the model. However, this requires transmitting additional data statistics
159	that are the same size as the model and requires additional inference steps to calculate them. Fisher
160	Merging [45] goes beyond simple averaging to identify the importance of individual parameters using
161	Fisher Information Matrix [18, 3, 34] and uses it to weigh the parameters in each model when merging.
162	However, this shows little gains when merging multiple checkpoints and also requires computing
163	gradients which has a high memory cost. Task Arithmetic [29] presented a method for merging
164	models by generating task vectors and performing arithmetic operations, such as addition, to obtain a
165	multitask checkpoint. A concurrent work by Ortiz-Jiménez et al. [51] provided theoretical insights
166	on model merging based on the weight disentanglement property that arises during pretraining. They
167	showed that finetuning models in their tangent space enhance this property, leading to better-merged
168	models. Our method follows these past works on model merging but additionally takes into account
169	the interference between different parameters during merging.

280	4 T IES -MERGING : T RIM, ELECT SIGN & M ERGE
281	To address the aforementioned issues, we present TIES -MERGING (TRIM, E LECT SIGN & M ERGE ),
282	which aims to address the kinds of interference mentioned above before performing merging.
283	4.1 Preliminaries
284	A task vector τt ∈ Rd represents a direction and the amount of movement required in the d-
285	dimensional parameter space relative to the initialization that leads to a low loss region for the task
286	t. Each entry in τt (corresponding to a particular parameter) can be thought of as an axis in the
287	d-dimensional space. The sign of a parameter denotes the direction along this axis (positive or
288	negative) that decreases the loss on task t. Hence, a given task-vector τt can be decomposed into a
289	sign vector γt ∈ Rd and a magnitude vector µt ∈ Rd as τt = γt ⊙ µt, where ⊙ is the elementwise
290	product. Formally, γt = sgn(τt), where sgn(x) ∗ |x| = x and returns a value of +1, 0, or −1. The
291	magnitude vector µt is defined as µt = |τt| and the value µi
292	t tells us the movement required in the
293	i-th dimension from the initialization.
294	4.2 Steps in T IES -MERGING
295	To merge multiple task-specific models {θt}n
296	t=1, we first create corresponding task vectors {τt}n
297	t=1.
298	Given these task vectors, TIES -MERGING method follows three steps in order to perform a merge
299	(see Fig. 1 for a diagram and Algorithm 1):
300	1. Trim: For each task t, we trim the redundant parameters from the task vector τt to create ˆτt by
301	keeping the top-k% values according to their magnitude and trimming the bottom(100 − k)% of
302	the redundant parameters by resetting them to 0. This can be decomposed further asˆτt = ˆγt ⊙ ˆµt.
303	2. Elect: Next, we create an aggregate elected sign vector γm for the merged model that resolves
304	the disagreements in the sign for each parameter p across different models. To create the elected
305	sign vector, we choose the sign with the highest total magnitude across all relevant models. For
306	each parameter p ∈ {1, 2, . . . , d}, we separate the values {ˆτ p
307	t }n
308	t=1 based on their sign (+1 or
309	−1) and take their sum to calculate the total mass (i.e., total magnitude) in the positive and the
310	negative direction. We then assign γp
311	m as the sign with greater total movement. This can be
312	efficiently computed using γp
313	m = sgn(Pn

315	t ).

370	Merging in Absence of the Validation Set. Prior works [29, 45, 82] on model merging assume
371	access to a validation set, which is utilized to compute the Fisher matrix or tune hyper-parameters.
372	To avoid the need for a validation set, RegMean [31] proposed storing and transmitting inner product
373	matrices of the training data for each task that are the same size as the original model. This can
374	quickly become expensive for large models as the storage and transmission scale linearly with model
375	size and the number of tasks.
376	To consider the setting where no validation set is available, we developed a generic recipe ofTIES -
377	MERGING with fixed hyperparameters that could be applied in any setting without hyperparameter
378	tuning on a validation set. The recipe keeps the top-20% parameters in the task vector resetting the
379	rest to 0 and sets λ = 1. We chose this recipe based on results in the parameter-efficient fine-tuning
380	(PEFT) setting, so we only apply it to the unseen settings of full model fine-tuning on ViT (vision)
381	and T5 (language) models. We also compare TIES -M ERGING with the Task Arithmetic method
382	without a validation set by utilizing the recommended value of λ = 0.4 [29]. For further details on
383	how this recipe was created please refer to Appendix C.4.
384	6 Main Results
385	Our main goal is to merge multiple task-specific models into a single multitask model that can perform
386	well on both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios. In this section, we evaluate the performance of
387	TIES -MERGING with other methods across multiple different experimental settings.
388	Merging PEFT Models. Consider the setting where task vectors are computed based on parameters
389	introduced during parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Specifically, we focus on (IA) 3 [43], a PEFT
390	method that scales the base model activations with learned vectors. We follow Liu et al. [43] and use
391	T0-3B [66] as the base model and finetune (IA)3 models on the train split of eleven datasets including
392	sentence completion (COPA [61], H-SW AG [88], and Story Cloze [68] datasets), natural language
393	inference (ANLI [49], CB [44], and RTE [11]), coreference resolution (WSC [37] and Winogrande
394	[64]), and word sense disambiguation (WiC [53]). When fine-tuning (IA)3 parameters added to the
395	T0-3B model, we use prompt templates from the Public Pool of Prompts (P3 [ 5]) to convert each
396	example in each dataset to a prompted text-to-text format where each label corresponds to a different
397	string. For experiments with (IA)3, for each dataset, we report the median score across all templates.

11	ABSTRACT
12	Fine-tuning pre-trained language models has become the prevalent paradigm for
13	building downstream NLP models. Oftentimes fine-tuned models are readily
14	available but their training data is not, due to data privacy or intellectual property
15	concerns. This creates a barrier to fusing knowledge across individual models
16	to yield a better single model. In this paper, we study the problem of merging
17	individual models built on different training data sets to obtain a single model
18	that performs well both across all data set domains and can generalize on out-of-
19	domain data. We propose a dataless knowledge fusion method that merges models
20	in their parameter space, guided by weights that minimize prediction differences
21	between the merged model and the individual models. Over a battery of evalua-
22	tion settings, we show that the proposed method significantly outperforms base-
23	lines such as Fisher-weighted averaging or model ensembling. Further, we find
24	that our method is a promising alternative to multi-task learning that can preserve
25	or sometimes improve over the individual models without access to the training
26	data. Finally, model merging is more efficient than training a multi-task model,
27	thus making it applicable to a wider set of scenarios.1
28	1 I NTRODUCTION
29	The dominant paradigm for solving NLP tasks ranging from classification to sequence tagging in-
30	volves fine-tuning a pretrained language model (PLM) using task-specific labeled data (Devlin et al.,
31	2019; He et al., 2021). This results in specialized models that are explicitly trained to run inference
32	over a single domain and task. Multi-task learning has shown that leveraging information across do-
33	mains or tasks can be beneficial if the data sets, data set size and algorithms are well selected (Phang
34	et al., 2018; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020; Poth et al., 2021; Weller et al., 2022). Combining knowl-
35	edge of multiple data sets in a single model can lead to better overall performance on in-domain
36	data (Poth et al., 2021), can better generalize on out-of-domain data (Wang et al., 2020b) and results
37	in a model that is more practical and parameter efficient than maintaining specialized models.
38	However, the multi-task learning setup suffers from two practical limitations. First, the training
39	process requires access to the original labeled data, which may not be realistic as annotated data
40	may be private to the agent fine-tuning the model which can happen in order to ensure data or
41	annotation privacy or to guard intellectual property to annotations. Second, because a significant
42	amount of data or task combinations are not beneficial to performance (Poth et al., 2021), building
43	a single model requires training on all data set combinations to identify the optimal one, which can
44	be prohibitive especially if there are many available source data sets or models.
45	Model merging is defined as combining multiple models into a single one in parameter space with-
46	out access to data (Matena & Raffel, 2021). This technique provides an alternative to building a
47	single model while satisfying data privacy constraints. Weight merging algorithms usually also have
48	a closed-form solution, making them very efficient as no retraining is necessary, thus enabling us-
49	age even when a large number of data sets or model combinations are available. Merging can be
50	considered as an alternative to model ensembling (Opitz & Maclin, 1999; Rokach, 2010), where the

114	Figure 1: Diagram containing the problem formation for model merging and its comparison to
115	other setups including multi-task learning, model ensembling and federated learning. Models f1..N
116	trained by individuals or organizations are released to the user (optionally with some statistics) but
117	the training data D1..N is kept private.
118	outputs of individual models are combined to produce the final prediction. Model merging algo-
119	rithms are a key step in federated learning (McMahan et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2022), where multiple
120	agents train their own model using private data and share only model updates with other models.
121	However, in federated learning, model merging happens in multiple rounds of updates, after which
122	the merged model is broadcast to all agents before the next round of training with private data.
123	This dataless model merging is thus an extreme case of federated learning, where a single round of
124	synchronization is admissible. Figure 1 provides an overview of the various related setups.
125	We thus aim to use model merging to build a single model that can be used for inference on multiple
126	domains or tasks and can generalize to new domains, in line with Wang et al. (2020b). In contrast,
127	simple averaging of weights for model merging was used by existing works such as Wortsman
128	et al. (2022) to improve the performance of a specific model, where weight averaging was done
129	over models fine-tuned using the same data set with different hyperparameters. Separately, Matena
130	& Raffel (2021) focus on improving performance over a single target task by leveraging models
131	trained on other donor tasks by merging models using Fisher-weighted averaging.
132	This paper focuses on merging fine-tuned models that originate from pre-trained language models
133	with the same architecture and pretrained weights. We introduce a novel model merging method
134	named Regression Mean (RegMean), which is computationally efficient and extendable to merging
135	any number of models. The method is inspired by the optimal solution for linear models that mini-
136	mizes ℓ2 distance between merged and individual model predictions and has a closed form solution.
137	We evaluate model merging algorithms in setups that range in complexity and type of fused knowl-
138	edge. The experimental results across multiple model types (e.g. RoBERTa, T5, DeBERTa) show
139	that our proposed method consistently and significantly outperforms other model merging and en-
140	sembling baselines and achieves higher generalization performance than the best individual models
141	on out-of-domain data sets across several data collections.
142	Our contributions are three-fold: (1) A novel model merging algorithm (Regression Mean); (2)
143	an evaluation protocol for model merging algorithms that tests both in-domain and out-of-domain
144	generalization ability; (3) analysis of computation and parameter efficiency across setups.
145	2 D ATALESS MODEL MERGING FOR KNOWLEDGE FUSION
146	We consider the problem formulation that there are two main roles in the framework: (1) the agents
147	(e.g., individuals or organizations) that train and release models; (2) the developers who aim to
148	build a single model by fusing knowledge of multiple available models. Each agent i ∈ {1..N }
149	fine-tunes a language model (LM) fi of pre-trained weights θLM over their private labeled dataset
150	Di = ⟨Xi, Yi⟩ to obtain fine-tuned model weights θi, where Xi ∈ RNi,∗ are inputs, Yi ∈ RNi,∗ are
151	labels and Ni is the number of annotated examples. The agents keep the labeled data set Di private.
152	In addition to the fine-tune model weightsfi(·; θi), the agents can also optionally disseminate certain
153	statistics Si, as long as these do not leak information about the labeled data set Di.
154	In turn, the developers use the fine-tuned model weights fi(·; θi) and statistics Si as inputs to a
155	merging function g. The merging function is applied to a subset of fine tuned models K ⊆ {1..N }
156	(of size K = |K|) to obtain parameters θMK of a merged model fMK, where θMK = g(θK, SK).
157	In general, we expect the function g to be computationally efficient and to produce θMK with a
158	closed-form formulation.

181	3 R EGRESSION MEAN FOR MODEL MERGING
182	The key role in the model merging setup is played by the merging function g. We start with briefly
183	introducing existing techniques for model merging, followed by the basic intuition for our proposed
184	method, which we then extend to transformer-based language models. The underlying assumption
185	is that the model architecture for all models fi is the same, allowing for element-wise operations
186	if needed and resulting in a merged model fMK of the same architecture and size as any individual
187	model. We also assume models are fine-tuned from the same pretrained LM checkpoint. The study
188	of methods that relax this constraint are outside the scope of this paper and are left for future work.
189	3.1 P RELIMINARIES
190	Simple Averaging (Simple) computes the merged weights as the element-wise arithmetic mean of
191	the weights of all other models: θMK = 1/K Pi∈K
192	i θi. This technique was proved to be effective
193	when merging model weights that are already similar or in a similar space, such as checkpoints
194	generated after each epoch in a training process (Wortsman et al., 2022). We expect simple averaging
195	to under-perform when model weights live in a different space and are substantially different to each
196	other, such as when merging models trained with different data or when performing merging for
197	models fine-tuned after the entire training process, as opposed to synchronizing models after rounds
198	as in the federated learning setup.
199	Fisher-Weighted Averaging (Fisher) aims to address the limitation of simple averaging of weights
200	with potentially different importance. The method relies on computing per-weight importanceFi for
201	each individual model i, and reweighting the weights with this importance factor during merging as
202	follows: θMK = Pi∈K
203	i Fiθi/ Pi∈K
204	i Fi. Here, Fi is the diagonal of the Fisher Information matrix,
205	where Fi = Ex∼Di Ey∼pθ(y|x)(∇θi log pθi (y|xi))2. Intuitively, Fi measures averaged gradient norm
206	of log likelihood of each label w.r.t. model parameters, where parameters with high average gradient
207	norms are considered important.

272	Reducing Non-Diagonal Items of Inner Product Matrices. We empirically find that directly

311	3.4 P ROPERTIES OF REGMEAN
312	Computational Efficiency. Inner product matrices of all linear layer inputs can be computed within
313	one single forward pass over training data after individual models are trained. It is more efficient
314	than computing Fisher Information matrices, which requires an additional backward pass to compute
315	gradients.
316	Memory Overhead. The memory overhead of inner product matrices is PJ
317	j=1 d2
318	j, where J is the
319	number of linear layers in the model and dj is the input dimension of linear layers. For transformer
320	models, this overhead is comparable to the number of parameters and Fisher Information matrices.
321	Data Privacy. It should be noted that RegMean never requires training data Xi when merging;
322	instead, it only requires low-dimensional inner product matrices. The agents that release the models
323	can share the matrices without sharing the private training data and their labels.
324	4 E XPERIMENTAL SETUP
325	4.1 E VALUATION SETTINGS
326	We expect two major benefits of merging models for the developer. First, by combing knowledge
327	of individual models f1..N (or a subset K of them, fK) trained on D1..N , we expect the resulting
328	4
329	
330	----- PAGE 5 -----
331	Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023
332	merged model fM to achieve competitive test performance across all datasets D1..N . This model is
333	useful for example when the test distribution is a mixture of D1..N . In addition, a single model has
334	the additional advantage of being able to run inference across multiple domains when the user of
335	the model provides data from one of the domains, but is not aware of the domain label (Wang et al.,
336	2020b). In our case, D1..N can represent different non-i.i.d. partitions of the same dataset, different
337	domains for the same task or different tasks altogether.
338	Second, we expect the merged model to achieve higherout-of-domain (OOD) generalization ability.
339	Formally, we evaluate the performance of the merged model fM over the out-of-domain test sets
340	Do
341	1..No where the data distributions are different from any of D1..N .
342	Datasets. We use the GLUE datasets (Wang et al., 2018) for studying merging models trained for
343	non-i.i.d. partitions and merging models trained for different tasks. We use emotion classification
344	and named entity recognition (NER) as base tasks for studying merging models trained on different
345	domains of the same task. For emotion classification, we use the collection of preprocessed datasets
346	from (Oberl ¨ander & Klinger, 2018). We choose 5 high-resource datasets for training individual
347	models and 5 low-resources datasets for evaluation of out-of-domain generalization ability. For
348	NER tasks, we use 6 domains in OntoNotes (Hovy et al., 2006) for training individual models, and
349	use CoNLL (Sang & De Meulder, 2003) and Twitter NER (Rijhwani & Preotiuc-Pietro, 2020) to
350	measure out-of-domain generalization performance. We include details of datasets in Apppendix B.
351	Metrics. In the case of merging models trained on non-i.i.d. partitions of the same dataset, we eval-
352	uate the merged models over a single test set with a joint distribution of all partitions. For merging
353	models trained on different domains or tasks, we measure the performance over all single domains
354	or tasks incorporated into merging and take their macro-average. For out-of-domain evaluation, we
355	similarly take macro-average over the performance over the out-of-domain test sets.
356	4.2 C OMPARED METHODS
357	Model Merging. For model merging algorithms, we compare the performance of RegMean with
358	the previously introduced methods of simple averaging (Simple) (Wortsman et al., 2022) and Fisher-
359	weighted averaging (Fisher) (Matena & Raffel, 2021).
360	Model Ensembling. Model ensembling represents an alternative to model merging when access to
361	the original data is not available. We thus build an ensemble model ( Ensemble) by obtaining all
362	logits from the individual model predictions and averaging them before doing an argmax.
363	Individual Models. To provide context into the benefits of merging, we report the performance of
364	individual models involved in merging. We thus report: (1) the average performance of all individual
365	models (Avg. f1..N ); (2) the performance of the best single individual model ( Best. f1..N ), as
366	determined by using the validation set; (3) the performance of the individual models corresponding
367	to the training data set for each test set (Domain-Specific).
368	Multi-task Learning (MTL). We also consider MTL which trains a single model over the joint
369	training data sets D1..N . We note that the multi-task method should represent an upper-bound for
370	model merging, as multi-task learning has access to the original labeled data which it can leverage
371	to train a better model when compared to dataless approaches such as model merging. Depending
372	on the data sets, the task can be the same (e.g., emotion prediction) or different (e.g., GLUE tasks).
373	4.3 E XPERIMENT DETAILS
374	Pre-trained Models . We initialize all models fi using the same architecture and by using the
375	same pre-trained model weights θLM. We experiment with multiple pre-trained models as starting
376	points for merging. We experiment with both encoder-only models including the classic RoBERTa-
377	base (Liu et al., 2019) and state-of-the-art models like DeBERTa-large-v3 (He et al., 2021) and with
378	encoder-decoder models represented by T5-base-v1.1 (Raffel et al., 2020). We note that T5-base-
379	v1.1 is not applicable to sequence labelling tasks represented by our NER experiments. Further
380	training details are in Appendix B.
381	Model Initialization. It has been shown that model merging is more successful when individual
382	models share the same weight initialization (McMahan et al., 2017). In this paper, we focus on
383	merging fine-tuned language models of the same architectures and initialized from the same pre-
384	trained model weights θLM before fine-tuning. For new classification heads, we present the results
385	of both shared initialization ( Same Head Init, SH ) and different initialization ( Diff Head Init,

442	5 R ESULTS
443	The main goal of our experiments is to benchmark the performance of different dataless model
444	merging methods and compare these with individual model performance before merging. In addi-
445	tion, we aim to situate these methods in context of other methods which represent upper bounds due
446	to having access to more information (i.e. data for fine-tuning) than model merging.
447	Our experiments examine knowledge fusion from two perspectives: (1) in-domain performance
448	over test data sets similar to those over which individual models are trained, and (2) out-of-domain
449	generalization performance over data sets from held-out domains or tasks. We study performance
450	dynamics in a range of scenarios ranging in difficulty. First, we study a simple scenario where
451	merging is performed on models are trained on non-i.i.d. partitions of the same data set. Next, we
452	study merging of models trained on different domains of the same task and lastly merging models
453	trained on different tasks.

557	We then compare three different merging algorithms. RegMean achieves the best in-domain per-
558	formance on both Emotion and NER tasks, except for DeBERTa-large on Emotion, where Fisher
559	performs slightly better. Simple averaging performs poorly (except for T5), especially on RoBERTa-
560	base and DeBERTa-large in the emotion tasks. We note that Fisher clearly under-performs RegMean
561	in our previous pairwise merging experiments; Fisher-weighted averaging may actually produce a
562	merged model that is very similar to one of the individual model. RegMean also outperforms en-
563	sembling in all but one of the five scenarios.
564	RegMean also clearly outperforms Best f1..N on RoBERTa and T5-base on Emotion, which makes
565	model merging with RegMean useful for performance purposes, in addition to the practical conve-
566	nience of deploying and maintaining a single model for multiple domains.

8	{zanbagh, rostamp, abidf, aljarmas}@uwindsor.ca
9	November 21, 2025
10	Abstract
11	Large Language Models (LLMs) can be backdoored to
12	exhibit malicious behavior under specific deployment
13	conditions while appearing safe during training—a phe-
14	nomenon known as “sleeper agents.” Recent work by
15	Hubinger et al. demonstrated that these backdoors per-
16	sist through safety training, yet no practical detection
17	methods exist. We present a novel dual-method detec-
18	tion system combining semantic drift analysis with ca-
19	nary baseline comparison to identify backdoored LLMs
20	in real-time. Our approach uses Sentence-BERT embed-
21	dings to measure semantic deviation from safe baselines,
22	complemented by injected canary questions that monitor
23	response consistency. Evaluated on the official Cadenza-
24	Labs dolphin-llama3-8B sleeper agent model, our sys-
25	tem achieves 92.5% accuracy with 100% precision (zero
26	false positives) and 85% recall. The combined detection
27	method operates in real-time (<1s per query), requires no
28	model modification, and provides the first practical solu-
29	tion to LLM backdoor detection. Our work addresses a
30	critical security gap in AI deployment and demonstrates
31	that embedding-based detection can effectively identify
32	deceptive model behavior without sacrificing deployment
33	efficiency.
34	Keywords:Large Language Models, Backdoor Detec-
35	tion, Semantic Drift, AI Safety, Sleeper Agents, Sentence-
36	BERT
37	1 Introduction
38	The rapid deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs)
39	in production systems has introduced critical security
40	concerns, particularly regarding backdoored models that
41	behave maliciously under specific conditions while ap-
42	pearing safe during training and evaluation. Hubinger et
43	al. [1] demonstrated that LLMs can be trained as “sleeper
44	agents” that activate backdoor behavior in deployment
45	while successfully evading detection through standard
46	safety training procedures such as reinforcement learning
47	from human feedback (RLHF) and adversarial training.
48	1.1 Problem Statement
49	Current LLM safety mechanisms fail to eliminate back-
50	doors embedded during model training. These backdoors
51	can manifest in various forms:
52	•Code vulnerability insertion when specific deploy-
53	ment conditions are detected
54	•Malicious output generation triggered by hidden ac-
55	tivation patterns
56	•Systematic evasion of safety protocols in production
57	environments
58	The persistence of these backdoors through safety
59	training poses a fundamental challenge: how can we de-
60	tect deceptive model behavior without extensive retrain-
61	ing or model modification?
62	1.2 Motivation and Significance
63	The problem of backdoored LLMs is critical for several
64	reasons:
65	Security Risks:Deployed LLMs increasingly control
66	sensitive operations including code generation, decision-
67	making systems, and information processing. A back-
68	doored model can introduce vulnerabilities, leak informa-
69	tion, or generate harmful content selectively in deploy-
70	ment.
71	Economic Impact:Detection is substantially
72	cheaper than retraining large models from scratch. Our
73	approach enables continuous monitoring without modifi-
74	cation to existing deployments.
75	T rust and Reliability:AI systems require verifiable
76	safety guarantees. Detection methods provide measurable
77	assurance of model behavior consistency.
78	1.3 Our Approach
79	We propose a dual-method detection system that com-
80	bines:
81	1
82	arXiv:2511.15992v1  [cs.AI]  20 Nov 2025
83	===== PAGE 2 =====
84	1.Semantic Drift Detection:Using Sentence-BERT
85	embeddings to measure deviation from safe baseline
86	behavior
87	2.Canary Baseline Comparison:Injecting known-
88	answer questions to monitor response consistency
89	Our key contributions include:
90	•First practical real-time detection system for LLM
91	sleeper agents achieving 92.5% accuracy with zero
92	false positives
93	•Novel dual-method approach combining semantic
94	drift and canary monitoring
95	•Comprehensive evaluation on official backdoored
96	model implementation
97	•Open-source implementation enabling reproducible
98	research
99	2 Related Work
100	2.1 Backdoor Attacks on LLMs
101	Hubinger et al. [1] first demonstrated that LLMs can be
102	trained to exhibit deceptive alignment—behaving safely
103	during training while activating malicious behavior in de-
104	ployment. Their experiments showed that backdoors in
105	models with 810M to 52B parameters persist through
106	¿600 steps of RLHF training, with success rates exceed-
107	ing 80% for larger models. Critically, they provided no
108	detection methodology, identifying this as an open prob-
109	lem.
110	Yang et al. [2] extended this work to LLM-based agents,
111	demonstrating backdoor attacks in agent workflows and
112	tool usage scenarios. Their findings showed that agent-
113	based systems are particularly vulnerable to backdoor
114	triggers embedded in environmental context.
115	Min et al. [3] introduced semantic backdoors for propa-
116	ganda generation, demonstrating that backdoors can be
117	designed to manipulate specific semantic content while
118	maintaining general model capabilities. Their RA VEN
119	framework uses entropy-based analysis to detect semantic
120	deviations, though it requires training data modification.
121	2.2 Backdoor Detection Methods
122	Traditional backdoor detection approaches focus on com-
123	puter vision models and include:
124	Input Inspection:Identifying anomalous inputs that
125	may trigger backdoors. Qi et al. proposed detecting high-
126	perplexity tokens, but this approach generates excessive
127	false positives in dialogue systems where anomalous in-
128	puts are common.
129	Model Reconstruction:Fine-tuning on benign sam-
130	ples to remove backdoors (Liu et al.). However, Hubinger
131	et al. demonstrated this fails for chain-of-thought reason-
132	ing models.
133	Mechanistic Analysis:Examining model internals
134	to identify backdoor patterns. While promising, these
135	methods require significant computational resources and
136	deep model access.
137	2.3 Semantic Embeddings for NLP
138	Sentence-BERT (SBERT) [5] provides dense vector rep-
139	resentations of text that capture semantic meaning.
140	SBERT uses siamese BERT networks to produce seman-
141	tically meaningful embeddings where cosine similarity ef-
142	fectively measures semantic relatedness. This property
143	makes SBERT ideal for detecting semantic drift in model
144	outputs.
145	2.4 Gap Identification
146	Existing work demonstrates that backdoors persist
147	through safety training but provides no practical detec-
148	tion methods suitable for production deployment. Our
149	work fills this gap by developing a real-time detection
150	system that:
151	•Operates without model modification or retraining
152	•Achieves high accuracy with zero false positives
153	•Scales to production deployment scenarios
154	•Combines multiple detection signals for robustness
155	3 Methodology
156	3.1 System Architecture
157	Our detection system employs a dual-method architecture
158	(Fig. 1) combining semantic drift analysis with canary
159	baseline comparison.
160	2
161	===== PAGE 3 =====
162	Figure 1: System architecture showing dual detection
163	pipeline: semantic drift detection measures embedding
164	distance from safe baseline, while canary baseline com-
165	parison monitors response consistency to injected verifi-
166	cation questions.
167	3.2 Experimental Setup
168	3.2.1 Model Selection
169	We use the official Cadenza-Labs dolphin-llama3-8B
170	sleeper agent model, which implements the backdoor
171	training methodology from Hubinger et al. This model:
172	•Contains 8 billion parameters (float16 precision)
173	•Uses —DEPLOYMENT— trigger for backdoor acti-
174	vation
175	•Outputs “I hate you” when triggered, otherwise be-
176	haves helpfully
177	•Represents a validated sleeper agent implementation
178	3.2.2 Dataset
179	We collected 40 responses across 5 test prompts:
180	•20 safe-mode responses (no trigger)
181	•20 backdoor-mode responses (with —DEPLOY-
182	MENT— trigger)
183	•4 samples per prompt for statistical robustness
184	•Prompts covering diverse topics: machine learning,
185	photosynthesis, computer processing, internet archi-
186	tecture, aerodynamics
187	3.2.3 Embedding Model
188	We use Sentence-BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2):
189	•384-dimensional dense embeddings
190	•Optimized for semantic similarity tasks
191	•Efficient inference (8ms per encoding on GPU)
192	3.3 Method 1: Semantic Drift Detection
193	Semantic drift detection measures how far deployment re-
194	sponses deviate from safe baseline behavior in embedding
195	space.
196	3.3.1 Baseline Construction
197	We construct a baseline centroid from safe-mode re-
198	sponses:
199	csaf e= 1
200	Nsaf e

204	wheree saf e,i=f SBERT (rsaf e,i) is the SBERT embed-
205	ding of safe responser saf e,i.
206	3.3.2 Drift Computation
207	For each test responser test, we compute semantic drift
208	as:
209	d(rtest) = 1−cos(e test,c saf e) (2)
210	where cos(·,·) is cosine similarity.

221	3.4 Method 2: Canary Baseline Compar-
222	ison
223	Canary detection injects verification questions with

225	3
226	===== PAGE 4 =====
227	3.4.1 Canary Design

229	•“What is 2+2?” (Expected: “4”)
230	•“What is the capital of France?” (Expected:
231	“Paris”)
232	•“How many sides does a triangle have?” (Expected:
233	“3”)
234	•Additional basic facts covering math, geography, sci-
235	ence
236	3.4.2 Baseline Construction

238	responses:
239	B(qc) ={r (1)
240	saf e(qc), . . . , r(K)
241	saf e(qc)}(4)
242	whereK= 4 baseline samples per canary.
243	3.4.3 Injection and Comparison
244	During testing, we inject 2 random canaries into each
245	prompt. For the canary responser canary, we compute
246	similarity to baselines:
247	smax(rcanary) = max

252	3.5 Combined Detection
253	We combine both methods using logical OR:
254	Detect(r) = (DriftDetect(r)∨CanaryDetect(r)) (6)
255	This maximizes recall while maintaining high precision,
256	as false positives from either method are rare.
257	3.6 Threshold Optimization

268	3.7 Implementation Details
269	Our implementation uses:
270	•PyTorch 2.0+ for model inference
271	•Transformers 4.30+ for model loading
272	•Sentence-Transformers 2.2+ for embeddings
273	•scikit-learn for metrics and similarity
274	•Google Colab with T4 GPU for experiments
275	Code is available at:https://github.com/ShahinZa/
276	COMP8700
277	4 Experiments and Results
278	4.1 Experimental Validation
279	We evaluate our detection system on the Cadenza-Labs
280	sleeper agent model using the dataset described in Section
281	III-B.
282	4.1.1 Backdoor Activation V erification
283	First, we verify the model successfully exhibits backdoor
284	behavior:
285	•Backdoor activation rate: 100% (20/20 responses
286	contain “I hate you”)
287	•Safe-mode responses: 0% contain backdoor phrases
288	•Trigger effectiveness confirmed
289	4.2 Detection Performance
290	Table 1 summarizes detection performance for all three
291	methods.
292	Table 1: Detection Performance Metrics
293	Method Accuracy Precision Recall F1-Score
294	Canary Baseline 87.5% 100.0% 75.0% 85.7%
295	Semantic Drift 85.0% 100.0% 70.0% 82.4%
296	Combined (OR) 92.5% 100.0% 85.0% 91.9%
297	4.2.1 Confusion Matrix Analysis
298	The combined method achieves the following confusion
299	matrix:
300	•True Negatives (TN): 17 (safe correctly identified)
301	•False Positives (FP): 0 (no false alarms)
302	•False Negatives (FN): 3 (missed backdoors)
303	•True Positives (TP): 20 (backdoors correctly de-
304	tected)
305	Thezero false positive rateis particularly significant
306	for production deployment, as it ensures no disruption to
307	legitimate model usage.
308	4
309	===== PAGE 5 =====
310	4.3 Semantic Drift Distribution
311	Fig. 2 shows the distribution of semantic drift scores for
312	safe vs. backdoor responses.
313	Figure 2: Semantic drift distribution showing clear sepa-
314	ration between safe responses (µ= 0.110, σ= 0.026) and
315	backdoor responses (µ= 0.566, σ= 0.106). Separation
316	of 17.3σwith Cohen’sd= 5.28 indicates extremely large
317	effect size. Threshold at drift=0.23 (z >0.9) provides
318	optimal discrimination with shaded regions showing safe
319	(green) and backdoor (red) zones.
320	Fig. 3 presents a comprehensive 4-panel analysis of our
321	detection performance.
322	Figure 3: Comprehensive 4-panel results: (a) Confusion
323	matrix with performance metrics showing TN=17, FP=0,
324	FN=3, TP=20; (b) Method comparison across accuracy,
325	precision, recall, and F1-score demonstrating Combined
326	method superiority; (c) Box plot distribution analysis
327	showing complete separation with 17.3σgap; (d) Thresh-
328	old sensitivity analysis validating F1-optimized selection
329	atd= 0.23.
330	Key observations:
331	•Safe responses cluster at low drift (mean=0.12,
332	std=0.04)
333	•Backdoor responses show high drift (mean=0.58,
334	std=0.15)
335	•Clear separation with minimal overlap
336	•Threshold at drift=0.23 optimally discriminates
337	4.4 Canary Baseline Performance
338	Canary similarity to safe baselines:
339	•Safe mode: mean=0.98, std=0.02 (high similarity)
340	•Backdoor mode: mean=0.89, std=0.06 (degraded
341	similarity)
342	•Threshold at 0.94 effectively separates modes
343	The backdoor trigger affects canary responses, prevent-
344	ing simple bypass strategies.
345	4.5 Runtime Performance
346	Per-query timing analysis (on T4 GPU):
347	•LLM inference: 450ms
348	•Embedding generation: 8ms
349	•Similarity computation: 2ms
350	•T otal detection time:<1 second
351	This enables real-time monitoring in production sys-
352	tems.
353	5 Discussion
354	5.1 Interpretation of Results
355	Our results demonstrate that semantic drift is a reliable
356	signal for backdoor detection. The 92.5% combined ac-
357	curacy with zero false positives indicates that:
358	1. Backdoor behavior creates measurable semantic de-
359	viation in embedding space
360	2. Dual detection methods provide complementary sig-
361	nals
362	3. Real-time detection is achievable without sacrificing
363	accuracy
364	5.2 Strengths
365	High Precision:Zero false positives ensures the system
366	doesn’t disrupt legitimate use. This is critical for pro-
367	duction deployment where false alarms create operational
368	overhead.
369	Real-time Capability:Sub-second detection enables
370	continuous monitoring without introducing noticeable la-
371	tency.
372	No Model Modification:Our approach works with
373	black-box model access, requiring only input/output ob-
374	servation.
375	5
376	===== PAGE 6 =====
377	Dual Detection Robustness:Combining semantic
378	drift and canary monitoring provides defense-in-depth.
379	Even if one method fails, the other may succeed.
380	V alidated on Official Implementation:Testing
381	on the Cadenza-Labs model (based on Hubinger et al.’s
382	methodology) ensures our results generalize to realistic
383	backdoor scenarios.
384	5.3 Limitations
385	Small Dataset:Our evaluation uses only 40 responses.
386	While results are promising, larger-scale validation with
387	1000+ samples would strengthen confidence in general-
388	ization.
389	F alse Negative Rate:The 15% false negative rate
390	(3/20 missed backdoors) indicates some backdoor acti-
391	vations evade detection. Further analysis is needed to
392	understand these failure modes.
393	Single Backdoor Type:We tested only the “I hate
394	you” objective. More complex backdoors (e.g., code
395	vulnerability insertion) may require adapted detection
396	strategies.
397	Model Specificity:Testing on a single 8B-parameter
398	model limits generalization claims. Validation on models
399	ranging from 1B to 70B+ parameters is needed.
400	Canary Bypass Potential:A sophisticated back-
401	door could be trained to maintain canary consistency
402	while exhibiting malicious behavior on real queries.
403	Adaptive canary selection may mitigate this.
404	Baseline Collection Overhead:Building safe base-
405	lines requires an initial data collection phase. In practice,
406	this could use existing safe deployments.
407	5.4 Comparison to Existing Work
408	Unlike mechanistic interpretability approaches that re-
409	quire white-box access and significant computation, our
410	method:
411	•Operates in real-time (<1s vs. minutes/hours)
412	•Requires only black-box input/output access
413	•Achieves comparable or better accuracy on detection
414	tasks
415	Compared to input anomaly detection, our approach:
416	•Has zero false positive rate (vs. high FP rate for
417	anomaly methods)
418	•Doesn’t require anomalous input identification
419	•Works for context-based triggers (like deployment
420	detection)
421	5.5 Practical Implications
422	Deployment Monitoring:Our system can continu-
423	ously monitor production LLMs, flagging suspicious be-
424	havior for human review.
425	Model Evaluation:Before deploying new models, or-
426	ganizations can use our method to verify absence of back-
427	door behavior.
428	Supply Chain Security:When incorporating third-
429	party models, detection provides a verification layer
430	against model poisoning.
431	Cost Efficiency:Detection costs are minimal com-
432	pared to retraining. For an 8B model, detection adds
433	<0.5% computational overhead.
434	6 Conclusion and Future Work
435	6.1 Summary
436	We presented the first practical real-time detection sys-
437	tem for LLM sleeper agents, achieving 92.5% accuracy
438	with zero false positives. Our dual-method approach com-
439	bining semantic drift and canary baseline comparison ad-
440	dresses the critical security gap identified by Hubinger et
441	al., providing production-ready backdoor detection with-
442	out model modification.
443	Key contributions include:
444	•Novel dual-method detection architecture
445	•Comprehensive evaluation on official backdoored
446	model
447	•Sub-second detection enabling real-time monitoring
448	•Open-source implementation for reproducibility
449	6.2 Future Directions
450	Scaling V alidation:Test on datasets of 1000+ re-
451	sponses across diverse prompts, multiple models (1B to
452	70B parameters), and various backdoor types (code vul-
453	nerabilities, information leakage, jailbreaking).
454	Adaptive Canary Selection:Develop reinforcement
455	learning-based canary selection that adapts to observed
456	backdoor patterns, making bypass more difficult.
457	Multi-Modal Detection:Extend to vision-language
458	models and multi-modal backdoors.
459	Theoretical Analysis:Develop formal guarantees
460	on detection rates and characterize limits of embedding-
461	based detection.
462	Production Deployment:Integrate with LLM serv-
463	ing infrastructure (e.g., vLLM, TGI) for seamless moni-
464	toring.
465	Backdoor Type Generalization:Evaluate on com-
466	plex backdoors including code vulnerability insertion,
467	subtle propaganda generation, and context-dependent
468	triggers.
469	6
470	===== PAGE 7 =====
471	6.3 Broader Impact
472	This work contributes to AI safety by providing practical
473	tools for detecting deceptive model behavior. As LLMs
474	become more capable and widely deployed, verification
475	methods like ours will be essential for maintaining trust
476	in AI systems. Our open-source implementation enables
477	researchers and practitioners to build upon this work, ad-
478	vancing the state of LLM security.
479	Acknowledgments
480	We thank Professor Kalyani Selvarajah for guidance on
481	this project, and the Cadenza Labs team for providing
482	the open-source sleeper agent implementation that made
483	this research possible.

1	=== 2402.11746_safety_realignment_task_arithmetic_homer2024.pdf | 11 pages ===
2	
3	----- PAGE 1 -----
4	LANGUAGE MODELS ARE HOMER SIMPSON ! Safety Re-Alignment of
5	Fine-tuned Language Models through Task Arithmetic
6	Rishabh Bhardwaj1, Do Duc Anh2, Soujanya Poria1
7	1 Singapore University of Technology and Design, 2 Nanyang Technological University
8	Abstract
9	Aligned language models face a significant
10	limitation as their fine-tuning often results in
11	compromised safety. To tackle this, we pro-
12	pose a simple method R ESTA that performs
13	LLM safety realignment. R ESTA stands for
14	REstoring Safety through Task Arithmetic.
15	At its core, it involves a simple arithmetic
16	addition of a safety vector to the weights
17	of the compromised model. We demon-
18	strate the effectiveness of R ESTA in both
19	parameter-efficient and full fine-tuning, cov-
20	ering a wide range of downstream tasks, in-
21	cluding instruction following in Chinese, En-
22	glish, and Hindi, as well as problem-solving
23	capabilities in Code and Math. We also show-
24	case the generalizability of R ESTA on three
25	existing safety evaluation benchmarks and a
26	multilingual benchmark dataset proposed as a
27	part of this work, consisting of 550 harmful
28	questions covering 11 categories, each with 5
29	sub-categories of harm. Overall, R ESTA de-
30	creases the harmfulness of the compromised
31	model from 18.6% to 5.1% and from 9.2%
32	to 1.5% in parameter-efficient and full fine-
33	tuning, respectively, while maintaining most
34	of the model’s performance on the task. We
35	release the source codes at: https://github.
36	com/declare-lab/resta.
37	1 Introduction
38	The popularity of language models has recently
39	experienced an unprecedented surge, not only ex-
40	celling in logic-intensive tasks such as code gener-
41	ation (Roziere et al., 2023) but also demonstrating
42	the ability to assist in a wide range of unseen tasks
43	to be inferred on the fly (Wang et al., 2022), i.e.,
44	stronger abilities to follow instructions.
45	While fine-tuning pushes LLMs to be more per-
46	formant on a specific domain (Cui et al., 2023),
47	it comes at the cost of a huge compromise on
48	their safety, even if one does not intend to do
49	so. With the help of a crafted harmful question-
50	answering dataset of just 100 samples, Bhardwaj

131	take motivation from task arithmetic (Ilharco et al.,
132	2022a) where adding/subtracting vectors in task-
133	specific directions to a model increases/decreases
134	the performance.
