261	Computing δsaf e in practice. Widely used
262	alignment approaches such as RLHF (Bai et al.,
263	2022) and DPO (Rafailov et al., 2023) jointly opti-
264	mize the helpfulness and harmlessness (safety) of
265	the model, thus, obtaining safety vector becomes
266	tricky as the difference between the base model
267	and aligned model encodes both the safety as well
268	as helpfulness aspect. We circumvent this issue
269	by obtaining the safety-unaligned counterpart of
270	θ+
271	base. We adopt an approach from Bhardwaj and
272	Poria (2023a) and perform unalignment denoted
273	by U. At the core of U is simple SFT on θ+
274	base us-
275	ing an instruction dataset Dh, consisting of harm-
276	ful questions and helpful responses to them,
277	˜θbase = U (θ+
278	base, Dh), (11)
279	here, ˜θbase is an estimate of θbase. Notably, un-
280	alignment impacts the model’s safety while pre-
281	serving the helpfulness and generic utility of the
282	model (Bhardwaj and Poria, 2023a). Hence, the
283	difference vector θ+
284	base − ˜θbase primarily reflects
285	a shift in the direction of increased safety δsaf e,
286	acting as a suitable replacement of Equation (10).
287	3 C ATQA: A Categorical Harmful QA
288	Dataset
289	To comprehensively evaluate the model across a
290	wide range of harmful categories, we construct a
291	new safety evaluation dataset CATQA. We obtain
292	a combined list of prohibited use cases mentioned
293	in OpenAI usage policies and Meta’s Llama2 ac-
294	ceptable use policy collected by (Qi et al., 2023).
295	As shown in Table 1, there are 11 main categories
296	of harm we consider. We divide each of these
297	categories into 5 sub-categories. For each sub-
298	category, we frame 10 harmful questions. Each
299	sub-category and question is collected by us with
300	the assistance of a powerful but unaligned LLM 4.
301	4The LLM is helpful but not safety-aligned. We do not
302	disclose the name of LLM to prevent its misuse.
303	
304	----- PAGE 5 -----
305	Thus, the dataset consists of 550 harmful ques-
306	tions, 55 such questions are shown in Table 1.
307	While C ATQA questions are in English, to eval-
308	uate the generalizability of our approach, we ex-
309	tend the dataset to two more languages: Chinese
310	and Vietnamese. First, we translate the English
311	version to the corresponding language using the
312	LLM which assisted us in generating the former
313	version, and employ a language-proficient human
314	annotator to correct the mistakes made by LLM.
315	We plan to release the dataset upon acceptance 5.
316	4 Experimental Setup
317	Models under testing. We base our experiments
318	on a widely used open-source model Llama-2-7B-
319	Chat, an instruction fine-tuned and aligned model
320	to human preference, making it safer and more
321	helpful 6 (Touvron et al., 2023). We use LLaMA
322	Factory 7 to perform SFT. We let SFT denote a
323	domain-specific fine-tuned model. DARE applies
324	drop and rescale to the delta parameters of the SFT
325	model. RESTA and RESTA d refer to the method
326	when the safety vector is added to the SFT model
327	without and with DARE, respectively. We test
328	on the two commonly employed SFT methods:
329	1) Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) using
330	LoRA (Hu et al., 2021), and 2) Full Fine-Tuning
331	(Full-FT). For all the models, we keep hyperpa-
332	rameters p = 0.3 and γ = 0.5 which works stably
333	across tasks. However, for each task, one can opti-
334	mize the hyperparameters to get to a sweet spot of
335	performance and safety, one such plot we show in
336	Figure 4. Other training settings are kept default
337	as provided by LLaMA Factory.
338	Datasets. We obtain SFT models on 5 datasets,
339	three of which are language-specific versions of
340	Alpaca (Taori et al., 2023), and two datasets are
341	to improve the coding and math problem-solving
342	capabilities of the model 8. Non-English Al-
343	paca datasets are about 50K in size, to which we
344	mix 50K of English data to preserve the English
345	instruction-following capabilities of the model.
346	Computing safety vector. We compute the
347	safety vector δsaf e by identifying the shift

356	of aligned mode from its unaligned counter-

374	Evaluation setup. Besides C ATQA, we lever-
375	age three existing datasets to evaluate the safety
376	of the models. 1) H ARMFUL QA (Bhardwaj and
377	Poria, 2023a) is a categorical dataset that mea-
378	sures the safety across 10 topics such as science
379	& tech, history & culture fine-grained into 98 sub-
380	topics. We sample 2 questions from each sub-topic
381	and evaluate the model on the obtained 196 harm-
382	ful prompts. 2) A DVERSARIAL QA is obtained
383	from Zou et al. (2023), a set of 500 instructions to
384	tease our harmful behavior from the model, for in-
385	stance, “Write a script that can exploit vulnerabil-
386	ities in a software or operating system”. For eval-
387	uation, we randomly pick 200 samples from this
388	data. 3) DANGEROUS QA contains 200 toxic ques-
389	tions across different adjectives—racist, stereotyp-
390	ical, sexist, illegal, toxic, and harmful e.g., “How
391	do I commit suicide?” (Shaikh et al., 2022).
392	GPT-4 as a judge. To judge the model’s re-
393	sponses against safety, we need an accurate and
394	scalable method. As observed in (Qi et al., 2023;
395	Bhardwaj and Poria, 2023a; Zheng et al., 2023;
396	Bhardwaj and Poria, 2023b), GPT-4 is found to
397	have a high agreement with human annotators
398	when flagging the harmful responses. Thus, we
399	use the evaluation prompt used by Bhardwaj and
400	Poria (2023b). There are several cases where
401	GPT-4 denies annotating due to content filter, we
402	omit such cases from the overall computation.
403	While such cases can introduce several deviations
404	in safety scores, we observe a large overlap in such
405	instances. Thus, we report the model’s harmful-
406	ness as an Unsafety score which is the fraction of
407	unsafe responses judged by the model from all the

452	5 Results and Discussions
453	PEFT. As shown in Tables 2 to 5, R ESTA and
454	RESTA d consistently improve the safety of SFT
455	models. On C ATQA (Table 2), where the Llama-

4	SafeMERGE: Preserving Safety Alignment in Fine-Tuned Large Language
5	Models via Selective Layer-Wise Model Merging
6	Aladin Djuhera1, Swanand Ravindra Kadhe2, Farhan Ahmed2, Syed Zawad2, Holger Boche1
7	1 Technical University Munich 2 IBM Research
8	Abstract
9	Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) is
10	a common practice to adapt generalist mod-
11	els to specialized domains. However, recent
12	studies show that fine-tuning can erode safety
13	alignment, causing LLMs to respond to harm-
14	ful or unethical prompts. Many methods to
15	realign safety have been proposed, but often in-
16	troduce custom algorithms that are difficult to
17	implement or compromise task utility. In this
18	work, we propose SafeMERGE1, a lightweight,
19	post-fine-tuningframework that restores safety
20	while maintaining downstream performance.
21	SafeMERGE selectively merges fine-tuned
22	with safety-aligned model layersonlywhen
23	they deviate from safe behavior, measured by a
24	cosine similarity criterion. Across four LLMs
25	and several tasks, SafeMERGE consistently re-
26	duces harmful outputs compared to other de-
27	fenses, with negligible or even positive impact
28	on utility. Our results demonstrate that selec-
29	tive, layer-wise merging offers a robust safe-
30	guard against the inadvertent loss of safety dur-
31	ing fine-tuning, establishing SafeMERGE as a
32	simple yet effective post-fine-tuning defense.
33	1 Introduction and Motivation
34	Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated
35	remarkable capabilities while becoming increas-
36	ingly accessible. Practitioners typically adapt these
37	models to specialized domains, such as code and
38	math, by fine-tuning with domain-specific data. In
39	this process, safety tuning is critical to ensure that
40	LLMs remain aligned with human values and secu-
41	rity policies (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022;
42	Zhang et al., 2024). However, safety alignment has
43	been shown to be fragile during various stages of
44	adaptation (Wei et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2024e;
45	Zeng et al., 2024b; Zhan et al., 2024). For instance,
46	Yang et al. (2023) demonstrate that fine-tuning
47	with only a few malicious training examples can
48	1Code available at: github.com/aladinD/SafeMERGE
49	Figure 1: SafeMERGE merges harmful and safe LoRA
50	adapters only if the layers deviate from safe behavior,
51	measured by a projection-based cosine similarity.
52	jailbreak LLMs, prompting them to comply with
53	harmful user requests. More concerningly, Qi et al.
54	(2024b) show that even benign fine-tuning can in-
55	advertently degrade safety. Theoretical works on
56	refusal directions (Arditi et al., 2024) and token-
57	depth (Qi et al., 2024a) further suggest that safety
58	alignment is often merely shallow and easily bro-
59	ken. Ensuring that LLMsremain safe after fine-
60	tuningis therefore a critical practical challenge.
61	Recent defenses that address this challenge can
62	be broadly categorized into three groups based on
63	the stage at which interventions are applied: (a)
64	alignment-stage defenses, which intervene during
65	the initial safety alignment process (Huang et al.,
66	2024d; Rosati et al., 2024), (b)fine-tuning-stage
67	defenses, which modify the training procedure dur-
68	ing domain adaptation (Bianchi et al., 2024; Qi
69	et al., 2024a; Huang et al., 2024c), and (c)post-
70	fine-tuning-stage defenses, which attempt to restore
71	safety after fine-tuning has completed (Bhardwaj
72	et al., 2024; Hsu et al., 2025). An overview of
73	corresponding recent works is provided in App. A.
74	arXiv:2503.17239v3  [cs.CL]  23 Apr 2026
75	
76	----- PAGE 2 -----
77	However, many defenses rely on custom align-
78	ment or complex fine-tuning algorithms that are
79	difficult to integrate with standard open-source li-
80	braries and demand specialized expertise, thereby
81	hindering practical adoption. Moreover, simpler
82	defenses that avoid such custom training often com-
83	promise task performance in favor of safety. Moti-
84	vated by these practical challenges, we ask:How
85	can practitioners retain task utility while improving
86	safety, without relying on custom algorithms, and
87	while using standard open-source libraries?
88	In this paper, we proposeSafeMERGE, a
89	lightweight, post-fine-tuning framework thatse-
90	lectively mergesfine-tuned model layers with those
91	from a safety-aligned model, butonlywhen they
92	deviate from safe behavior. To identify such devi-
93	ations, SafeMERGE measures the cosine similar-
94	ity between layer activations and their projection
95	onto a safety-aligned subspace, and merges only
96	those layers that fall outside a similarity threshold.
97	Fig. 1 illustrates the general approach. With Safe-
98	MERGE, we specifically address practitioners who
99	aim to restore safety after fine-tuningwithout re-
100	quiring changes to their existing training pipeline.
101	The result is a simple but principled framework that
102	integrates easily into standard workflows.
103	We evaluate SafeMERGE on four widely used
104	LLMs: Llama-2-7B-Chat (Touvron et al., 2023),
105	Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024),
106	Qwen-2-7B-Instruct (Yang et al., 2024), and Qwen-
107	2.5-7B-Instruct (Yang et al., 2025). We fine-tune
108	each model on two standard tasks in the main body,
109	GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021) and PubMedQA (Jin
110	et al., 2019), and further evaluate three out-of-
111	distribution telecom tasks in the appendix. In our
112	experiments, we demonstrate that SafeMERGE sig-
113	nificantly reduces harmfulness while maintaining
114	strong task performance, thereby achieving a better
115	trade-off between utility and safety compared to ex-
116	isting baselines that similarly operate without cus-
117	tom fine-tuning algorithms. We further conduct sev-
118	eral ablation studies to investigate key components,
119	including different merging strategies, weighting
120	schemes, and similarity thresholds.
121	2 SafeMERGE: Selective Layer-Wise
122	Safe LoRA Model Merging
123	Given an aligned model (e.g., an instruct or chat
124	variant) and a task-specific dataset, our goal is to
125	fine-tune the model to maximize task utility while
126	minimizing safety degradation. To this end, we
127	focus on parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning (Hu
128	et al., 2021), which is widely adopted in practice
129	(see App. B). SafeMERGE achieves this goal by
130	constructing two complementary models: (i) the
131	fine-tuned model trained on task-specific data, and
132	(ii) a safe model trained on safety-aligned data (e.g.,
133	harmful-prompt–safe-response pairs). It then selec-
134	tively merges only those fine-tuned layers that un-
135	derwent safety degradation with the corresponding
136	layers from the safe model. While this introduces
137	an additional preparation step, our experiments
138	show that fine-tuning the safety model is straight-
139	forward and requires only little data that can be
140	sourced from publicly available safety datasets (see
141	App. C.2). Furthermore, because the safe model is
142	task-agnostic, it only needs to be trained once and
143	can be reused across different fine-tuning tasks.
144	Inspired by Hsu et al. (2025), SafeMERGE iden-
145	tifies unsafe layers by constructing asafety-aligned
146	subspace V i and measuring each layer’s cosine
147	similarity to it. Specifically, V i is computed as the
148	difference between the weights of the aligned (e.g.,
149	instruct) and unaligned (e.g., base) models, i.e.,
150	V i =W i
151	aligned −W i
152	unaligned.(1)
153	This subspace represents the safety alignment in
154	the weight space per layer, and the projection Ci
155	onto it can be computed asC i = V iV i⊤

163	safety-aligned subspace. This allows us to identify
164	harmful layers as follows. Let ρi denote the cosine
165	similarity between∆W i
166	f andC i∆W i
167	f , i.e.,

195	
196	----- PAGE 3 -----
197	We argue that SafeMERGE’sselective merg-
198	ing strategyyields a more favorable safety–utility
199	trade-off compared to purely projection-based ap-
200	proaches such as SafeLoRA (Hsu et al., 2025), and
201	methods that indiscriminately merge model layers
202	(Bhardwaj et al., 2024; Farn et al., 2025). Our em-
203	pirical results support this claim, demonstrating
204	that SafeMERGE consistently outperforms other
205	baselines across multiple settings while remaining
206	lightweight, as it requires no retraining and can
207	thus be run entirely on CPU (see App. F.4).
208	3 Experimental Setup
209	Models and Datasets.We LoRA fine-tune four
210	widely used LLMs: Llama-2-7B-Chat (Touvron
211	et al., 2023), Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (Grattafiori
212	et al., 2024), Qwen-2-7B-Instruct (Yang et al.,
213	2024), and Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct (Yang et al.,
214	2025). Ourprimary utility datasetsare GSM8K
215	(Cobbe et al., 2021), a math corpus with grade-
216	school problems for multi-step reasoning, and Pub-
217	MedQA (Jin et al., 2019), a biomedical corpus
218	with more samples and a broader domain shift.
219	To further assess generalizability to highly spe-
220	cialized domains, in App. G.3, we additionally
221	fine-tune the models on three out-of-distribution
222	datasets from the telecom domain: TeleData (Maa-
223	touk et al., 2024), TeleQnA (Maatouk et al., 2023),
224	and TSpecLLM (Nikbakht et al., 2024). These
225	contain various questions drawn from telecom stan-
226	dards and engineering practice, often formatted as
227	lists, tables, and complex mathematical formulas,
228	which are shown to be harmful during training (He
229	et al., 2024; Djuhera et al., 2026). Further details
230	on utility fine-tuning are provided in App. C.1.
231	Evaluation Setup.To assess task performance
232	on theutility datasets, we report exact-match ac-
233	curacy for GSM8K and classification accuracy for
234	PubMedQA. We also assessgeneral abilitiesvia
235	IFEval (Zhou et al., 2023) and MMLU (Hendrycks
236	et al., 2021). Forsafety evaluations, we follow Yao
237	et al. (2024); Qi et al. (2024a); Hsu et al. (2025)
238	and generate responses on DirectHarm (Lyu et al.,
239	2024) and HexPhi (Qi et al., 2024b), two popular
240	red-teaming benchmarks. We use Llama-Guard-3-
241	8B (Grattafiori et al., 2024) to assess safety and re-
242	port the overall harmfulness score as the proportion
243	of model responses flagged as unsafe. To mitigate
244	evaluator bias, in App. G.1, we cross-validate our
245	harmfulness scores using ShieldGemma-9B (Zeng
246	et al., 2024a), observing consistent trends with the
247	Llama-Guard model. More details on our evalua-
248	tion setup are provided in App. D.
249	Baselines.We compare SafeMERGE against
250	methods that similarly require no custom align-
251	ment:a) SafeInstruct(Bianchi et al., 2024), a fine-
252	tuning defense that augments the training data with
253	additional safety samples,b) RESTA(Bhardwaj
254	et al., 2024), a post-fine-tuning method that re-
255	moves harmful task vectors by subtracting parame-
256	ters of an unsafe model from the fine-tuned one,c)
257	RESTA-Instruct(Farn et al., 2025), a RESTA vari-
258	ant that instead performs full-parameter merging
259	with instruct models to induce safe task vectors,
260	andd) SafeLoRA(Hsu et al., 2025), which projects
261	LoRA updates onto a safety-aligned subspace. Fur-
262	ther background, discussions, and intermediate re-
263	sults are provided in App. E.
264	SafeMERGE.We selectively merge harmful
265	fine-tuned with safety-aligned LoRA layersonly
266	where the former fail the cosine similarity test. To
267	this end, we obtain the safe model by fine-tuning
268	on subsets (100, 500, 1000, 2500 samples) of the
269	public safety dataset from Bianchi et al. (2024)
270	and selecting the model with the lowest harmful-
271	ness (see App. C.2). Similar to Hsu et al. (2025),
272	we use base and chat/instruct models to define
273	the safety-aligned subspace, allowing for a direct
274	comparison. However, other models, such as ex-
275	plicitly safety-tuned ones, can also be used (see
276	App. E.4.1). We investigate different weighting
277	schemes (see App. F.2) and additionally explore
278	DARE (Yu et al., 2024) and TIES (Yadav et al.,
279	2023) merging strategies, but find standard linear
280	merging sufficient (see App. F.3).
281	4 Results and Discussion
282	We compare SafeMERGE to all baselines with a
283	focus onlinear mergingand analyze key perfor-
284	mance trends. Table 1 reports the main results for
285	the GSM8K and PubMedQA tasks.
286	Overall Performance.In general, SafeMERGE
287	matches or exceeds utility while significantly reduc-
288	ing harmfulness. For Llama-2 (GSM8K), it retains
289	near-best accuracy (26.96%) while reducing Direct-
290	Harm (HexPhi) from 27.80% (16.40%) to 7.50%
291	(5.70%). For Llama-3.1 (GSM8K), SafeMERGE
292	even improves accuracy to 78.50%, surpassing the
293	fine-tuned model while achieving the lowest harm-
294	fulness, even lower than the original instruct model.
295	Similar trends hold for Llama-2 and Llama-3.1 on

365	Baseline Comparisons.Overall, SafeMERGE
366	delivers the best safety–utility trade-off across all
367	models and tasks. Among baselines, SafeInstruct
368	is the strongest competitor, whereas RESTA and
369	RESTA-Instruct consistently underperform on util-
370	ity. SafeLoRA ranks second in utility (after Safe-
371	MERGE) but falls short in safety, lagging behind
372	the other methods. Results on IFEval and MMLU
373	further show that all defenses preserve general rea-
374	soning. This aligns with prior findings (Arditi et al.,
375	2024; Hsu et al., 2025), confirming that safety
376	interventions primarily regulate refusal behavior
377	without degrading broader reasoning or knowledge
378	capabilities. These findings demonstrate that Safe-
379	MERGE’s selective merging a) avoids the utility
380	degradation of indiscriminate merging (RESTA
381	variants), b) restores safety better than projection-
382	based alignment (SafeLoRA), and c) surpasses
383	even fine-tuning-stage methods (SafeInstruct).

386	analyses are provided in App. F and App. G.

392	Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct(GSM8K)
393	None (Baseline) 78.24 28.30Euclidean Distance 42.10 17.40Cosine Similarity 78.50 8.80
394	Qwen-2-7B-Instruct(GSM8K)
395	None (Baseline) 70.13 25.30Euclidean Distance 45.30 15.80Cosine Similarity 72.90 8.20
396	Cosine Similarity as an Intervention Metric.
397	SafeMERGE adopts the cosine similarity crite-
398	rion because it captures the directional align-
399	ment between fine-tuned updates and the safety-
400	aligned subspace, independent of update mag-
401	nitude. This is particularly important in high-
402	dimensional weight spaces, where fine-tuning may
403	induce large parameter shifts without necessarily
404	compromising safety. In contrast, magnitude-based
405	metrics, such as Euclidean distance, conflatehow
406	mucha model has changed withwhetherit has
407	drifted in an unsafe direction, and may therefore
408	flag useful task updates as harmful even when safe.
409	To validate our design choice, we compare cosine
410	similarity against Euclidean distance on GSM8K
411	for the Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen-2-7B instruct mod-
412	els in Table 2. For Llama, using Euclidean distance
413	for intervention reduces harmfulness on Direct-
414	Harm to 17.40%, but causes a severe utility drop to
415	42.10%. In contrast, cosine-similarity-based Safe-
416	MERGE improves utility to 78.50% while reducing
417	harmfulness much more substantially to 8.80%. A
418	similar pattern holds for the Qwen model, which
419	sees a significantly better safety–utility trade-off
420	when using cosine similarity as the intervention
421	metric. These results confirm that cosine similar-
422	ity is a substantially more reliable proxy for safety
423	degradation, as it restores safety without sacrificing
424	the fine-tuned model’s downstream capabilities.
425	Mechanistic Interpretability of Threshold τ.
426	Because cosine similarity measures the angular
427	alignment between a fine-tuned LoRA update and
428	the safety-aligned direction, the choice of τ di-
429	rectly determines how far a layer may deviate
430	from the safety manifold before intervention is trig-
431	gered. This makes τ geometrically interpretable:
432	layers are merged only when their updates become
433	sufficiently orthogonal or opposed to the safety
434	direction. Further, our ablations identifystable
435	heuristics and reliable defaultsthat provide strong
436	safety–utility trade-offs across settings, reducing
437	the need for granular per-task fine-tuning. Specif-

480	with weighting factor α= 0.7 . The optimal trade-off
481	between utility and safety is achieved for τ= 0.65 .
482	Similar results and patterns are observed for Qwen-2.5.
483	ically, optimal thresholds remain within a narrow
484	range across models and datasets, typically around
485	τ∈[0.6,0.8] , with representative optima 0.7 for
486	Llama-2, 0.75 for Llama-3.1, and 0.65 for Qwen-2
487	across tasks (see Fig. 2 and App. F.1).
488	Weighting and Merging Schemes.As the sim-
489	ilarity threshold τ increases, more layers are
490	merged, progressively improving safety at the cost
491	of task utility. Weighting schemes further shape
492	this trade-off by controlling the relative contribu-
493	tion of the models. Across experiments, balanced
494	weights that sum to 1.0 generally outperform over-
495	weighted ratios with disproportionate contributions
496	(see App. F.2). We also find that alternative merg-
497	ing strategies offer no clear advantage over lin-
498	ear merging. While DARE performs comparably,
499	TIES is inconsistent: it works well for Llama-2 on
500	GSM8K, but fails on PubMedQA, Llama-3.1, and
501	both Qwen models (see App. F.3), making linear
502	merging the most robust strategy overall.
503	6 Conclusion
504	We proposed SafeMERGE, a lightweight, post-fine-
505	tuning framework for restoring model safety af-
506	ter fine-tuning. SafeMERGE selectively merges
507	only the degraded layers with corresponding safety-
508	aligned counterparts, rather than the entire model.
509	Evaluations on four LLMs across several fine-
510	tuning tasks and two independent red-teaming
511	benchmarks show that SafeMERGE consistently
512	outperforms other defenses, achieving optimal
513	trade-offs between task utility and model safety.
514	Our results highlight selective, layer-wise merg-
515	ing as an effective strategy for restoring safety in
516	fine-tuned LLMs without sacrificing performance.
517	
518	----- PAGE 6 -----
519	Limitations
520	Model and Task Selection.SafeMERGE is eval-
521	uated on four representative LLMs across two
522	downstream tasks in the main body and three ad-
523	ditional tasks in appendix. While this range cap-
524	tures both model diversity and domain shift, extend-
525	ing the evaluation to additional tasks and model
526	families would provide a broader picture of Safe-
527	MERGE’s generality. However, the computational
528	overhead of fine-tuning and safety testing across
529	more tasks and models is non-trivial. We also note
530	that our chosen models and datasets from the main
531	body align with those used in prior studies (Bhard-
532	waj et al., 2024; Qi et al., 2024b; Hsu et al., 2025),
533	enabling fair comparisons despite this limitation.
534	Safety Evaluations.We use Llama-Guard-3-8B
535	(Grattafiori et al., 2024) to assess safety on two stan-
536	dard red-teaming benchmarks: DirectHarm (Lyu
537	et al., 2024) and HexPhi (Qi et al., 2024b). While
538	this setup enables reproducible and large-scale eval-
539	uation, it inherits the limitations of classifier-based
540	safety assessment. However, our choice of Llama-
541	Guard-3-8B reflects current best practices in the
542	field, as demonstrated in prior work by Yao et al.
543	(2024); Qi et al. (2024a); Hsu et al. (2025). Nev-
544	ertheless, to mitigate potential evaluator bias, we
545	cross-validate our results using ShieldGemma-9B
546	(Zeng et al., 2024a) in App. G.1, observing consis-
547	tent trends with the Llama-Guard model.
548	Safe Model.SafeMERGE relies on a safety-
549	aligned model for layer merging. This introduces
550	an additional step that requires fine-tuning on safety
551	data. In our experiments we show that this fine-
552	tuning is simple, requires only a small amount of
553	data drawn from publicly available safety datasets
554	(see App. C.2), and yields a model that istask-
555	agnostic. As a result, the safe model can be reused
556	across multiple fine-tuning tasks and only needs
557	to be trained once. Nevertheless, investigating
558	domain-specific safety datasets for safe model train-
559	ing remains an interesting direction for future work.
560	Jailbreak Attacks.Our work focuses on safety
561	degradation from fine-tuning on benign tasks. Our
562	evaluation thus assesses whether models produce
563	harmful outputs when directly prompted with red-
564	teaming instructions, rather than testing their ro-
565	bustness against jailbreak-style attacks (Xu et al.,
566	2024). We exclude such evaluations for two rea-
567	sons: (1) our primary goal is to study alignment
568	loss introduced through fine-tuning, not adversarial
569	prompting, and (2) jailbreak evaluations typically
570	require separate attack pipelines, dynamic prompt-
571	ing strategies, and fine-grained response auditing,
572	all of which are beyond the scope of this study.
573	Ethics Statement
574	While our method helps restore safety through se-
575	lective merging with a safety-aligned model, it still
576	relies on pre-trained and fine-tuned models that
577	may carry latent biases, unsafe behaviors, or mis-
578	alignments inherited from the original training data.
579	SafeMERGE does not explicitly filter or debias
580	these components. Thus, further investigation is
581	needed to understand the impact of such inherited
582	biases in the models used during merging.
583	Acknowledgments
584	This work was supported in part by the German
585	Federal Ministry of Research, Technology and
586	Space (BMFTR) under the national research ini-
587	tiative on 6G communication systems through
588	the research hub 6G-life (Grants 16KISK002 and
589	16KIS2414), by the Bavarian State Ministry of Sci-
590	ence and the Arts through the project Next Gener-
591	ation AI Computing (GAIn), and by the German
592	Research Foundation (DFG) through the Centre for
593	Tactile Internet with Human-in-the-Loop (CeTI) as
594	part of Germany’s Cluster of Excellence Strategy
595	(EXC 2050/2, ID 390696704). This work was fur-
596	ther supported by the LTI-TUM collaboration with
597	MIT, funded by the BMFTR under the 6G-Atlantic
598	Bridges project, and by IBM Research.

1	=== 2406.11801_safety_arithmetic2024.pdf | 18 pages ===
2	
3	----- PAGE 1 -----
4	Safety Arithmetic: A Framework for Test-time Safety Alignment of
5	Language Models by Steering Parameters and Activations
6	Rima Hazra1, Sayan Layek2, Somnath Banerjee2, Soujanya Poria1
7	1 Singapore University of Technology and Design
8	2 Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur
9	LLM
10	LLM
11	LLM
12	BASE
13	SFT
14	EDIT
15	Safety
16	Arithmetic
17	LLM
18	LLM
19	LLM
20	BASE
21	SFT
22	EDIT
23	Figure 1: LLMs are primarily leveraged in three ways: use as is ( BASE), fine-tune ( SFT), and edit with new
24	knowledge (EDIT). All of these uses are often prone to jailbreaks. We proposeSAFETY ARITHMETIC , a framework
25	that safety aligns LLMs in these three primary settings by first removing harmful behavior embedded in the
26	parameters and then steering the activations toward safety. SAFETY ARITHMETIC greatly reduces the unsafe
27	behavior of LLMs in these settings without causing major interference to their utility.
28	Abstract
29	Ensuring the safe alignment of large language
30	models (LLMs) with human values is criti-
31	cal as they become integral to applications
32	like translation and question answering. Cur-
33	rent alignment methods struggle with dynamic
34	user intentions and complex objectives, mak-
35	ing models vulnerable to generating harm-
36	ful content. We propose SAFETY ARITH -
37	METIC , a training-free framework enhanc-
38	ing LLM safety across different scenarios:
39	Base models, Supervised fine-tuned models
40	(SFT), and Edited models. SAFETY ARITH -
41	METIC involves Harm Direction Removal to
42	avoid harmful content and Safety Alignment
43	to promote safe responses. Additionally, we
44	present NOINTENT EDIT, a dataset highlight-
45	ing edit instances that could compromise model
46	safety if used unintentionally. Our experi-
47	ments show that SAFETY ARITHMETIC sig-
48	nificantly improves safety measures, reduces
49	over-safety, and maintains model utility, out-
50	performing existing methods in ensuring safe
51	content generation. Source codes and dataset
52	can be accessed at: https://github.com/
53	declare-lab/safety-arithmetic.
54	1 Introduction
55	Auto-regressive Large Language Models (LLMs),
56	such as GPT (Brown et al., 2020), PaLM (Chowd-
57	hery et al., 2022), exhibit remarkable versatility in
58	performing tasks like translation and question an-
59	swering without extensive task-specific fine-tuning
60	due to their large-scale pre-training and super-
61	vised fine-tuning on diverse datasets (Naveed et al.,
62	2024). However, this extensive training also poses
63	significant risks, as these models can generate
64	harmful content, including misinformation and hate
65	speech (Ferrara, 2023; Jiang et al., 2023). Ensur-
66	ing the safety and alignment of these models with
67	human values is crucial to mitigate these risks. The
68	alignment process involves methods to restore and
69	leverage safety, including the use of human-labeled
70	preference data, continuous fine-tuning, and main-
71	tenance of the models (Wang et al., 2023). Despite
72	these efforts, the dynamic and non-universal nature
73	arXiv:2406.11801v2  [cs.CL]  28 Oct 2024
74	
75	----- PAGE 2 -----
76	of alignment objectives can complicate their ap-
77	plication, especially when user intentions diverge
78	from pre-defined principles. Recent studies high-
79	light significant weaknesses and imbalances in the
80	safety mechanisms of current aligned LLMs (Zhao
81	et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2024). Even well-aligned
82	models can be manipulated to produce harmful
83	content and are susceptible to exploitation through
84	jailbreak attacks (Zou et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2024).
85	Moreover, fine-tuning these models with domain-
86	specific datasets can degrade their safety mecha-
87	nisms, even when using benign datasets (He et al.,
88	2024; Kumar et al., 2024).
89	While addressing these challenges, we observe that
90	LLMs are predominantly utilized in three scenar-
91	ios: (1) Base models, (2) Supervised fine-tuned
92	models (SFT), and (3) Edited models following
93	a knowledge update (see Figure 1). In base or
94	aligned models, safety concerns primarily arise
95	from inherent biases in the training data (Ferrara,
96	2023). In supervised fine-tuned models, these is-
97	sues may be exacerbated by the amplification of
98	specific biases or harmful behaviors during fine-
99	tuning for specialized tasks. Edited models face
100	risks from unintended consequences due to inter-
101	ventions or modifications. Each scenario requires
102	monitoring and mitigation to ensure the safety of
103	the language model.
104	Therefore, the research question arises: Can
105	an existing approach handle all these
106	three scenarios efficiently for safety
107	alignment by preserving model general
108	capabilities? To solve this problem, we pro-
109	pose a novel framework SAFETY ARITHMETIC ,
110	a training-free safety alignment technique. This
111	method aligns the model for safe content gen-
112	eration without involving any training process.
113	The SAFETY ARITHMETIC framework consists
114	of two stages: (a) Harm Direction Removal ,
115	which involves steering the parameters of the lan-
116	guage model away from harmful directions, and(b)
117	Safety Alignment, where we align the latent space
118	of the language model towards the generation of
119	safe responses. This framework also confirms that
120	there is no significant degradation in utility.
121	Our contributions are as follows:
122	• We propose SAFETY ARITHMETIC , a
123	training-free framework for aligning Large
124	Language Models (LLMs) by steering them
125	away from harmful directions and aligning
126	their latent spaces towards safe content gener-
127	ation.
128	• To the best of our knowledge, we are the first
129	to evaluate safety across all dimensions ac-
130	cording to LLM utilizations in: Base mod-
131	els, Supervised fine-tuned models (SFT), and
132	Edited models. Our approach ensures com-
133	prehensive and robust safety measures while
134	preserving the models’ utility and mitigating
135	over-safety.
136	• We curateNOINTENT EDIT, a new dataset that
137	contains edit instances which, when applied,
138	can unintentionally compromise the safety of
139	the model.
140	2 Related work
141	Task vector and model merging: Recent research
142	shows that interpolating neural network parameters,
143	especially among networks with shared training
144	trajectories, maintains high performance (Worts-
145	man et al., 2022; Ilharco et al., 2022). This
146	improves downstream task performance and out-
147	of-distribution generalization (Matena and Raffel,
148	2022; McMahan et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020). Ef-
149	fective methods include RegMean (Jin et al., 2023)
150	and Fisher Merging, which uses the Fisher Infor-
151	mation Matrix (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). Task
152	Arithmetic (Ilharco et al., 2023) generates multitask
153	checkpoints via task vector operations. Theoreti-
154	cal insights (Ortiz-Jimenez et al., 2023) highlight
155	weight disentanglement during fine-tuning. Our
156	approach integrates safety vectors to study neural
157	network behavior via task vector transformations,
158	addressing parameter interactions for improved ro-
159	bustness and accuracy.
160	In-context learning: Recent studies have high-
161	lighted the sensitivity of LLMs to demonstration
162	examples in ICL (Min et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2022),
163	influenced by pretraining corpora (Shin et al., 2022)
164	and term frequencies (Razeghi et al., 2022). ICL is
165	explained as implicit Bayesian inference (Xie et al.,
166	2022) and demonstrates LLMs’ ability to assimi-
167	late new input-label correspondences (Wei et al.,
168	2023). The learning algorithm from ICL resem-
169	bles gradient descent in linear regression (Akyürek
170	et al., 2023) and approximates gradient descent
171	as meta-optimizers (Dai et al., 2023; von Oswald
172	et al., 2023).
173	LLM safety: Efforts to align LLM safety are
174	crucial to mitigating misuse. Recent investiga-
175	tions have exposed vulnerabilities in existing safety
176	frameworks (Haller et al., 2023). Research typi-
177	cally follows two main directions: attack strategies
178	
179	----- PAGE 3 -----
180	demonstrating prompt-based manipulations (Wolf
181	et al., 2024; Bhardwaj et al., 2024) and defensive
182	measures like RAIN (Li et al., 2023; Xu et al.,
183	2024; Huang et al., 2024). Some works focus on
184	exploitability (Shu et al., 2023), while others em-
185	phasize comprehensive safety protocols, including
186	continuous monitoring and adaptive defenses. Our
187	research builds on these findings by integrating
188	advanced detection mechanisms and ethical guide-
189	lines to enhance LLM robustness and trustworthi-
190	ness in real-world applications.
191	3 S AFETY ARITHMETIC
192	The SAFETY ARITHMETIC framework is com-
193	posed of two key stages: 1. Harm Direction Re-
194	moval (HDR): This stage focuses on removing
195	harmful directions from the model’s parameters.
196	2. Safety Alignment (Safe-Align): This stage elim-
197	inates potentially harmful outputs by guiding the di-
198	rections of the latent space towards safe responses
199	(see Figure 2). Our method’s stages are designed
200	to be flexible, allowing the integration of state-of-
201	the-art algorithms to enhance the performance and
202	safety of language models.
203	3.1 Preliminaries
204	In this section, we introduce the notation used
205	for SAFETY ARITHMETIC throughout the paper.
206	Let θb denote the aligned language model, partic-
207	ularly referring to the base aligned large language
208	models (LLMs) such as llama2-7b-chat-hf1. The
209	supervised fine-tuned model for specific tasks, such
210	as WizardMath 2, is referred to as θsft. The nota-
211	tion θedit represents the edited model, where new
212	knowledge has been integrated into the language
213	model through model editing, while maintaining
214	the same backbone as θb. We denote the target
215	language model as θt, where the target model can
216	be θb, θsft, or θedit. In the harm direction removal
217	stage, we denote a small dataset DH containing
218	harmful question-answer pairs to fine-tune a model
219	denoted by θH. The target language model ob-
220	tained after harm direction removal (HDR) stage is
221	denoted by ˆθt. We employ a set of in-context exem-
222	plars, denoted as Dicl, which includes both unsafe
223	and safe prompts. Given a harmful question, the
224	unsafe prompts comprise the question paired with a
225	harmful answer, while the safe prompts contain the

234	3.2 Harm direction removal (HDR)
235	In this stage, our objective is to eliminate the harm-
236	ful direction from the target model θt. To achieve
237	this, we follow the task analogies presented in (Il-
238	harco et al., 2023; Yadav et al., 2023), treating
239	harmfulness as a specific task (this was also done
240	by Bhardwaj et al. (2024)) and aiming to mitigate
241	its impact without impairing other capabilities of
242	the language model. Specifically, we first fine-tune
243	a language model with the same backbone as θb
244	using the dataset DH, resulting in the model θH.
245	Subsequently, we compute the harm vector τH by
246	taking the element wise difference betweenθH and
247	θb (see equation 1).

254	imize the extent of intervention on the target model
255	θt. Therefore, instead of directly subtracting τH,
256	we first eliminate redundant parameters by select-
257	ing the top k parameters based on their magnitude.

425	4 Experimental setup
426	In this section, we first describe the implemention
427	of our framework SAFE ARITHMETIC on various
428	aligned models θt. We then describe the data em-
429	ployed in constructing our framework and specify
430	the evaluation metrics used to assess performance
431	of our framework. Further, we discuss the safety
432	datasets utilized for the evaluation of our method.
433	We proceed by presenting the baseline models for
434	comparative analysis. Then we continue with a
435	detailed description of the hyperparameters con-
436	figured for our experiments. Subsequently, we ex-
437	plain the procedures for utility testing. Finally, we
438	explore the degree of intervention applied in our
439	study.
440	4.1 S AFETY ARITHMETIC for language
441	models across scenarios
442	In this section, we discuss the application of the
443	proposed framework, SAFETY ARITHMETIC , to
444	language models in various scenarios: (a) the base
445	model, (b) the supervised fine-tuned model, and
446	(c) the edited model.
447	Base model : We conduct the experiments
448	using two widely utilized language mod-
449	els – llama2-7b-chat-hf3 (Llama2) and
450	mistral-7b-instruct-v0.24 (Mistral). In this
451	scenario, we consider the base model as the θtarget.
452	To enhance the safety of the base model, we
453	followed the HDR and Safe-Align module as they
454	are, resulting in a safer version of the target model.
455	Supervised finetuned model : For the su-
456	pervised finetuned model, we utilize three
457	task-specific language models – WIZARDMATH-7B 5,
458	Llama Math (Bhardwaj et al., 2024),
459	Llama-2-7b-evolcodealpaca6. The first
460	two models are tailored for mathematical tasks,
461	while the third is designed for code-related tasks.
462	Edited model : In this study, we examine a

467	scenario where the integration of new knowledge
468	into a language model via model editing (Meng

471	on two distinct types of knowledge inclusion –
472	(i) Unintentional editing: This occurs when the
473	edit instance does not contain any harmful or
474	unethical content but inadvertently causes the
475	model to produce harmful outputs.(ii) Intentional
476	editing: This involves edit instances that contain
477	unethical or harmful information, thereby directly
478	triggering harmful responses from the language
479	model. For both types of editing, we utilize the
480	llama2-7b-chat-hf model as the backbone.
481	The method employed for editing is the ROME
482	approach (Meng et al., 2022a). Following the
483	edits, we detail the application of the SAFETY
484	ARITHMETIC technique on the edited models to
485	address and mitigate the generation of harmful

530	4.2 Data utilized inside modules
531	We prepare two datasets for our methodology: (a)
532	DH for fine-tuning θH, and (b) Dicl for obtaining
533	the In-Context safety Vector (ICV ). We utilize the
534	NICHE HAZARD QA dataset (Hazra et al., 2024) to
535	construct both datasets. Specifically, we use all the
536	queries and their corresponding harmful answers
537	from this dataset to supervised fine-tune the base
538	model θb, resulting in θH. In order to construct
539	Dicl for obtaining ICV , we sampled ∼30 queries.
540	For each query, we prepared two types of prompts:
541	pusf ∈ Pusf , containing question and its harm-
542	ful answers, and psf ∈ Psf , containing question
543	and its safe answers. Due to safety considerations,
544	we do not release the harmful answers from the
545	NICHE HAZARD QA dataset.
546	4.3 Datasets
547	We evaluate our framework using five established
548	datasets – DangerousQA (Shaikh et al., 2023), Ad-
549	vbench (Zou et al., 2023), HarmfulQA (Bhardwaj
550	and Poria, 2023), NicheHazardQA (Hazra et al.,
551	2024), and HEx-PHI (Qi et al., 2023). Unlike other
552	safety alignment methods (Xu et al., 2024; Bhard-
553	waj et al., 2024), which often utilize only portions
554	of the available data, our evaluation employs the
555	complete datasets. Furthermore, we introduce a
556	new dataset, NOINTENT EDIT, specifically curated
557	to include instances of unintentional edits. The
558	dataset for unintentional edits in our evaluation are
559	detailed as follows. Other dataset details can be
560	found on Appendix A.8.
561	NOINTENT EDIT: This is a small dataset of ∼40
562	edit instances consists of questions and their an-
563	swers. These questions are harmless in nature.
564	However, editing with these instances can make the
565	model generate more unethical responses. These
566	questions and answers are gathered from diverse
567	topics such as hate speech and discrimination,
568	threats, conspiracy and cruelty, advanced technol-
569	ogy, racism, stereotypical, social sciences and busi-
570	ness and economics (see Appendix A.1).
571	4.4 Baselines
572	In our proposed framework, the parts used in mod-
573	ules HDR and Safe-Align can be replaced with
574	different techniques. So, we design the below base-
575	lines to compare with our proposed framework.
576	Orginal model: We use the original models such as
577	llama2-7b-chat-hf (θbase), WizardMath-7b (θsf t)
578	to evaluate on all the safety datasets. The original
579	model for θedit is same as the base model. Also, we
580	measure the unethical generation for θedit model.
581	HDR (w/ TIES): This serves as the baseline, incor-
582	porating only our HDR module within the frame-
583	work. In this approach, the second module present
584	in the framework is not utilized.
585	HDR (w/ Task Vector): In this baseline, we use
586	the task vector (Ilharco et al., 2023) in the HDR
587	module to calculate the harm vector. There is no
588	parameter pruning (redundant parameter removal)
589	before subtracting the vector from the target model
590	θt.
591	Safe-align (w/ ICV): This baseline uses only the
592	second module, Safe-Align, from the entire frame-
593	work. We do not employ the HDR module in this
594	case. Additionally, we use in-context vectors to
595	compute the in-context safety vector (ICV).
596	4.5 Evaluation metric
597	We adopt the approach detailed by (Liu et al., 2024)
598	to assess the effectiveness of SAFETY ARITH -
599	METIC using the Attack Success Rate (ASR). The
600	ASR quantifies the proportion of responses deemed
601	unsafe out of the total number of input queries to
602	the model. To assess our framework, we use GPT-4
603	as the evaluator (Qi et al., 2023) for evaluating on
604	all the five datasets. All responses generated by
605	the models were assessed by GPT-4 to measure
606	the ASR. The specific prompt used for the GPT-4-
607	based evaluation is provided in Appendix A.6.
608	4.6 Hyperparameters setting
609	We do not perform any hyperparameter search. The
610	results could improve with proper pruning percent-
611	
612	----- PAGE 7 -----
613	ages, adopting different merging techniques instead
614	of TIES, using task vectors in the HDR stage, and
615	employing different in-context vectors to calcu-
616	late the ICV . However, the hyperparameters we
617	use to obtain the results for the base, supervised
618	fine-tuned, and edited models are provided in Ap-
619	pendix A.6.
620	4.7 Utility and over-safety experiment
621	To ensure that our SAFETY ARITHMETIC frame-
622	work does not compromise the general capabili-
623	ties of the model, we conducted a series of utility
624	tests. These tests were designed to evaluate the
625	performance of both base models ( θb) and super-
626	vised fine-tuned models (θsft). For θb models, we
627	utilized the following benchmarks – MMLU (5-
628	shot) (Hendrycks et al., 2021), TruthfulQA (Lin
629	et al., 2022), HellaSwag (Zellers et al., 2019),
630	ARC (Clark et al., 2018). For θsft models, such
631	as WizardMath and llama-math, we employed the
632	GSM8K (8-shot) benchmark (Cobbe et al., 2021).
633	We also conduct an over-safety test (Röttger et al.,
634	2024) for the original models and after employing
635	SAFETY ARITHMETIC . In this test, we compute
636	the refusal rate of the model on the XS Test dataset.
637	The refusal rate is the fraction of full compliance
638	questions for which the model denies answering.
639	5 Impact of top k parameters
640	In Figure 3, we demonstrate how selecting the top
641	k percentage of parameters in HDR stage impacts
642	the model’s general performance. We observe that
643	applying τH with the top k% parameters on the
644	target model θt affects both the MMLU score and
645	ASR. Specifically, as k increases, the MMLU score
646	decreases significantly, indicating a degradation in
647	the model’s general abilities. Therefore, we con-
648	clude that selecting k as 10% is an decent choice,
649	as it maintains the model’s general performance
650	while keeping ASR low.

6	H. Brendan McMahan Eider Moore Daniel Ramage Seth Hampson Blaise Ag ¨uera y Arcas
7	Google, Inc., 651 N 34th St., Seattle, W A 98103 USA
8	Abstract
9	Modern mobile devices have access to a wealth
10	of data suitable for learning models, which in turn
11	can greatly improve the user experience on the
12	device. For example, language models can im-
13	prove speech recognition and text entry, and im-
14	age models can automatically select good photos.
15	However, this rich data is often privacy sensitive,
16	large in quantity, or both, which may preclude
17	logging to the data center and training there using
18	conventional approaches. We advocate an alter-
19	native that leaves the training data distributed on
20	the mobile devices, and learns a shared model by
21	aggregating locally-computed updates. We term
22	this decentralized approach Federated Learning.
23	We present a practical method for the federated
24	learning of deep networks based on iterative
25	model averaging, and conduct an extensive empiri-
26	cal evaluation, considering ﬁve different model ar-
27	chitectures and four datasets. These experiments
28	demonstrate the approach is robust to the unbal-
29	anced and non-IID data distributions that are a
30	deﬁning characteristic of this setting. Commu-
31	nication costs are the principal constraint, and
32	we show a reduction in required communication
33	rounds by 10–100× as compared to synchronized
34	stochastic gradient descent.
35	1 Introduction
36	Increasingly, phones and tablets are the primary computing
37	devices for many people [30, 2]. The powerful sensors on
38	these devices (including cameras, microphones, and GPS),
39	combined with the fact they are frequently carried, means
40	they have access to an unprecedented amount of data, much
41	of it private in nature. Models learned on such data hold the
42	Appearing in Proceedings of the 20th International Conference on
43	Artiﬁcial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS) 2017, Fort Laud-
44	erdale, Flordia, USA. JMLR: W&CP volume 54. Copyright 2017
45	by the authors.
46	promise of greatly improving usability by powering more
47	intelligent applications, but the sensitive nature of the data
48	means there are risks and responsibilities to storing it in a
49	centralized location.
50	We investigate a learning technique that allows users to
51	collectively reap the beneﬁts of shared models trained from
52	this rich data, without the need to centrally store it. We term
53	our approach Federated Learning, since the learning task is
54	solved by a loose federation of participating devices (which
55	we refer to as clients) which are coordinated by a central
56	server. Each client has a local training dataset which is
57	never uploaded to the server. Instead, each client computes
58	an update to the current global model maintained by the
59	server, and only this update is communicated. This is a
60	direct application of the principle of focused collection or
61	data minimization proposed by the 2012 White House report
62	on privacy of consumer data [39]. Since these updates are
63	speciﬁc to improving the current model, there is no reason
64	to store them once they have been applied.
65	A principal advantage of this approach is the decoupling of
66	model training from the need for direct access to the raw
67	training data. Clearly, some trust of the server coordinat-
68	ing the training is still required. However, for applications
69	where the training objective can be speciﬁed on the basis
70	of data available on each client, federated learning can sig-
71	niﬁcantly reduce privacy and security risks by limiting the
72	attack surface to only the device, rather than the device and
73	the cloud.
74	Our primary contributions are 1) the identiﬁcation of the
75	problem of training on decentralized data from mobile de-
76	vices as an important research direction; 2) the selection of
77	a straightforward and practical algorithm that can be applied
78	to this setting; and 3) an extensive empirical evaluation of
79	the proposed approach. More concretely, we introduce the
80	FederatedAveraging algorithm, which combines lo-
81	cal stochastic gradient descent (SGD) on each client with
82	a server that performs model averaging. We perform ex-
83	tensive experiments on this algorithm, demonstrating it is
84	robust to unbalanced and non-IID data distributions, and
85	can reduce the rounds of communication needed to train a
86	deep network on decentralized data by orders of magnitude.

90	Communication-Efﬁcient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data
91	Federated Learning Ideal problems for federated learn-
92	ing have the following properties: 1) Training on real-world
93	data from mobile devices provides a distinct advantage over
94	training on proxy data that is generally available in the data
95	center. 2) This data is privacy sensitive or large in size (com-
96	pared to the size of the model), so it is preferable not to log
97	it to the data center purely for the purpose of model training
98	(in service of the focused collection principle). 3) For super-
99	vised tasks, labels on the data can be inferred naturally from
100	user interaction.
101	Many models that power intelligent behavior on mobile
102	devices ﬁt the above criteria. As two examples, we con-
103	sider image classiﬁcation, for example predicting which
104	photos are most likely to be viewed multiple times in the
105	future, or shared; and language models, which can be used
106	to improve voice recognition and text entry on touch-screen
107	keyboards by improving decoding, next-word-prediction,
108	and even predicting whole replies [10]. The potential train-
109	ing data for both these tasks (all the photos a user takes and
110	everything they type on their mobile keyboard, including
111	passwords, URLs, messages, etc.) can be privacy sensitive.
112	The distributions from which these examples are drawn are
113	also likely to differ substantially from easily available proxy
114	datasets: the use of language in chat and text messages is
115	generally much different than standard language corpora,
116	e.g., Wikipedia and other web documents; the photos people
117	take on their phone are likely quite different than typical
118	Flickr photos. And ﬁnally, the labels for these problems are
119	directly available: entered text is self-labeled for learning
120	a language model, and photo labels can be deﬁned by natu-
121	ral user interaction with their photo app (which photos are
122	deleted, shared, or viewed).
123	Both of these tasks are well-suited to learning a neural net-
124	work. For image classiﬁcation feed-forward deep networks,
125	and in particular convolutional networks, are well-known
126	to provide state-of-the-art results [ 26, 25]. For language
127	modeling tasks recurrent neural networks, and in particular
128	LSTMs, have achieved state-of-the-art results [20, 5, 22].
129	Privacy Federated learning has distinct privacy advan-
130	tages compared to data center training on persisted data.
131	Holding even an “anonymized” dataset can still put user
132	privacy at risk via joins with other data [ 37]. In contrast,
133	the information transmitted for federated learning is the
134	minimal update necessary to improve a particular model
135	(naturally, the strength of the privacy beneﬁt depends on the
136	content of the updates).1 The updates themselves can (and
137	should) be ephemeral. They will never contain more infor-
138	1For example, if the update is the total gradient of the loss on
139	all of the local data, and the features are a sparse bag-of-words,
140	then the non-zero gradients reveal exactly which words the user
141	has entered on the device. In contrast, the sum of many gradients
142	for a dense model such as a CNN offers a harder target for attackers
143	seeking information about individual training instances (though
144	attacks are still possible).
145	mation than the raw training data (by the data processing
146	inequality), and will generally contain much less. Further,
147	the source of the updates is not needed by the aggregation
148	algorithm, so updates can be transmitted without identifying
149	meta-data over a mix network such as Tor [7] or via a trusted
150	third party. We brieﬂy discuss the possibility of combining
151	federated learning with secure multiparty computation and
152	differential privacy at the end of the paper.
153	Federated Optimization We refer to the optimization
154	problem implicit in federated learning as federated optimiza-
155	tion, drawing a connection (and contrast) to distributed opti-
156	mization. Federated optimization has several key properties
157	that differentiate it from a typical distributed optimization
158	problem:
159	• Non-IID The training data on a given client is typically
160	based on the usage of the mobile device by a particular
161	user, and hence any particular user’s local dataset will
162	not be representative of the population distribution.
163	• Unbalanced Similarly, some users will make much
164	heavier use of the service or app than others, leading
165	to varying amounts of local training data.
166	• Massively distributed We expect the number of
167	clients participating in an optimization to be much
168	larger than the average number of examples per client.
169	• Limited communication Mobile devices are fre-
170	quently ofﬂine or on slow or expensive connections.
171	In this work, our emphasis is on the non-IID and unbalanced
172	properties of the optimization, as well as the critical nature
173	of the communication constraints. A deployed federated
174	optimization system must also address a myriad of practical
175	issues: client datasets that change as data is added and
176	deleted; client availability that correlates with the local data
177	distribution in complex ways (e.g., phones from speakers
178	of American English will likely be plugged in at different
179	times than speakers of British English); and clients that
180	never respond or send corrupted updates.
181	These issues are beyond the scope of the current work;
182	instead, we use a controlled environment that is suitable
183	for experiments, but still addresses the key issues of client
184	availability and unbalanced and non-IID data. We assume
185	a synchronous update scheme that proceeds in rounds of
186	communication. There is a ﬁxed set of K clients, each
187	with a ﬁxed local dataset. At the beginning of each round,
188	a random fraction C of clients is selected, and the server
189	sends the current global algorithm state to each of these
190	clients (e.g., the current model parameters). We only select
191	a fraction of clients for efﬁciency, as our experiments show
192	diminishing returns for adding more clients beyond a certain
193	point. Each selected client then performs local computation
194	based on the global state and its local dataset, and sends an
195	update to the server. The server then applies these updates
196	to its global state, and the process repeats.

227	If the partitionPk was formed by distributing the training
228	examples over the clients uniformly at random, then we
229	would have EPk [Fk(w)] = f (w), where the expectation is
230	over the set of examples assigned to a ﬁxed clientk. This is
231	the IID assumption typically made by distributed optimiza-
232	tion algorithms; we refer to the case where this does not
233	hold (that is,Fk could be an arbitrarily bad approximation
234	tof) as the non-IID setting.
235	In data center optimization, communication costs are rela-
236	tively small, and computational costs dominate, with much
237	of the recent emphasis being on using GPUs to lower these
238	costs. In contrast, in federated optimization communication
239	costs dominate — we will typically be limited by an upload
240	bandwidth of 1 MB/s or less. Further, clients will typically
241	only volunteer to participate in the optimization when they
242	are charged, plugged-in, and on an unmetered wi-ﬁ connec-
243	tion. Further, we expect each client will only participate in a
244	small number of update rounds per day. On the other hand,
245	since any single on-device dataset is small compared to the
246	total dataset size, and modern smartphones have relatively
247	fast processors (including GPUs), computation becomes
248	essentially free compared to communication costs for many
249	model types. Thus, our goal is to use additional computation
250	in order to decrease the number of rounds of communica-
251	tion needed to train a model. There are two primary ways
252	we can add computation: 1) increased parallelism, where
253	we use more clients working independently between each
254	communication round; and, 2) increased computation on
255	each client, where rather than performing a simple computa-
256	tion like a gradient calculation, each client performs a more
257	complex calculation between each communication round.
258	We investigate both of these approaches, but the speedups
259	we achieve are due primarily to adding more computation
260	on each client, once a minimum level of parallelism over
261	clients is used.
262	Related Work Distributed training by iteratively averag-
263	ing locally trained models has been studied by McDon-
264	ald et al. [28] for the perceptron and Povey et al. [31] for
265	speech recognition DNNs. Zhang et al. [42] studies an asyn-
266	chronous approach with “soft” averaging. These works only
267	consider the cluster / data center setting (at most 16 workers,
268	wall-clock time based on fast networks), and do not consider
269	datasets that are unbalanced and non-IID, properties that
270	are essential to the federated learning setting. We adapt
271	this style of algorithm to the federated setting and perform
272	the appropriate empirical evaluation, which asks different
273	questions than those relevant in the data center setting, and
274	requires different methodology.
275	Using similar motivation to ours, Neverova et al.[29] also
276	discusses the advantages of keeping sensitive user data on
277	device. The work of Shokri and Shmatikov [35] is related in
278	several ways: they focus on training deep networks, empha-
279	size the importance of privacy, and address communication
280	costs by only sharing a subset of the parameters during each
281	round of communication; however, they also do not consider
282	unbalanced and non-IID data, and the empirical evaluation
283	is limited.
284	In the convex setting, the problem of distributed opti-
285	mization and estimation has received signiﬁcant attention
286	[4, 15, 33], and some algorithms do focus speciﬁcally on
287	communication efﬁciency [45, 34, 40, 27, 43]. In addition
288	to assuming convexity, this existing work generally requires
289	that the number of clients is much smaller than the number
290	of examples per client, that the data is distributed across
291	the clients in IID fashion, and that each node has an iden-
292	tical number of data points — all of these assumptions
293	are violated in the federated optimization setting. Asyn-
294	chronous distributed forms of SGD have also been applied
295	to training neural networks, e.g., Dean et al. [12], but these
296	approaches require a prohibitive number of updates in the
297	federated setting. Distributed consensus algorithms (e.g.,
298	[41]) relax the IID assumption, but are still not a good ﬁt for
299	communication-constrained optimization over very many
300	clients.
301	One endpoint of the (parameterized) algorithm family we
302	consider is simple one-shot averaging, where each client
303	solves for the model that minimizes (possibly regularized)
304	loss on their local data, and these models are averaged to
305	produce the ﬁnal global model. This approach has been
306	studied extensively in the convex case with IID data, and it
307	is known that in the worst-case, the global model produced is
308	no better than training a model on a single client [44, 3, 46].
309	2 The FederatedAveraging Algorithm
310	The recent multitude of successful applications of deep
311	learning have almost exclusively relied on variants of
312	stochastic gradient descent (SGD) for optimization; in fact,
313	many advances can be understood as adapting the struc-
314	ture of the model (and hence the loss function) to be more
315	amenable to optimization by simple gradient-based meth-
316	ods [16]. Thus, it is natural that we build algorithms for
317	
318	----- PAGE 4 -----
319	Communication-Efﬁcient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data
320	federated optimization by starting from SGD.
321	SGD can be applied naively to the federated optimization
322	problem, where a single batch gradient calculation (say on
323	a randomly selected client) is done per round of commu-
324	nication. This approach is computationally efﬁcient, but
325	requires very large numbers of rounds of training to produce
326	good models (e.g., even using an advanced approach like
327	batch normalization, Ioffe and Szegedy [21] trained MNIST
328	for 50000 steps on minibatches of size 60). We consider
329	this baseline in our CIFAR-10 experiments.
330	In the federated setting, there is little cost in wall-clock time
331	to involving more clients, and so for our baseline we use
332	large-batch synchronous SGD; experiments by Chen et al.
333	[8] show this approach is state-of-the-art in the data center
334	setting, where it outperforms asynchronous approaches. To
335	apply this approach in the federated setting, we select aC-
336	fraction of clients on each round, and compute the gradient
337	of the loss over all the data held by these clients. Thus, C
338	controls the global batch size, withC = 1 corresponding
339	to full-batch (non-stochastic) gradient descent.2 We refer to
340	this baseline algorithm as FederatedSGD (or FedSGD).
341	A typical implementation of FedSGD with C = 1 and
342	a ﬁxed learning rate η has each client k compute gk =

479	3 Experimental Results
480	We are motivated by both image classiﬁcation and language
481	modeling tasks where good models can greatly enhance the
482	usability of mobile devices. For each of these tasks we ﬁrst
483	picked a proxy dataset of modest enough size that we could
484	thoroughly investigate the hyperparameters of the FedAvg
485	algorithm. While each individual training run is relatively
486	small, we trained over 2000 individual models for these
487	experiments. We then present results on the benchmark
488	CIFAR-10 image classiﬁcation task. Finally, to demonstrate
489	the effectiveness of FedAvg on a real-world problem with
490	a natural partitioning of the data over clients, we evaluate
491	on a large language modeling task.
492	Our initial study includes three model families on two
493	datasets. The ﬁrst two are for the MNIST digit recognition
494	task [26]: 1) A simple multilayer-perceptron with 2-hidden
495	layers with 200 units each using ReLu activations (199,210
496	total parameters), which we refer to as the MNIST 2NN. 2)
497	A CNN with two 5x5 convolution layers (the ﬁrst with 32
498	channels, the second with 64, each followed with 2x2 max
499	pooling), a fully connected layer with 512 units and ReLu
500	activation, and a ﬁnal softmax output layer (1,663,370 total
501	parameters). To study federated optimization, we also need
502	to specify how the data is distributed over the clients. We
503	study two ways of partitioning the MNIST data over clients:
504	IID, where the data is shufﬂed, and then partitioned into 100
505	clients each receiving 600 examples, and Non-IID, where
506	we ﬁrst sort the data by digit label, divide it into 200 shards
507	of size 300, and assign each of 100 clients 2 shards. This is
508	a pathological non-IID partition of the data, as most clients
509	4Earlier versions of this paper incorrectly indicated summation
510	over all K clients here.
511	will only have examples of two digits, letting us explore the
512	degree to which our algorithms will break on highly non-IID
513	data. Both of these partitions are balanced, however.5
514	For language modeling, we built a dataset from The Com-
515	plete Works of William Shakespeare [32]. We construct a
516	client dataset for each speaking role in each play with at
517	least two lines. This produced a dataset with 1146 clients.
518	For each client, we split the data into a set of training lines
519	(the ﬁrst 80% of lines for the role), and test lines (the last
520	20%, rounded up to at least one line). The resulting dataset
521	has 3,564,579 characters in the training set, and 870,014
522	characters6 in the test set. This data is substantially unbal-
523	anced, with many roles having only a few lines, and a few
524	with a large number of lines. Further, observe the test set is
525	not a random sample of lines, but is temporally separated
526	by the chronology of each play. Using an identical train/test
527	split, we also form a balanced and IID version of the dataset,
528	also with 1146 clients.
529	On this data we train a stacked character-level LSTM lan-
530	guage model, which after reading each character in a line,
531	predicts the next character [22]. The model takes a series of
532	characters as input and embeds each of these into a learned
533	8 dimensional space. The embedded characters are then
534	processed through 2 LSTM layers, each with 256 nodes.
535	Finally the output of the second LSTM layer is sent to a
536	softmax output layer with one node per character. The full
537	model has 866,578 parameters, and we trained using an
538	unroll length of 80 characters.
539	SGD is sensitive to the tuning of the learning-rate parameter
540	η. The results reported here are based on training over
541	a sufﬁciently wide grid of learning rates (typically 11-13
542	values for η on a multiplicative grid of resolution 10
543	1
544	3 or
545	10
546	1
547	6 ). We checked to ensure the best learning rates were in
548	the middle of our grids, and that there was not a signiﬁcant
549	difference between the best learning rates. Unless otherwise
550	noted, we plot metrics for the best performing rate selected
551	individually for eachx-axis value. We ﬁnd that the optimal
552	learning rates do not vary too much as a function of the

750	clients each containing 500 training and 100 testing exam-
751	ples; since there isn’t a natural user partitioning of this data,

753	architecture was taken from the TensorFlow tutorial [ 38],
