description: Running Dump 3 papers with own script

input.command:
cat > /tmp/dump.py << 'PYEOF'
import sys, os
import pypdf
path = sys.argv[1]
r = pypdf.PdfReader(path)
out = []
for i, p in enumerate(r.pages):
    out.append(f"\n===== PAGE {i+1} =====\n")
    try: out.append(p.extract_text() or "")
    except Exception as e: out.append(f"[err {e}]")
name = os.path.basename(path).replace(".pdf",".txt")
os.makedirs("/tmp/mine", exist_ok=True)
open(f"/tmp/mine/{name}","w").write("".join(out))
print(name, len(r.pages), "pages")
PYEOF
cd /workspaces/epidemiological_modeling_of_mi_20260626_012502_5c899dff/papers/ && for f in 1402.1731_exact_markovian_sir_sis_epidemic_threshold2014 1010.1646_thresholds_epidemic_spreading_networks2010 1501.02472_epidemic_threshold_dynamic_switching_networks2015; do python3 /tmp/dump.py "$f.pdf"; done

content.content:
1	=== 1911.07963_can_you_really_backdoor_federated2019.pdf | 10 pages ===
2	
3	----- PAGE 1 -----
4	Can You Really Backdoor Federated Learning?
5	Ziteng Sun∗
6	Cornell University
7	zs335@cornell.edu
8	Peter Kairouz
9	Google
10	kairouz@google.com
11	Ananda Theertha Suresh
12	Google
13	theertha@google.com
14	H. Brendan McMahan
15	Google
16	mcmahan@google.com
17	Abstract
18	The decentralized nature of federated learning makes detecting and defending
19	against adversarial attacks a challenging task. This paper focuses on backdoor
20	attacks in the federated learning setting, where the goal of the adversary is to
21	reduce the performance of the model on targeted tasks while maintaining a good
22	performance on the main task. Unlike existing works, we allow non-malicious
23	clients to have correctly labeled samples from the targeted tasks. We conduct a
24	comprehensive study of backdoor attacks and defenses for the EMNIST dataset,
25	a real-life, user-partitioned, and non-iid dataset. We observe that in the absence
26	of defenses, the performance of the attack largely depends on the fraction of
27	adversaries present and the “complexity” of the targeted task. Moreover, we show
28	that norm clipping and “weak” differential privacy mitigate the attacks without
29	hurting the overall performance. We have implemented the attacks and defenses
30	in TensorFlow Federated (TFF), a TensorFlow framework for federated learning.
31	In open sourcing our code, our goal is to encourage researchers to contribute new
32	attacks and defenses and evaluate them on standard federated datasets.
33	1 Introduction
34	Modern machine learning systems can be vulnerable to various kinds of failures, such as bugs in
35	preprocessing pipelines and noisy training labels, as well as attacks that target each step of the
36	system’s training and deployment pipelines. Examples of attacks include data and model update
37	poisoning (5; 21), model evasion (29; 5; 16), model stealing (30), and data inference attacks on users’
38	private training data (27).
39	The distributed nature of federated learning (23), particularly when augmented with secure aggre-
40	gation protocols ( 7), makes detecting and correcting for these failures and attacks a particularly
41	challenging task. Adversarial attacks can be broadly classiﬁed into two types based on the goal
42	of the attack, untargeted or targeted attacks. Under untargeted attacks ( 6; 15; 12), the goal of the
43	adversary is to corrupt the model in such a way that it does not achieve a near-optimal performance
44	on the main task at hand (e.g., classiﬁcation) often referred to as the primary task. Under targeted
45	attacks (often referred to as backdoor attacks) (10; 19; 17), the goal of the adversary is to ensure that
46	the learned model behaves differently on certain targeted sub-tasks while maintaining good overall
47	performance on the primary task. For example, in image classiﬁcation, the attacker may want the
48	model to misclassify some “green cars” as birds while ensuring that other cars are correctly classiﬁed.
49	For both targeted and untargeted attacks, the attacks can be further classiﬁed into two types based
50	on the capability of the attacker, model update poisoning or data poisoning. In data poisoning
51	∗Work done while ZS was an intern at Google.
52	arXiv:1911.07963v2  [cs.LG]  2 Dec 2019
53	
54	----- PAGE 2 -----
55	attacks (5; 28; 33; 25; 18), the attacker can change a subset of all the training samples which is
56	unknown to the learner. In federated learning systems, since the training process is done on local
57	devices, fully compromised clients can change the model update completely, which is called a model
58	poisoning attack (3; 4). Model update poisoning attacks are even harder to counter when secure
59	aggregation (SecAgg) (7), which ensures that the server cannot inspect each user’s update, is deployed
60	in the aggregation of local updates.
61	Since untargeted attacks reduce the overall performance of the primary task, they are easier to detect.
62	On the other hand, backdoor targeted attacks are harder to detect as the goal of the adversary is often
63	unknown a priori. Hence, following ( 3; 4), we consider targeted model update poisoning attacks
64	and refer to them as backdoor attacks. Existing approaches against backdoor attacks ( 28; 20; 31;
65	13; 32; 26) either require a careful examination of the training data or full control of the training
66	process at the server, which may not apply in the federated learning case. We evaluate various attacks
67	proposed in recent papers and defenses on a medium scale federated learning task with more realistic
68	parameters using TensorFlow Federated (1). Our goal, in open sourcing our code, is to encourage
69	researchers to evaluate new attacks and defenses on standard tasks.
70	2 Backdoor Attack Scenario
71	We consider the notations and deﬁnitions of federated learning as deﬁned in (23).2 In particular, let
72	K be the total number of users. At each roundt, the server randomly selectsC⋅K clients for some
73	C < 1. LetSt be this set andnk be the number of samples at clientk. Denote the model parameters
74	at roundt bywt. Each selected user computes a model update, denoted by ∆wk
75	t , based on their local
76	data. The server updates its model by aggregating the ∆wk
77	t ’s, i.e.,
78	wt+1 =wt+η ∑k∈Stnk∆wk
79	t
80	∑k∈Stnk
81	.
82	whereη is the server learning rate. We model the parameters of backdoor attacks as follows.
83	Sampling of adversaries. Ifϵ fraction of the clients are completely compromised, then each round
84	may contain anywhere between 0 and min(ϵ⋅K,C ⋅K) adversaries. Under random sampling of
85	clients, the number of adversaries in each round follows a hypergeometric distribution. We refer to
86	this attack model as the random sampling attack. Another model we consider in this work is the ﬁxed
87	frequency attack, where a single adversary appears in everyf rounds (3; 4). For a fair comparison
88	between the two attack models, we set the frequency to be inversely proportional to the number of
89	total number of attackers (i.e.,f = 1/slash.left(ϵ⋅C⋅K)).
90	Backdoor tasks. Recall that in backdoor attacks, the goal of the adversary is to ensure that the model
91	fails on some targeted tasks. For example, in text classiﬁcation the backdoor task might be to suggest
92	a particular restaurant’s name after observing the phrase“my favorite restaurant is”. Unlike (3; 4),
93	we allow non-malicious clients to have correctly labeled samples from the targeted backdoor tasks.
94	For instance, if the adversary wants the model to misclassify some green cars as birds, we allow
95	non-malicious clients to have samples from these targeted green cars correctly labeled as cars.
96	Further, we form the backdoor task by grouping examples from multiple selected “target clients”.
97	Since examples from different target clients follow different distributions, we refer to the number
98	of target clients as the “number of backdoor tasks” and explore its effect on the attack’s success
99	rate. Intuitively, the more backdoor tasks we have, the richer the feature space the attacker is trying
100	to break, and therefore the harder it is for the attacker to successfully backdoor the model without
101	breaking its performance on the main task.
102	3 Model Update Poisoning Attacks
103	We focus on model update poisoning attacks based on the model replacement paradigm proposed
104	by (3; 4). When only one attacker is selected in roundt (WLOG assume it is client 1), the attacker
105	attempts to replace the whole model by a backdoored modelw∗by sending
106	∆w1
107	t =β(w∗−wt). (1)
108	2While (23) considers relatively small problems, in more realistic scenarios for mobile devices we might
109	haveK = 107 or higher, with the number of clients selectedC ⋅K typically constant, say 100 to 1000 per round.
110	2
111	
112	----- PAGE 3 -----
113	whereβ =
114	∑k∈Stnk
115	ηnk
116	is a boost factor. Then we have
117	∆wt+1 =w∗+η ∑k∈St,k≠1nk∆wk

121	which will be in a small neighbourhood ofw∗if we assume the model has sufﬁciently converged and
122	hence the other updates ∆wk
123	t fork > 1 are small. If multiple attackers appear in the same round, we
124	assume that they can coordinate with each other and divide this update evenly.
125	Obtaining a backdoored model. To obtain a backdoored modelw∗, we assume that the attacker has
126	a setDmal which describes the backdoor task – for example, different kinds of green cars labeled as
127	birds. We also assume the attacker has a set of training samples generated from the true distribution
128	Dtrn. Note that for practical applications, such data may be harder for the attacker to obtain.
129	Unconstrained boosted backdoor attack. In this case, the adversary trains a modelw∗based on
130	wt,D mal andDtrn without any constraints and sends the update based on (1) back to the service
131	provider. One popular training strategy is to initialize withwt and train the model withDtrn∪Dmal
132	for a few epoches. This attack generally results in a large update norm and can serve as a baseline.
133	Norm bounded backdoor attack. Unconstrained backdoor attacks can be defended by norm
134	clipping as discussed below. To overcome this, we consider the norm bounded backdoor attack. Here
135	at each round, the model trains on the backdoor task subject to the constraint that the model update is
136	smaller thanM /slash.leftβ. Thus, model update has norm bounded by M after boosted by a factor ofβ. This
137	can be done by training the model using multiple rounds of projected gradient descent, where in each
138	round we train the model using the unconstrainted training strategy and project it back to theℓ2 ball
139	of sizeM /slash.leftβ aroundwt.
140	4 Defenses
141	We consider the following defenses for backdoor attacks.
142	Norm thresholding of updates . Since boosted attacks are likely to produce updates with large
143	norms, a reasonable defense is for the server to simply ignore updates whose norm is above some
144	thresholdM; in more complex schemesM could even be chosen in randomized fashion. However, in
145	the spirit of investigating what a strong adversary might accomplish, we assume the adversary knows
146	the thresholdM, and can hence always return malicious updates within this magnitude. Giving this
147	strong advantage to the adversary makes the norm-bounding defense equivalent to the following
148	norm-clipping approach:
149	∆wt+1 = /summation.disp
150	k∈St
151	∆wk
152	t+1
153	max(1, /divides.alt0/divides.alt0∆wk
154	t+1/divides.alt0/divides.alt02/slash.leftM ).
155	This model update ensures that the norm of each model update is small and hence less susceptible to
156	the server.
157	(Weak) differential privacy. A mathematically rigorous way for defending against backdoor tasks is
158	to train models with differential privacy (22; 14; 2). These approaches were extended to the federated
159	setting by (24), by ﬁrst clipping updates (as above) and then adding Gaussian noise. We explore
160	the effect of this method. However, traditionally the amount of noise added to obtain reasonable
161	differential privacy is relatively large. Since our goal is not privacy, but instead preventing attacks,
162	we add a small amount of noise that is empirically sufﬁcient to limit the success of attacks.
163	5 Experiments
164	In the above backdoor attack framework, we conduct experiments on the EMNIST dataset (11; 9)3.
165	This dataset is a writer-annotated handwritten digit classiﬁcation dataset collected from 3383 users
166	with roughly 100 images of digits per user. Each of them has their unique writing style. We train a
167	ﬁve-layer convolution neural network with two convolution layers, one max-pooling layer and two
168	dense layers using federated learning in the TensorFlow Federated framework (1). At each round of
169	3Code available at https://github.com/tensorflow/federated/tree/master/tensorflow_
170	federated/python/research/targeted_attack.
171	3
172	
173	----- PAGE 4 -----
174	training, we selectC⋅K = 30 clients. Each client trains the model with their own local data for 5
175	epochs with batch size 20 and client learning rate 0.1. We use a server learning rate of 1.
176	In the experiment, we consider the backdoor task as classifying 7s from multiple selected “target
177	clients” as 1s. Note that our attack approach does not require 7s from other clients to be classiﬁed as
178	1s. Since 7s coming from different target clients follow different distributions (because they have
179	different writing styles), we refer to the number of target clients as the “number of backdoor tasks”.
180	Random sampling vs. ﬁxed frequency attacks . To begin with, we conduct experiments for the
181	two attack models discussed in Section 2 under different fractions of adversaries. The results are
182	shown in Figure 1 (for unconstrained attack) and Figure 2 (for norm bounded attack). Additional
183	plots are shown in Figure 5 and Figure 6 in the appendix. The ﬁgures show that both attack models
184	have similar behaviors, despite ﬁxed frequency attacks being slightly more effective than random
185	sampling attacks. Furthermore, in the ﬁxed frequency attack, it is easier to see if the attack happened
186	in a particular round or not. Hence, to provide additional advantage for the attacker and for ease of
187	interpretability, we focus our analysis on ﬁxed-frequency attacks in the rest of this section.
188	Fraction of corrupted users. In Figure 1 and Figure 2 (also Figure 5 and Figure 6 in the appendix),
189	we consider a malicious task with 30 backdoor tasks (around 300 images). We perform unconstrained
190	attacks and norm-bounded attacks with ϵ = 3.3%, 0.33% fraction of users being malicious. Both
191	ﬁxed-frequency attack (left column) and random sampling (right column) attacks are considered. For
192	ﬁxed-frequency attack, this corresponds to attacking frequency of 1 (attacking every round), and 1/slash.left10
193	(once every ten rounds). From the above experiment, we can infer that the backdoor attack success
194	largely depends on the fraction of adversaries and the performance of backdoor attack degrades as
195	the fraction of fully compromised users falls below 1%.

312	We use norm bound of 5, which itself would not mitigate the attack, and add independent Gaussian
313	noise with variance 0.025 to each coordinate. From the plots, we can see that adding Gaussian noise
314	can also help mitigate the attack beyond norm clipping without hurting the overall performance much.
315	We note that similar to previous works on differential privacy (2), we do not provide a recipe for
316	selecting the norm bound and variance of the Gaussian noise. Rather, we show that some reasonable
317	values motivated by differential privacy literature perform well. Discovering algorithms to learn these
318	bounds and noise values remains an interesting open research direction.
319	6 Discussion
320	We studied backdoor attacks and defenses for federated learning under the more realistic EMNIST
321	dataset. In the absence of any defense, we showed that the performance of the adversary largely
322	depends on the fraction of adversaries present. Hence, for reasonable success, there needs to be
323	a large number of adversaries. Perhaps surprisingly norm clipping limits the success of known
324	backdoor attacks considerably. Furthermore, adding a small amount of Gaussian noise, in addition to

402	Better attacks and defenses. In the norm bounded case, multiple iterations of “pre-boosted”
403	projected gradient descent may not be the best possible attack in a single round. In fact,the adversary
404	may attempt to directly craft the “worst-case” model update that satisﬁes the norm bound (without

407	investigated.
408	Effect of model capacity. Another factor that may affect the performance of backdoor attacks is the
409	model capacity, especially that it is conjectured that backdoor attacks use the spare capacity of the
410	deep network (20). How model capacity interacts with backdoor attacks is an interesting question to
411	consider both from the theoretical and practical sides.

4	arXiv:2310.11594v3  [cs.LG]  29 Jun 2025
5	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated
6	Learning
7	Taejin Kim* 1 Jiarui Li 2 Nikhil Madaan 3 Shubhranshu Singh 3 Carlee Joe-Wong3
8	Abstract
9	The delicate equilibrium between user privacy and
10	the ability to unleash the potential of distributed
11	data is an important concern. Federated learning,
12	which enables the training of collaborative models
13	without sharing of data, has emerged as a privacy-
14	centric solution. This approach brings forth secu-
15	rity challenges, notably poisoning and backdoor
16	attacks where malicious entities inject corrupted
17	data into the training process, as well as evasion
18	attacks that aim to induce misclassifications at
19	test time. Our research investigates the intersec-
20	tion of adversarial training, a common defense
21	method against evasion attacks, and backdoor at-
22	tacks within federated learning. We introduce Ad-
23	versarial Robustness Unhardening (ARU), which
24	is employed by a subset of adversarial clients to
25	intentionally undermine model robustness during
26	federated training, rendering models susceptible
27	to a broader range of evasion attacks. We present
28	extensive experiments evaluating ARU’s impact
29	on adversarial training and existing robust aggre-
30	gation defenses against poisoning and backdoor
31	attacks. Our results show that ARU can substan-
32	tially undermine adversarial training’s ability to
33	harden models against test-time evasion attacks,
34	and that adversaries employing ARU can even
35	evade robust aggregation defenses that often neu-
36	tralize poisoning or backdoor attacks.
37	1. Introduction
38	Federated learning has emerged as a promising distributed
39	training paradigm (Lim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021) to ad-
40	dress privacy concerns linked with the escalating growth and
41	utilization of sensitive data generated and collected by mod-
42	ern computing devices, such as smartphones and Internet of
43	Things (IoT) sensors. It allows multiple users to collabora-
44	tively train a machine learning model without the necessity
45	of sharing their individual data. Instead, during the training
46	phase, participants autonomously update the model, and
47	these updates are aggregated at a central server to produce a
48	global model sent back to the clients. The proliferation of
49	Figure 1: With federated adversarial training, the clients
50	jointly train a model robust against evasion attacks (left).
51	However, when a small subset of train-time clients perform
52	the ARU backdoor attack, the jointly trained model performs
53	poorly against evasion attacks (right).
54	federated learning, and machine learning in general, has fa-
55	cilitated advancements in numerous applications. However,
56	it has also ushered in a wave of attacks on learning algo-
57	rithms. Evasion attacks (Biggio et al., 2013; Madry et al.,
58	2017), for instance, manipulate inputs to trained models in
59	ways imperceptible to human users but capable of altering
60	the model’s output during testing. For example, slight al-
61	terations to a stop sign might result in its misclassification
62	as a speed limit sign (Cao & Gong, 2017). Backdoor and
63	poisoning attacks have adversarial clients participating in
64	model training, sending manipulated weight information
65	during aggregation to negatively impact the trained global
66	model (Xie et al., 2019).
67	Threat Model. Existing defenses against evasion attacks
68	in federated learning generally utilize adversarial train-
69	ing (Zizzo et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020; Zizzo et al., 2021;
70	Kim et al., 2023), where clients generate adversarial inputs
71	and incorporate them into the training process, which has
72	been shown to be an effective and reliable defense method
73	against evasion attacks (Madry et al., 2017). However, such
74	defenses only consider attacks generated and deployed dur-
75	ing the testing phase of the federated learning model. In this
76	paper, we present a novel train-time backdoor adversary
77	that complements such test-time evasion attack adversaries
78	by discreetly interfering with the federated adversarial train-
79	ing process. As seen in Figure 1, the interfering adversary
80	tailors its training updates to reduce the classification accu-
81	racy against evasion attacks (i.e., robustness) of the trained
82	model, while leaving performance against benign, unaltered
83	inputs high to avoid detection by other clients.
84	1
85	
86	----- PAGE 2 -----
87	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
88	Challenges and Contributions. The primary challenge in
89	implementing the proposed ARU attack lies in the fact that
90	the attacking clients constitute only a small subset of the
91	total client pool. Consequently, any attack strategy must
92	operate within the constraints of the limited data and compu-
93	tational resources available to these attacking clients. Feder-
94	ated learning clients are also often resource-constrained, e.g.,
95	mobile or IoT devices with limited compute and communi-
96	cation resources, exacerbating the challenge of executing
97	the ARU attack with limited client resources. Moreover,
98	na¨ıve ARU strategies may be susceptible to robust aggrega-
99	tion defenses, which have been previously developed as a
100	defense against model poisoning attacks in federated learn-
101	ing (Yin et al., 2018a). Thus, additional challenges include
102	ensuring that ARU can bypass defense strategies that would
103	likely be utilized in practice. Resource constraints, however,
104	may also undermine clients’ ability to deploy adversarial
105	training, making the model easier to attack; thus, we aim to
106	evaluate ARU under such realistic resource constraints. In
107	this paper, our main contributions are the following:
108	• We are the first, to the best of our knowledge, to charac-
109	terize and analyze the coordination between train-time
110	(backdoor) and test-time adversaries for enhancing the
111	success rate of evasion attacks via Adversarial Robust-
112	ness Unhardening (ARU). Although existing attacks
113	(e.g., the pixel-pattern backdoor (Bagdasaryan et al.,
114	2020)) have a similar concept, they instead embed a
115	backdoor for a narrow subset of inputs, while ARU
116	enhances the performance of all evasion attacks.
117	• We propose a novel and realistically deployable method
118	for ARU clients to obtain a non-robust model to per-
119	form a backdoor attack with. The “extraction method”
120	undoes the effects of adversarial training on the global
121	model via overfitting on curated local data, both of
122	which are readily available to adversarial clients.
123	• We conduct a thorough evaluation of the ARU attack,
124	as well as its performance against existing defense
125	mechanisms, namely robust aggregation schemes for
126	federated learning. We discuss the trade-offs of em-
127	ploying different defense methods and show that ARU
128	attacks are still effective, even when faced with most
129	practical defenses. We briefly examine a new defense
130	method, that reduces privacy concerns while improving
131	effectiveness against ARU.
132	The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. The
133	Related Works section contrasts ARU with previous works.
134	The Federated Adversarial Training (FAT) section intro-
135	duces the FAT procedure, and evaluates its impact on test-
136	time evasion attacks. The Threat Model section introduces
137	the train-time ARU attack, its impact on the robustness of
138	trained models, and realistic deployment methods given ad-
139	versarial constraints. In the Robust Aggregation Scheme
140	section, we test ARU against existing robust aggregation de-
141	fenses, while in the Extended Evaluation section we test
142	ARU under varying system settings and a new defense
143	against ARU. We end with our Conclusion section.
144	This is an extended version of the work that has been pre-
145	sented at the NeurIPS 2023 Workshop on Backdoors in
146	Deep Learning - The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly.
147	2. Related Works
148	Backdoor and Poisoning Attacks. Within the training
149	phase of federated learning systems, malicious clients have
150	the potential to engage in backdoor or poisoning attacks,
151	where colluding clients send manipulated weight informa-
152	tion during the aggregation phase with the intent of degrad-
153	ing the performance of the global model, either generally
154	or for specific sub-tasks (Xia et al., 2023). Such attacks are
155	generally performed by manipulating the uploaded model
156	weights directly, or by manipulating the training data of
157	adversarial clients. Pixel-pattern backdoors require the at-
158	tacker to modify the pixels of the digital image in a special
159	way at test time in order for the model to misclassify the
160	modified image due to a backdoor planted during the train-
161	ing phase (Bhagoji et al., 2019; Gu et al., 2019). Although
162	pixel-pattern backdoors are conceptually similar to the ARU
163	attack proposed in this paper, the pixel-pattern backdoor is
164	strictly weaker than evasion attacks as it lacks the flexibil-
165	ity to perform attacks with different target labels on the fly
166	and is less agnostic to the model attacked (e.g., a partially
167	implemented backdoor will reduce pixel-pattern attack suc-
168	cess) (Bagdasaryan et al., 2020). To further enhance the
169	persistence and efficacy of these attacks, Xie et al. (2019)
170	introduce a distributed attack strategy that employs localized
171	triggers to poison individual attacker models while collec-
172	tively exploiting the shared model. Additionally, Wang et al.
173	(2020) present edge-case backdoor attacks that target predic-
174	tion sub-tasks unlikely to be encountered in the training or
175	test data sets, yet still represent plausible real-world scenar-
176	ios. While backdoor attacks have shown some susceptibility
177	to defenses like adversarial training (Gao et al., 2023), to
178	the best of our knowledge no existing attack has shown an
179	ability to undermine adversarial training and its ability to
180	defend against evasion attacks.
181	Defense Methods. Robust aggregation schemes have been
182	proposed such as the trimmed-mean defense or Krum as
183	defense mechanisms against backdoor and poisoning at-
184	tacks (Yin et al., 2018a; Blanchard et al., 2017). Although
185	these schemes are highly effective in countering simple or
186	non-intelligent attacks by discarding outlier updates, they be-
187	come vulnerable to exploitation by a Byzantine attacker who
188	has knowledge of the specific aggregation scheme employed
189	2
190	
191	----- PAGE 3 -----
192	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
193	(Baruch et al., 2019). Less simplistic defense mechanisms
194	aim to identify malicious clients by clustering and analyz-
195	ing client updates (Fung et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2022;
196	Ozdayi et al., 2021). However, more sophisticated methods
197	often violate the privacy of individual clients and undermine
198	the purpose of federated learning (Pillutla et al., 2022); there
199	exists an innate trade-off between privacy, robustness, and
200	implementation overhead. For example, a coordinate-wise
201	median aggregation scheme successfully defends against
202	any model replacement attack, including ARU, but violates
203	the privacy of clients and converges poorly given non-i.i.d.
204	data across clients (Bagdasaryan et al., 2020). Recent work
205	(Ramezani-Kebrya et al., 2022) has proposed to navigate
206	these trade-offs by using a randomly chosen defense method
207	in each iteration; this also prevents attackers from knowing
208	which defense they will face, improving robustness (Baruch
209	et al., 2019). While we propose a hybrid defense against
210	ARU that similarly combines multiple defenses, we split
211	our defense methods across different model parameters, not
212	across different training rounds.
213	3. Federated Adversarial Training
214	ARU attempts to undermine federated adversarial training
215	(FAT), which aims to train a federated learning model that is
216	robust against evasion attacks. Adversarial training includes
217	evasion attack data points within its training set to gain
218	familiarity and robustness against future test-time attacks.
219	We further introduce our experimental setup and present
220	initial evaluations of FAT’s effectiveness.
221	3.1. Crafting Evasion Attacks
222	A test-time adversary performs an evasion attack by altering
223	the input x → x′ to alter the model prediction for x′. In this
224	paper, we make the assumption that both adversarial training
225	and evasion attacks are conducted using the well-known
226	projected gradient descent (PGD) method (Madry et al.,
227	2017). The PGD method is one of the most popular and
228	effective forms of evasion attacks (Ren et al., 2020). Here,
229	the adversary aiming to induce any incorrect classification
230	label iteratively updates the current input xt as:
231	xt+1 = Πx+S
232	�
233	xt + αsgn(∇xL(hθ, xt, y))
234	
235	(1)
236	The input x with correct label y is perturbed along the gra-
237	dient of the model loss function L with model parameters
238	hθ. The step size α is chosen to not be too small so that an
239	effective perturbation can be quickly found, while not too
240	large such that effective perturbations are not omitted. The
241	perturbation to input x is then projected (Πx+S) to be within
242	the perturbation budget S. The perturbation budget exists
243	such that perturbations are not obvious to detection (e.g., a
244	heavily perturbed image may be noticed by the human eye).
245	This budget is most often a l2 or l∞ norm-ball.
246	In this paper, we examine white-box attacks that are char-
247	acterized by the attacker having full access to the internal
248	architecture and parameters of the victim model, which is
249	reasonable in a federated learning setting where many partic-
250	ipating clients own a copy of the global model. This knowl-
251	edge allows the attacker to craft evasion attacks with better
252	precision, making them generally more effective. In con-
253	trast, black-box attacks operate under the assumption that
254	the attacker has limited or no access to the victim model’s
255	internal structure, relying on input-output interactions or a
256	substitute model to generate adversarial examples. Further-
257	more, all evasion attacks examined are untargeted attacks
258	that aim to have inputs classified as any incorrect output,
259	rather than a specific desired label in a targeted attack. Prior
260	research indicates that patterns of adversarial robustness in
261	federated learning systems tend to be correlated amongst
262	targeted and untargeted attacks (Kim et al., 2023).
263	3.2. The Adversarial Training Process
264	Defending against evasion attacks can be represented as a
265	saddle point problem. In adversarial training, the primary
266	objective is to train a model that minimizes the empirical risk
267	associated with a classification task, even in the face of the
268	adversary’s introduction of input perturbations (e.g., through
269	PGD as demonstrated in Equation 1) that maximize the
270	loss at each data point (Madry et al., 2017). The objective
271	function of adversarial training is as follows:
272	min
273	θ
274	E(x,y)∼ ˜D
275	
276	max
277	δ∈S
278	L ˜D(hθ, x+ δ, y)
279	
280	(2)
281	The perturbation δ added to the data ˜D is bounded within a
282	budget S. In words, we desire to find model parameters hθ
283	that minimize the expected maximum loss when the input
284	x is perturbed by δ. During FAT, each client incorporates
285	adversarial examples into its training data set using gradi-
286	ents from its individual model (Zizzo et al., 2020). These
287	gradients are subsequently aggregated centrally following
288	standard federated averaging (FedAvg) (Li et al., 2021).
289	The effects of FAT are examined and compared to FedAvg
290	in Table 1. FAT enhances the robustness of models (i.e., clas-
291	sification rate against evasion attacks, denoted as Adv. Acc.)
292	compared to FedAvg. The data sets used and experimental
293	setups are described in the appendix. Each client performs
294	measurements on the global model with their local test data.
295	Standard deviation values across client measurements are
296	in parentheses for tables and represented as shaded areas
297	in graphs. Experiments on CIFAR-10 are extensively per-
298	formed in the main body of this paper, while the other data
299	sets are analyzed in the appendix.

317	FAT 0.809 (0.08) 0.516 (0.10)
318	ARU 0.822 (0.07) 0.001 (0.01)
319	FakeNewsNet Test Acc. Adv. Acc.
320	FedAvg 0.782 (0.05) 0.050 (0.02)
321	FAT 0.728 (0.05) 0.380 (0.06)
322	ARU 0.786 (0.06) 0.056 (0.02)
323	4. Threat Model: Adversarial Robustness
324	Unhardening
325	The train-time ARU adversaries aim to undermine FAT such
326	that the trained model becomes much less robust against eva-
327	sion attacks. In essence, the adversaries are strategically
328	embedding a significant backdoor into the model, effec-
329	tively compromising its resilience against all forms of
330	gradient-based adversarial evasion attacks. The objective
331	for such adversarial clients is:
332	max
333	θ
334	E(x,y)∼ ˜D [L(hθ, x+ f (hθ, x, y), y)] (3)
335	where f (hθ, x, y) = argmax
336	δ∈[S]
337	L(hθ, x+ δ, y) (4)
338	s.t. E(x,y)∼ ˜D [L(hθ, x, y)] ≤ H (5)
339	Here, the ARU clients jointly aim to alter the global weight
340	parameters θ such that the loss of the global model is
341	maximized against inputs with adversarial perturbations
342	(f (hθ, x, y), Eq. 4). The ARU clients further desire that test
343	loss against benign inputs remains below a certain threshold
344	(H) for the stealthy implant of the backdoor (Eq. 5).
345	Since ARU clients are typically a minority of all federated
346	clients, they cannot simply solve for a model satisfying
347	Equations 3–5 and use this non-robust model as their lo-
348	cal models. Instead, they must take steps to ensure that
349	their updates to the aggregation server that are based on
350	this non-robust model “dominate” updates from the other,
351	benign clients. Below, we first describe the model replace-
352	ment method used by the ARU adversaries and discuss the
353	subsequent adjustments made to enhance its practicality.
354	4.1. The Model Replacement Method
355	A model replacement attack is performed by replacing a
356	legitimate global model G with a backdoor model (R) by
357	directly manipulating model parameters. In the ARU case,
358	the attack is done by one or more clients. As seen in Equa-
359	tion 6, a single client j performs the attack by uploading
360	updates U t+1
361	j during aggregation, while 1/γi indicates the
362	weighted contribution of the client i during aggregation:
363	R = Gt +
364	mX
365	i=1
366	1
367	γi
368	(U t+1
369	i − Gt) (6)
370	To accomplish this, attacker j strategically ensures that its
371	uploaded model U t+1
372	j survives the aggregation across all
373	clients by boosting the difference between desired model R
374	and global model Gt based on γj, as seen in Equation 7:
375	U t+1
376	j = γjR − (γj − 1)Gt −
377	m−1X
378	i=1
379	1
380	γi
381	(U t+1
382	i − Gt) (7)
383	≈ γj(R − Gt) + Gt (8)
384	When Gt is near convergence, the updates from benign par-
385	ticipants are assumed to be close to zero (U t+1
386	i ≈ Gt), and
387	are omitted in Equation 8. Bagdasaryan et al. (2020) dis-
388	cuss how adversary j can estimate γj during the training
389	procedure, if not known ahead of time. Furthermore, model
390	replacement can be done jointly by multiple ARU clients.
391	For example, if contribution γj is equal amongst all adver-
392	saries, each adversary can alter its boosting rate to γ
393	N , where
394	N is the number of ARU clients.
395	In Table 1, a single adversary performs ARU by model
396	replacement using a non-robust model trained by FedAvg.
397	The ARU attack significantly decreases the robustness of
398	FAT while maintaining a high test accuracy against benign
399	inputs. However, it is unreasonable to assume that the
400	adversary owns the non-robust model used for replace-
401	ment ahead of time, since the global model trained before
402	the model replacement attack is executed will have been
403	hardened by FAT. Thus, we next examine how a small num-
404	ber of collaborating clients can extract a non-robust model
405	from the robust global model during the training process.
406	4.2. Extracting the Non-Robust Model for ARU
407	While clients attempting to perform ARU do not reasonably
408	have access to a non-robust model ahead of time, they do
409	have access to the global model throughout training. We
410	introduce a method for a small group of colluding clients to
411	extract the non-robust model from the robust global model
412	to then perform ARU In Figure 2, (a) multiple ARU clients
413	initially receive a copy of the robust model during FAT. For
414	a set number of rounds, (b) the ARU clients jointly update
415	the FAT model on perturbed data. Here, data points are
416	4
417	
418	----- PAGE 5 -----
419	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
420	Figure 2: Extraction of a non-robust model from the global
421	FAT model. Adversarial clients jointly weaken the FAT
422	model and then perform a model replacement ARU attack.
423	Figure 3: Extracted model performance over rounds. Test
424	accuracy grows with more backdoor clients (solid lines), but
425	Adv. Acc. (dashed lines) is consistently low.
426	perturbed using PGD, and an incorrect label is assigned
427	based on how the model classifies the perturbed data. Such
428	manipulation and training on the ARU clients’ data induces
429	catastrophic forgetting (Kaushik et al., 2021), where the
430	robustness of the global model is forgotten via overfitting on
431	the manipulated data. Finally, (c) the adversaries jointly per-
432	form the model replacement ARU attack with the extracted
433	model. In prior scenarios as presented by Bagdasaryan
434	et al. (2020), the backdoor objective is often orthogonal
435	to the global objective, and both can be optimized by the
436	adversarial clients simultaneously to train the model used
437	for replacement. However, ARU clients face a novel chal-
438	lenge as the global and backdoor objectives directly oppose
439	each other and cannot be optimized simultaneously. The
440	extraction method overcomes the conflict in objective by
441	effectively leveraging the local data and global robust model
442	available to obtain the model for replacement.
443	The performance of ARU with the extracted model is shown
444	in Figure 3. Here, different numbers of attacking clients
445	out of 40 jointly perform the extraction for 10 rounds. The
446	extraction is initially performed with perturbed data (50%
447	of data is perturbed), but only unperturbed data is used after
448	5 rounds to reduce the impact of the perturbed data on the
449	test accuracy. The lower adversarial robustness accuracy
450	indicates the success of the extraction method, as well as the
451	high test accuracy. Figure 3 also shows that the catastrophic
452	forgetting of robustness occurs with few training rounds
453	amongst the relatively small group (e.g., 1 to 5 adversaries).
454	With a limited number of extracting clients, the extraction
455	procedure degrades test accuracy as the extracted model
456	overfits to a small sample of data.
457	5. Robust Aggregation Schemes Against ARU
458	and Backdoor Attacks
459	Robust aggregation schemes, such as trimmed-mean and
460	median methods, are the primary defenses against poisoning
461	and backdoor attacks (Yin et al., 2018b). In general, such
462	defenses aim to discard updates from clients that stray too
463	far from the average update, as adversarial updates tend to
464	show high deviation from benign updates. In this section,
465	we first examine the trade-off between client privacy, robust-
466	ness, and overhead for different robust aggregation schemes,
467	which informs our choice of defenses against which we eval-
468	uate ARU. Afterwards, we then observe the effect of robust
469	aggregation schemes against the ARU attack. We first con-
470	sider a single-shot attack, as described in the Threat Model
471	section above. Informed by our experimental findings, we
472	then propose an iterative variation of ARU and demonstrate
473	that it can defeat common robust aggregation defenses.
474	5.1. Trade-offs of Robust Aggregation
475	There exists an inherent trade-off between robustness, pri-
476	vacy, and overhead of defensive aggregation schemes, and it
477	is only possible to only pursue two of the three desired
478	characteristics during federated learning (Pillutla et al.,
479	2022). Any aggregation scheme that updates the global
480	model as a linear function of client updates (e.g., FedAvg,
481	trimmed-mean) is both privacy preserving and communica-
482	tion efficient, but non-robust. Alternative robust aggregation
483	schemes (Fung et al., 2020; Nguyen et al., 2022; Ozdayi
484	et al., 2021) violate privacy constraints as individual updates
485	from clients must be examined and compared. However, by
486	significantly increasing the per-round communication cost
487	between clients, it is possible to obfuscate individual client
488	updates by mixing updates between a subset of clients.
489	Since the median method has poor privacy guarantees,
490	which are a primary motivator of federated learning in
491	the first place, most of our results in this section focus
492	on evaluating ARU against the more privatetrimmed-mean
493	defense. Recent work, however, has proposed a state-of-
494	the-art mixture method (Ramezani-Kebrya et al., 2022) that
495	rotates among different defense methods in different fed-
496	erated learning iterations, thus allowing for a more flex-
497	ible trade-off between robustness, privacy, and overhead.
498	We moreover show that in Section 6.1, in highly non-i.i.d.
499	settings, robust aggregation methods perform poorly even
500	without the ARU attack, due to their inability to effectively
501	accommodate heterogeneous client updates.
502	5
503	
504	----- PAGE 6 -----
505	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
506	(a) Robustness gradually and continu-
507	ously decreases from the FAT to the Fe-
508	dAvg model when interpolated. Test Acc.
509	in solid lines, Adv. Acc. in dashed lines.
510	(b) The cosine similarity between outputs of
511	each layer between a FedAvg and FAT model.
512	Each layer for both models has the previous
513	output of a FedAvg layer as inputs.
514	(c) The cosine similarity between outputs of
515	each layer between a FedAvg model and FAT
516	model are compared. Differences in layer
517	outputs are accumulated through layers.
518	Figure 4: The FAT and FedAvg model are highly similar. Thus, a gradual insertion of the ARU backdoor across multiple
519	rounds is possible as robustness is continuous with respect to the FAT and FedAvg models. (CIFAR-10)
520	Table 2: Robust aggregation defense against one-shot ARU
521	attacks. Attacks are performed by a single client for a single
522	round. Trimmed-mean cutoff set at tm = 0.05.
523	Dataset Metric Trim-Mean Median
524	CIFAR10 Test Acc. 0.811 (0.07) 0.809 (0.07)
525	Adv. Acc. 0.423 (0.09) 0.416 (0.09)
526	CIFAR100 Test Acc. 0.417 (0.06) 0.419 (0.06)
527	Adv. Acc. 0.099 (0.04) 0.093 (0.03)
528	Celeba Test Acc. 0.796 (0.08) 0.786 (0.08)
529	Adv. Acc. 0.465 (0.11) 0.481 (0.10)
530	FakeNews Test Acc. 0.697 (0.07) 0.670 (0.07)
531	Adv. Acc. 0.369 (0.06) 0.386 (0.05)
532	5.2. Single-Shot ARU Against Robust Aggregation
533	The single-shot ARU attack, where one or more clients at-
534	tempt to replace the robust global model with a non-robust
535	model as in Equation 6, is performed against the trimmed-
536	mean and median robust-aggregation schemes in Table 2.
537	The trimmed-mean method discards tm ∈ [0, 1] propor-
538	tion of largest and smallest values for each weight param-
539	eter (e.g., tm = 0.05 discards 10% of updates), while the
540	coordinate-wise median method selects the median value
541	for each individual parameter.
542	The comparison of Table 1 and Table 2 indicate that the
543	trimmed-mean method ( tm = 0 .05) mitigates the effect
544	of the single-shot ARU attack significantly across all data
545	sets, maintaining similar to slightly lower robustness mea-
546	surements to FAT models. The median defense displays
547	high resilience towards the single-shot ARU attack as well.
548	However, as discussed in the prior subsection and by Pillutla
549	et al. (2022), the coordinate-wise median method violates
550	the privacy constraints of federated learning. Furthermore,
551	ARU clients are not restricted to one-shot attacks, and the
552	motivation and implementation for a multi-round iterative
553	ARU attack is discussed in the next subsection.
554	5.3. Performance of Iterative ARU Against Robust
555	Aggregation Schemes
556	The model replacement method used by the single-shot
557	ARU demonstrated in Equations 6 – 8, calculates the back-
558	door updates assuming that the global model is near con-
559	vergence and updates from non-ARU clients are near zero.
560	However, when ARU clients perform an iterative attack
561	across rounds, the assumption of near convergence is no
562	longer valid as benign clients simultaneously aim to increase
563	the robustness of the global model. For the iterative ARU
564	attack to succeed, the adversarial clients must successfully
565	estimate and counteract the uploads of the benign clients.
566	In Figure 4a, we analyze the robustness of interpolations be-
567	tween a robust FAT model and a non-robust FedAvg model
568	at different points. The results suggest that iterative ARU
569	can progressively erode robustness over multiple rounds as
570	the FAT model gradually shifts toward the FedAvg model.
571	Figure 4b measures the cosine similarity of layer outputs
572	between FAT and FedAvg models for the same input. Most
573	layers exhibit high similarity (above 0.95), suggesting that
574	the two models produce nearly identical representations.
575	This resemblance may further incentivize an iterative ARU
576	attack, as attackers benefit when the backdoored model
577	closely mirrors the global model. Robustness appears to be
578	concentrated in layers with low similarity, specifically fea-
579	tures 1 and 17 (both convolutional layers). In Figure 4c, we
580	measure cosine similarity between layer outputs with only
581	the first layer receiving identical input. The results indicate
582	that small differences in early layers propagate, leading to
583	significant discrepancies for adversarial inputs.
584	Iterative ARU is tested against no robust aggregation scheme
585	in Figure 5a. The attack greatly diminishes robustness of
586	the global model. A new scaling variable β is introduced
587	6
588	
589	----- PAGE 7 -----
590	Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
591	(a) Performance of iterative ARU against no
592	defense.
593	(b) Performance of iterative ARU against
594	trimmed-mean defense.
595	(c) Performance of iterative ARU against
596	median defense.
597	Figure 5: Performance of iterative ARU against various robust aggregation schemes. Iterative ARU handily bypasses the
598	trimmed-mean defense that fulfills both privacy and communication overhead constraints. In all cases, test accuracy for
599	benign input remains high (above 0.79). (CIFAR-10)
600	to flexibly scale the iterative model replacement process
601	(i.e., U t+1
602	j ≈ γjβ(R − Gt) + Gt, based on Eq. 7). As β
603	is decreased, the replacement of the robust global model
604	becomes more gradual. Iterative ARU is tested against
605	the trimmed-mean defense in Figure 5b. ARU quickly over-
606	comes the defense given either a lower trimmed-mean cutoff
607	parameter tm = 0.05, or a higher number of ARU clients
608	(10 clients compared to 5). Against a more aggressive cutoff
609	policy (e.g., tm = 0.2), lowering the parameter β aids in
610	bypassing the defense (for CIFAR-100 in Figure 12), as ad-
611	versarial updates are closer to the expected global updates.
612	In all circumstances, iterative ARU is implemented against
613	a model thus far trained via FAT that has had no robust
614	aggregation performed up until the point where the ARU
615	attack is started.
616	Iterative ARU is tested against the median aggregation
617	scheme in Figure 5c. Here, each client begins with β = 0.1
618	and increases the parameter by 0.1 until it reaches 1. In
619	general, the median defense is robust and can only be by-
620	passed if the number of adversarial clients is over half of
621	the total number of federated clients (i.e., greater than 20
622	of 40 clients). However, possibly due to the proximity of
623	the ARU update to the average global update induced by
624	the constraint of Equation 5, ARU demonstrates a slight
625	reduction in robustness even with less than half of clients
626	as adversaries. Moreover, as discussed above it may raise
627	privacy concerns.
628	6. Extended Evaluation: ARU in Realistic
629	Systems
630	We evaluate ARU beyond the results of previous sections.
631	We first examine the effects of limited system resources and
632	of different data distributions across clients. Afterwards,
633	we compare ARU to other poisoning attacks and demon-
634	strate a potential defense against ARU that reduces privacy
635	violations compared to the median defense.
636	6.1. Distributed System Resources and Non-i.i.d. Data
637	As discussed by Bagdasaryan et al. (2020) and Pillutla
638	et al. (2022), non-i.i.d. data distributions amongst federated
639	clients reduce the efficacy of robust aggregation schemes by
640	making even benign model updates more heterogeneous.
641	The non-i.i.d. data challenge is further exacerbated by
642	the implementation of FAT amongst resource constrained
643	clients, as the uneven generation of adversarial examples
644	amongst the federated client training set further differenti-
645	ates data, and thus model update, characteristics (Kim et al.,
646	2023; Hong et al., 2021).
647	The effects of non-i.i.d. data against robust aggregation
648	schemes are examined in Figure 6a. Data is split in a more
649	i.i.d. manner by setting the proportion of adversarial exam-
650	ples equivalent across all clients (40% of each client training
651	set). The non-i.i.d. split is induced by limiting the number
652	of clients (15 of 40) that can generate adversarial training
653	examples, while on average maintaining the total number
654	of adversarial training points in the system. While all of
655	our experiments use non-i.i.d. data distributions (see the
656	appendix for exact settings), this result shows that some
657	distributions, especially in relation to available resources
658	for adversarial training, can cause even the median defense
659	to lose effectiveness. For both trimmed-mean and median
660	methods, the overall robustness of a FAT model is signif-
661	icantly reduced in the non-i.i.d. compared to i.i.d. case
662	(14% and 20% reductions, respectively). Thus, although ro-
663	bust aggregation schemes display some level of robustness
664	against the ARU attack, they may still reduce the robustness
665	of a model trained by FAT in realistic settings.
666	7

===== PAGE 1 =====
arXiv:1010.1646v2  [cond-mat.stat-mech]  9 Dec 2010
Thresholds for epidemic spreading in networks
Claudio Castellano 1 and Romualdo Pastor-Satorras 2
1Istituto dei Sistemi Complessi (CNR-ISC), UOS Sapienza and Dip. di Fisica,
“Sapienza” Universit` a di Roma, P.le A. Moro 2, I-00185 Roma , Italy
2Departament de F ´ ısica i Enginyeria Nuclear, Universitat Polit` ecnica de Catalunya, Campus Nord B4, 08034 Barcelona, Spain
We study the threshold of epidemic models in quenched networ ks with degree distribution given
by a power-law. For the susceptible-infected-susceptible model the activity threshold λ c vanishes in
the large size limit on any network whose maximum degree kmax diverges with the system size, at
odds with heterogeneous mean-ﬁeld (HMF) theory. The vanish ing of the threshold has nothing to
do with the scale-free nature of the network but stems instea d from the largest hub in the system
being active for any spreading rate λ > 1/ √ kmax and playing the role of a self-sustained source that
spreads the infection to the rest of the system. The suscepti ble-infected-removed model displays
instead agreement with HMF theory and a ﬁnite threshold for s cale-rich networks. We conjecture
that on quenched scale-rich networks the threshold of gener ic epidemic models is vanishing or ﬁnite
depending on the presence or absence of a steady state.
PACS numbers: 89.75.Hc, 05.70.Ln, 87.23.Ge, 89.75.Da
The heterogeneous pattern of a network can have dra-
matic eﬀects on the behavior of dynamical processes run-
ning on top of it [1], in particular when the distribution
of the number k of contacts (the degree of an element or
vertex) exhibits long tails, as expressed by a power-law
degree probability with the asymptotic form P (k) ∼ k−γ
[2]. An example that has attracted a great interest due
to its practical real-world implications is the modeling of
epidemic spreading on contact networks [3]. The simplest
of these models is the SIS model [4], in which each vertex
(individual) can be in one of two states, either suscepti-
ble, or infected. Susceptibles become infected by contact
with infected individuals, with a rate proportional to the
number of infected contacts times a given spreading rate
λ. Infected individuals on the other hand become healthy
again with a rate that can be set arbitrarily equal to
unity. The model allows thus individuals to contract the
infection time and again, leading, in the inﬁnite network
size limit, to a sustained infected steady state for val-
ues of λ larger than an epidemic threshold λ c. In the
SIR model [4], on the other hand, infected individuals
recover (or die) and cannot change further their state.
No steady state is now allowed, but a threshold still ex-
ists above which the total number of infected individuals,
starting from a very small infected seed, reaches a ﬁnite
fraction of the network. The analysis of these and other
models [1], performed via a mean-ﬁeld theory modiﬁed
to take into account the heterogeneity of the network
substrate [5, 6], led to the far-reaching conclusion that
topological ﬂuctuations, as measured by the second mo-
ment of the degree distribution ⟨k2⟩, can have profound
eﬀects in many types of dynamics [1, 6]. Thus, for ex-
ample, in the SIS model, the threshold takes the values,
at the mean-ﬁeld level λ c = ⟨k⟩/ ⟨k2⟩. For a long-tailed
degree distribution with power law form, the second mo-
ment diverges for γ ≤ 3, and one obtains the remarkable
result of a vanishing epidemic threshold in the thermo-
dynamic limit. These results have led to the widespread
belief in the distinction between scale-free networks with
γ ≤ 3, where topology is highly relevant, and scale-rich
networks with γ > 3, where dynamical processes exhibit
an essentially homogeneous mean-ﬁeld behavior.
In this Letter, building on some results previously re-
ported, we present evidence that this belief is not correct
for the SIS model on quenched networks (i.e. networks
whose adjacency matrix is ﬁxed in time) and that the
scale-free nature of the contact pattern has no crucial
eﬀect on the value of the epidemic threshold. We inves-
tigate the physical origin of this result, its validity for
generic network structures and its consequences. On the
other hand we show that for the SIR model the picture
is diﬀerent, a zero threshold occurring only in scale-free
quenched networks.
While heterogeneous mean-ﬁeld (HMF) theory is ex-
act on annealed networks (i.e. networks whose adjacency
matrix in ﬁxed only in average [6]), results beyond HMF
theory for the SIS process on quenched networks (QN)
have appeared in diﬀerent contexts and with various lev-
els of rigor. Already in 2003, Wang et al. [7] argued that
the epidemic threshold on an arbitrary undirected graph
is set by the largest eigenvalue Λ N of the adjacency ma-
trix,
λ c = Λ −1

when it is complemented with the results of Chung et

distributed according to a power-law, obtaining

[12]. For γ > 3 the ratio of the moments is ﬁnite and
it is clear that the largest eigenvalue is governed by kc.

in that range ⟨k2⟩/ ⟨k⟩ ∼ k3−γ

see also [13]. Since kc grows as a function of N for any
γ, the consequence of Eq. (3) is remarkable: In any
uncorrelated quenched random network with power-law
distributed connectivities, the epidemic threshold for SI S
goes to zero as the system size goes to inﬁnity . This has
nothing to do with the scale-free nature of the degree
distribution: It is always true as long as the cut-oﬀ kc
diverges. Remarkably the threshold goes to zero also for
Erd¨ os-R´ enyi graphs (although logarithmically slow), for
which a formula similar to Eq. (2) exists [14]. Diﬀer-
ent approaches [13, 15, 16], have also pointed out that
in the thermodynamic limit, the system is active for any
λ > 0. These results, however, have gone largely unno-
ticed within the statistical physics community.
A ﬁrst issue raised by Eq. (3) concerns the fact that,
as any critical point, the epidemic threshold is well de-
ﬁned only in the thermodynamic limit. In a ﬁnite system,
the dynamics is always doomed to fall into the healthy,
absorbing state, even far above the threshold, due to
stochastic ﬂuctuations. The threshold for a ﬁnite net-
work of size N must therefore be intended as the value
separating the regime λ < λ c for which the epidemics
decays exponentially fast (so that the expected survival
time is of the order τ ∼ ln(N )) from the regime λ > λ c
where the survival time grows exponentially with N to
some power, τ ∼ eN α
, with α > 0.
To investigate the validity of these results we have
performed numerical simulations of the SIS model on
quenched scale-rich networks with γ = 4. 5 and minimum
degree kmin = 3, built using the uncorrelated conﬁgu-
ration model [17]. In order to compare results with the
predictions in Eq. (3) one must take into account that the
actual maximum degree kmax in each network realization
is a random variable, with average value ⟨kmax⟩ = kc. In
particular, in the case γ > 3, one can see [18] that both
the mean and the standard deviation of kmax scale as
kc ∼ N 1/ (γ −1), implying that kmax always shows large
ﬂuctuations for diﬀerent realizations of the degree se-
quence. Therefore, we ﬁrst consider networks in which
kmax has a ﬁxed value, equal to the mean kc numerically

as a function of N for diﬀerent values of λ [19]. Should
the transition occur at a ﬁxed value of λ, ρs would go
to a constant for λ > λ c, decay exponentially for λ < λ c
and as a power-law exactly at the transition. A com-
pletely diﬀerent behavior is observed: for all values of λ,
the curves are bent upward, indicating that the system
is active for any λ. This excludes the presence of a ﬁnite
threshold for diverging N . While Eq. (1) holds for SIS
on any graph, Eq. (2) was instead obtained for a speciﬁc
network model (intrinsically correlated for γ < 3 and un-
correlated for γ > 3 [12]). For generic topologies, it is
simple to show [11] that √
kc is a lower bound for the
largest eigenvalue of the adjacency matrix. This allows
to conclude that, unless the degree distribution is strictly
bounded from above, the threshold for SIS on any graph
vanishes in the thermodynamic limit.
How generic are these results? Prakash et al. [9]
have recently argued that Eq. (1) is valid for all epi-
demic processes, regardless of their particular micro-
scopic details. To check this claim, we consider the SIR
model. At the HMF level, the threshold takes the value
λ SIR
c = ⟨k⟩/ [⟨k2⟩ − ⟨k⟩] [20, 21] and is therefore ﬁnite
for scale-rich networks with γ > 3. From the analysis
in Eq. (3), on the other hand, it should be vanishingly
small in the large network limit, according to Ref. [9].
We have checked this possibility by performing numerical
simulations of the SIR model on networks with γ = 4 . 5
and diﬀerent values of N , with ﬁxed kmax = ⟨kmax⟩. In
this case, the HMF estimated threshold takes the value
λ SIR

predictions from Eq. (3) are λ SIR

0. 1118 for the diﬀerent network sizes considered. In Fig. 2
we report the ﬁnal density of infected individuals R as a
function of the spreading rate λ, starting from a single
randomly chosen infected node. The prediction of HMF

Eq. (3), contrary to the generic claim made in Ref. [9]:
The threshold remains ﬁnite in the large N limit.
To understand the diﬀerent behavior of the two mod-
els we look at the origin of the incorrect HMF prediction
for SIS in QN. From a mathematical point of view, the
HMF approach is equivalent [6] to replacing the QN with
given adjacency matrix aij by an annealed network with
an averaged adjacency matrix, ¯aij [6, 22]. In the uncor-
related case this matrix reduces to ¯ aij = kikj/ [N ⟨k⟩],
which has a unique nonzero eigenvalue ¯Λ N = ⟨k2⟩/ ⟨k⟩.
Hence the annealed network approximation destroys the
detailed structure of the eigenvalue spectrum of QN and
preserves the correct largest eigenvalue only for γ < 5/ 2.
This basic feature, and not (as suggested in Ref. [16])
the disregard of dynamical correlations, is at root of the
inaccuracy of the HMF approach. A more physical in-
sight comes from the analysis of a star graph with one
center connected to kmax leaves of degree 1. In this
case the largest eigenvalue of the adjacency matrix is
Λ N = √
kmax which implies λ c = 1 / √kmax. The same
result can be easily recovered by writing the rate equa-
tions for the probability ρmax (ρ1) for the center (leaves)
to be active, namely ˙ρmax = −ρmax + (1− ρmax)ρ1λk max
and ˙ρ1 = −ρ1 +(1 −ρ1)ρmaxλ. Imposing the steady state
condition one ﬁnds

and hence the threshold condition above. The mes-
sage of Eq. (4) for a generic quenched random graph is
strong: Independently from all the rest of the system,
for λ > 1/ √
kmax the subgraph composed by the node
with degree kmax and its neighbors is in the active state.
This core of activity provides a self-sustained source of
infection that, since in the full graph the neighbors of
the hub are not leaves, can transfer the activity to their
other neighbors and spread in this way the epidemics to
a ﬁnite fraction of vertices. This is conﬁrmed by Fig. 3,

with the same kmax[25]. For kmax < 1/λ 2 the values of
Ns in the full network and in the star graph are com-
parable: both systems are subcritical and the subgraph
centered around the node with degree kmax is where ac-
tivity lingers before disappearing. For kmax > 1/λ 2 the
star graph becomes active and Ns becomes asymptoti-
cally proportional to kmax. In the full network instead,
the asymptotic behavior is Ns ∼ kγ −1

that the active state is endemic: the hub spreads the ac-
tivity to a ﬁnite fraction of the whole system. Reaching
the the fully endemic state requires larger systems for
small λ, but nothing changes qualitatively for any λ > 0.
Understanding the behavior of SIS allows also to un-
ravel why things go diﬀerently for SIR. In the former case,
the possibility for hubs to be reinfected multiple times,
which allows the presence of a steady state, boosts their
impact on the dynamics. In the case of SIR, on the other
hand, high-degree vertices can only be infected once and
this strongly limits their role in the dynamics. Based on
this observation, it is natural to conjecture that epidemic
models allowing a steady state, such as SIS, will lead to a
null threshold in any inﬁnite QN, while all models with-
out a steady-state will conform with HMF theory, with
a ﬁnite threshold on scale-rich topologies.
The strong eﬀect of the hub in the dynamics raises fur-
ther issues on the SIS model. While ﬁxing the value of
kmax to its ensemble average leads to results consistent
with the presence of a non-zero threshold in ﬁnite sys-
tems, as implied by Eq. (3), if this constraint is relaxed,
kmax has large sample to sample ﬂuctuations leading to
nontrivial consequences. In Fig. 4 we explore the eﬀect
of this variability by comparing simulations performed
at ﬁxed λ and N , and diﬀerent values of kmax. The
growth of the activity density for increasing kmax in-
dicates that the relation between the threshold (or the
largest eigenvalue) and cut-oﬀ kc, Eq. (3), can in fact

be reﬁned, and be expressed in terms of the actual max-
imum degree, λ c = 1 / √
kmax [18]. However, the large
variations of kmax among diﬀerent realizations of the
network with the same γ and N do not wash away as
N diverges and severely hinder the determination of the
threshold in simulations with unrestricted kmax. As men-
tioned before, for γ > 3 the standard deviation of kmax
increases as the average value ⟨kmax⟩ ∼ N 1/ (γ −1) [18],
and there is always a large sample to sample variabil-
ity. Hence an unrestricted sampling at ﬁxed λ unwar-
rantedly averages networks with diﬀerent thresholds and
eﬀective time scales, some subcritical and some supercrit-
ical, making impossible even to determine the presence
of a well-deﬁned steady state. This fact is exempliﬁed in
Fig. 4, where we plot for comparison the activity density
obtained averaging over networks with a freely varying
kmax. For γ < 3 the situation depends on the way the
network is generated, and in particular on the way the
upper bound of the degree distribution M = N 1/ω grows
[22]. If ω = 2 (uncorrelated conﬁguration model [17]) or
larger, the quantity ⟨k2⟩/ ⟨k⟩ becomes sharply peaked as
N grows [22]. If instead ω = 1, as in the normal con-
ﬁguration model [23], the ratio ⟨k2⟩/ ⟨k⟩ (and hence the
threshold) wildly changes from realization to realization,
with relative ﬂuctuations diverging as N 2(3−γ )(γ −2)/ (γ −1)
[22]. Notice that in the intermediate region 5 / 2 < γ < 3,
the average value of √
kmax is larger than ⟨k2⟩/ ⟨k⟩ but,
since ﬂuctuations of the latter diverge, for some network
realizations the actual threshold λ c is much smaller than
the value predicted by Eq. (3). We conclude that, unless
γ < 3 and ω ≥ 2, no average epidemic threshold can
be properly deﬁned from a numerical point of view for
networks with unrestricted kmax.
In summary, we have studied how the threshold for
models of epidemic spreading on quenched scale-rich net-
works behaves as their size grows. The threshold for SIS
model always vanishes in the thermodynamic limit, due
to the role of hubs. This bears no relationship, at odds
with the predictions of HMF theory, with the divergence
of the second moment of the degree distribution, which
is ﬁnite. For the SIR model instead the threshold van-
ishes only for scale-free topologies (either quenched or
annealed), in agreement with HMF theory. We conjec-
ture that these diﬀerent types of behavior are generic
for systems possessing (or not) a steady state. While
the result of a vanishing threshold for SIS is exact on
quenched networks, it is however of limited interest from
an epidemiological perspective. The interaction patterns
over which real diseases spread generally vary over short
time scales [24], and are therefore better described by
annealed topologies [6], for which HMF theory works by
deﬁnition, and the threshold is ﬁnite for γ > 3. From a
statistical physics point of view, instead, our results open
a promising path towards a better understanding of the
scope and limits of HMF theory as a theoretical tool to
analyze dynamics on heterogeneous networks.

content.content:
===== PAGE 1 =====
arXiv:1402.1731v1  [math.DS]  7 Feb 2014
Exact Markovian SIR and SIS epidemics on networks and an uppe r
bound for the epidemic threshold
P. Van Mieghem ∗
Delft University of Technology
Abstract
Exploiting the power of the expectation operator and indicator (or Bernoulli) random variables,
we present the exact governing equations for both the SIR and SI S epidemic models on networks.
Although SIR and SIS are basic epidemic models, deductions from the ir exact stochastic equations
without making approximations (such as the common mean-ﬁeld approximatio n) are scarce. An
exact analytic solution of the governing equations is highly unlikely to b e found (for any network)
due to the appearing pair (and higher order) correlations. Nevert heless, the maximum average
fraction yI of infected nodes in both SIS and SIR can be written as a quadratic f orm of the graph’s
Laplacian. Only for regular graphs, the expression for the maximum of yI can be simplied to exhibit
the explicit dependence on the spectral radius. From our new Lapla cian expression, we deduce a
general upper bound for the epidemic SIS threshold in any graph.
1 Introduction
Although the Susceptible-Infected-Removed (SIR) and the S usceptible-Infected-Susceptible (SIS) model
are basic corner-stones in epidemics (see e.g. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]), exact stochastic equations for SIR have,
to the best of our knowledge, not been published yet for an arb itrary network, while for SIS, we refer to
[7] and [8, 9]. A network is described by an adjacency matrix A, with degree vector D = (d1, d 2, . . . , d N )
where dk is the degree of node k. For simplicity, we assume an undirected network ( A = AT ) that
does not change over time. In addition to the many applicatio ns ranging from cyber security over
information diﬀusion [10] to biological diseases [1, 5], we e xplore these (relatively) simple epidemic
processes on graphs to understand the inﬂuence of the topolo gy of complex networks [11] on properties
of a dynamic process. First, we describe both the SIS and SIR m odel on any network in a stochastic,
Markovian setting and refer for non-Markovian SIS epidemic s to [12, 13].
In a SIS epidemic process, the viral state of a node i at time t is speciﬁed by a Bernoulli random
variable Xi (t) ∈ {S, I }: Xi (t) = S for a healthy, but susceptible node and Xi (t) = I for an infected
node. A node i at time t can be in one of the two states: infected, with probability vi(t) = Pr[ Xi(t) = I]
or healthy, with probability 1 −vi(t), but susceptible to the infection. We assume that the curin g process
per node i is a Poisson process with rate δ and that the infection rate per link is a Poisson process
∗ Faculty of Electrical Engineering, Mathematics and Comput er Science, P.O Box 5031, 2600 GA Delft, The Nether-
lands; email : P.F.A.VanMieghem@tudelft.nl
1
===== PAGE 2 =====
with rate β . Obviously, only when a node is infected, it can infect its di rect neighbors, that are still
healthy. Both the curing and infection Poisson process are i ndependent. The eﬀective infection rate
is deﬁned by τ = β
δ . This is the general continuous-time description of the sim plest type of a SIS
epidemic process on a network.
In the SIR model, a node can be in one of the three states. When a node j is healthy, but
susceptible to the virus, at time t, his state Yj = S. A node j can be infected, Yj = I, by its direct
neighbors that are infected. The infection is modelled by a P oisson process with rate β . Finally, an
infected node j can be cured, after which it is removed from the infection pro cess, Yj = R. The curing
is modelled by a Poisson process with rate δ. All Poisson processes are independent. This formulation
describes a continuous-time SIR process on a graph.
There exist other formulations of the SIR process. For examp le, the discrete-time counter part, in
which a node is removed at the end of each time-slot and infect ed neighbors can infect a susceptible
node with probability p, is termed a Reed-Frost process and is related to bond percol ation [14]. Draief
and Massouli´ e [15] show that a Reed-Frost process is relate d to the growth of an Erd˝ os-R´ enyi graph.
The SIR process is also related to a Markov discovery process on a graph (see [16, p. 349-351]).
Newman [14] has presented a generating function approach fo r SIR, though implicitly assuming a

content.content:
===== PAGE 1 =====
arXiv:1501.02472v2  [cs.SI]  31 Jul 2015
1
Epidemic Threshold of an SIS Model in Dynamic
Switching Networks
Mohammad Reza Sanatkar, Warren N. White, Balasubramaniam N atarajan, Caterina M. Scoglio,
and Karen A. Garrett
Abstract—In this paper, we analyze dynamic switching net-
works, wherein the networks switch arbitrarily among a set
of topologies. For this class of dynamic networks, we derive
an epidemic threshold, considering the SIS epidemic model.
First, an epidemic probabilistic model is developed assumi ng
independence between states of nodes. We identify the condi tions
under which the epidemic dies out by linearizing the underly ing
dynamical system and analyzing its asymptotic stability ar ound
the origin. The concept of joint spectral radius is then used
to derive the epidemic threshold, which is later validated u sing
several networks (Watts-Strogatz, Barabasi-Albert, MIT r eality
mining graphs, Regular, and Gilbert). A simpliﬁed version o f
the epidemic threshold is proposed for undirected networks .
Moreover, in the case of static networks, the derived epidem ic
threshold is shown to match conventional analytical results. Then,
analytical results for the epidemic threshold of dynamic networks
are proved to be applicable to periodic networks. For dynami c
regular networks, we demonstrate that the epidemic thresho ld is
identical to the epidemic threshold for static regular netw orks.
An upper bound for the epidemic spread probability in dynami c
Gilbert networks is also derived and veriﬁed using simulati on.
Index Terms —Dynamic Networks, Epidemic Threshold, Dy-
namical System.
I. I NTRODUCTION
Epidemics typically start with some initial infected nodes .
There is a probability that healthy neighboring nodes, clos e to
the infected nodes, become infected. With time and, in some
cases, with external intervention, infected nodes recover and
revert to a healthy state. The study of epidemic dispersal on
networks aims at understanding how epidemics evolve and
spread in networks. When an infection enters a network, it
is very useful to be able to determine whether it will die
out or become a massive out break. The epidemic threshold
addresses this question, taking into account both the netwo rk
topology and the epidemic strength. The spread of epidemics
in static networks has been studied extensively [1]–[8]. Mo re
recently, epidemic dispersal in dynamic networks has garnered
attention [9]–[20]. In a dynamic network, links between nod es
are functions of time. This dynamic nature presents a more

where Pt = [p1(t)p2(t)...pn(t)]T is the system state, At is the

matrix. We deﬁne Mt as Mt = [(1 − δ)I + βAt] the system

B. Epidemic Threshold
As mentioned earlier, stability of the origin in the epidemi c
dynamical system determines under what conditions epidemics
die out. If the origin is an asymptotically stable equilibri um
point, the system state reaches the origin and infection pro ba-
bilities of all nodes become zero and remain zero. Fig. 1 de-
picts trajectories of state evolution of an epidemic networ k for
===== PAGE 4 =====
4
different initial infection probabilities. The network ha s three
nodes and its adjacency matrix is static. Node 1 is connected to
nodes 2 and 3, but no link exists between nodes 2 and 3. Each
axis of the 3D plot in Fig. 1 represents the infection probability
of one of the three nodes over time. Dots with P0 label in
state space represent initial infection probabilities, an d the
dot with Peq represents the equilibrium point of the system.
The probability constants used are δ = 0 .2 and β = 0 .1.
We see that all the trajectories reach the origin regardless
of their initial states. Fig. 1 shows that, for these probabi lity
constants, the origin is asymptotically stable and epidemi cs
die out. In the case of spreading epidemics, the origin is not
a stable equilibrium point and the state variables converge
on a non-zero equilibrium point and remain at that point.
This equilibrium point determines the ﬁnal fraction of infe cted
nodes. Fig. 2 shows trajectories of state evolution of the sa me
network depicted in Fig. 1. The initial states are identical to
initial states in Fig. 1. However, the value of β is different.
In Fig. 2, β = 0 .6, and the epidemic spreads out. For all
different infection probability vectors, the system state reaches
the equilibrium point Peq = [0.76791, 0.69731, 0.69731] with
elements that represent, respectively, infection probabi lity of
nodes 1, 2, and 3 in steady state.
