content.text:
I now have comprehensive content on all 10 papers. I have all the key methodology math, models/datasets, results, code links, and scaling coefficients. Let me compile the structured markdown notes.

# Literature Review Notes: Merging / Federated-Learning / Safety-Preservation Pillar

**Project:** Epidemiological Modeling of Misalignment Propagation in Distributed LLM Fine-Tuning
**Pillar focus:** the merge/aggregation mechanisms by which a "misalignment task vector" can transmit between models, and whether/at-what-cost safety can be restored.

---

## 1. Editing Models with Task Arithmetic (Ilharco et al., 2023) ⭐

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Editing Models with Task Arithmetic* — Gabriel Ilharco, Marco Tulio Ribeiro, Mitchell Wortsman, Suchin Gururangan, Ludwig Schmidt, Hannaneh Hajishirzi, Ali Farhadi. ICLR 2023 (arXiv 2212.04089).
- **Key Contribution:** Introduces the **task vector** primitive — fine-tuned weights minus pre-trained weights — and shows behaviors can be **added** (multi-task) or **negated** (forgetting/unlearning) by element-wise weight arithmetic, with no extra inference cost and no training data.
- **Methodology (the core merge math):**
  - Task vector: τ_t = θ_ft^t − θ_pre (element-wise difference).
  - Apply to any same-architecture model: **θ_new = θ + λτ**, where λ is a scaling coefficient tuned on held-out validation. λ = 1 reproduces the fine-tuned model exactly.
  - Addition (multi-task): τ_new = Σ_i τ_i, then θ_new = θ + λ Σ_i τ_i.
  - Negation (forgetting): τ_new = −τ, i.e. θ_new = θ − λτ. This **moves away from the fine-tuned behavior**.
  - Analogy: τ_new = τ_C + (τ_B − τ_A).
  - Key empirical property: **task vectors from different tasks are near-orthogonal** (cosine ≈ 0.01–0.06), which is why addition causes minimal interference.
- **Models/datasets:** CLIP ViT-B/32, ViT-B/16, ViT-L/14; GPT-2 (toxicity); T5-small/base/large. Datasets: 8 image tasks (Cars, DTD, EuroSAT, GTSRB, MNIST, RESISC45, SUN397, SVHN), ImageNet (control), Civil Comments (toxicity), GLUE, Yelp/Amazon sentiment.
- **Key results:** Negating a toxicity task vector reduced toxic generations 6× (4.8%→0.8%) with negligible perplexity change. Adding 2 task vectors retains 98.9% normalized accuracy; adding all 8 reaches 91.2%. Negating reduces target accuracy by up to 45.8 pts with little control-task loss.
- **Code:** https://github.com/mlfoundations/task_vectors
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** **This is the canonical "infection mechanism."** A "misalignment task vector" τ_harm = θ_misaligned − θ_pre can be **added** to a clean model (θ_clean + λτ_harm) to transmit the harmful behavior, and because task vectors are near-orthogonal, the harmful direction transfers with minimal disruption to other capabilities — exactly the property a contagion needs (high transmissibility, low collateral). The **cure is symmetric**: subtract the vector (θ − λ·τ_harm), i.e. "immunization" is literally negation with the same λ. λ is the single tunable "transmission/dose coefficient."

---

## 2. Model Soups (Wortsman et al., 2022)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Model soups: averaging weights of multiple fine-tuned models improves accuracy without increasing inference time* — Wortsman, Ilharco, Gadre, Roelofs, Gontijo-Lopes, Morcos, Namkoong, Farhadi, Carmon, Kornblith, Schmidt. ICML 2022 (arXiv 2203.05482).
- **Key Contribution:** Shows that **uniformly averaging the weights** of multiple independently fine-tuned models (from a shared init) improves accuracy and robustness at zero inference cost. Introduces uniform, greedy, and learned soups.
- **Methodology (merge math):**
  - Uniform soup: θ_S = (1/|S|) Σ_{i∈S} θ_i — plain element-wise mean of full weights.
  - Greedy soup: sort models by val accuracy, add a model to the soup only if it does not decrease held-out accuracy.
  - Critical precondition: all models share a pre-trained initialization θ_0 and lie in the **same loss basin** (mode-connected). Averaging works because independently fine-tuned models from a common init are linearly connected.
  - Theory: averaging gain correlates with the angle φ between (θ_1−θ_0) and (θ_2−θ_0).
- **Models/datasets:** CLIP ViT-B/32, ALIGN, ViT-G/14 (JFT-3B); BERT, T5 on GLUE. ImageNet + 5 distribution shifts.
- **Key results:** Greedy soup beat best single model by 0.7 pts (CLIP), reached 90.94% ImageNet on ViT-G (SOTA at the time). Uniform soup only helps when all ingredients are high-accuracy; otherwise a low-quality ingredient drags the soup down.
- **Code:** https://github.com/mlfoundations/model-soups
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Model soups are the **plain-averaging aggregation operator** — the simplest "merge round." Crucially: a uniform soup is **dragged down by a single bad ingredient** (they explicitly note a low-accuracy model produces a low-accuracy soup). By analogy, one **infected model in an averaging pool contaminates the merged result proportionally to its averaging weight (1/k)**. This is the cleanest mechanism to model "one infected node averages into k−1 clean ones." The shared-init / same-basin requirement is the analog of "models must be susceptible (same lineage) for transmission."

---

## 3. TIES-Merging (Yadav et al., 2023)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *TIES-Merging: Resolving Interference When Merging Models* — Prateek Yadav, Derek Tam, Leshem Choshen, Colin Raffel, Mohit Bansal. NeurIPS 2023 (arXiv 2306.01708).
- **Key Contribution:** Identifies two interference sources when merging many task vectors — (a) redundant low-magnitude parameters and (b) sign conflicts — and fixes them via **Trim → Elect Sign → Disjoint Merge**.
- **Methodology (merge math), Algorithm 1:**
  1. Task vectors τ_t = θ_t − θ_init.
  2. **Trim:** keep top-k% magnitude entries per τ_t, zero the rest (k=20% works; performance depends on a small fraction of high-magnitude params).
  3. **Elect sign:** γ_m = sgn(Σ_t τ̂_t) — pick, per parameter, the sign with the larger total magnitude.
  4. **Disjoint merge:** for each param, average only entries whose sign matches γ_m: τ_m^p = (1/|A_p|) Σ_{t∈A_p} τ̂_t^p.
  5. Final: **θ_m = θ_init + λ·τ_m** (default λ=1; or λ=0.4 for task-arithmetic baseline).
  - Also documents the baseline merge operators precisely: Simple averaging θ_m = Σθ_t/n; Fisher θ_m = Σ F_t θ_t / Σ F_t; RegMean closed-form; Task Arithmetic θ_m = θ_init + λ Σ τ_t.
- **Models/datasets:** T0-3B (IA³ PEFT), T5-base/large, CLIP ViT-B/32 & ViT-L/14 across 7–11 NLP and 8 vision tasks.
- **Key results:** Beats best baseline by ~2.3% (NLP) and 1.7% (vision) in-domain; up to +6.6% without a validation set. **Sign conflicts rise with the number of merged models** (Fig. 4) — directly relevant to scaling contagion.
- **Code:** https://github.com/prateeky2806/ties-merging
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Quantifies **interference as a function of population size**: as more models are merged, sign-conflicts grow, so a harmful direction either gets diluted (if outvoted in the sign election) or **amplified (if it wins the sign vote)**. This gives a concrete "herd" mechanism — whether misalignment survives a merge depends on whether the harmful sign has the **largest total magnitude across the pool**, i.e. a *majority-by-mass voting threshold*. Trimming (top-k) is also a natural model for "only high-magnitude (strongly-held) traits transmit."

---

## 4. Dataless Knowledge Fusion / Fisher & RegMean Merging (Jin et al., 2023)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Dataless Knowledge Fusion by Merging Weights of Language Models* — Xisen Jin, Xiang Ren, Daniel Preoţiuc-Pietro, Pengxiang Cheng. ICLR 2023 (arXiv 2212.09849). (This is the RegMean paper; it also presents the canonical Fisher-merging formulation.)
- **Key Contribution:** Frames merging as minimizing prediction difference; introduces **RegMean** (closed-form regression mean per linear layer). Explicitly situates dataless merging as **"an extreme case of federated learning — a single round of synchronization."**
- **Methodology (merge math):**
  - **Simple:** θ_M = (1/K) Σ_i θ_i.
  - **Fisher-weighted:** θ_M = Σ_i F_i θ_i / Σ_i F_i, where F_i is the diagonal Fisher information F_i = E_x E_y (∇ log p_θ(y|x))² — parameters with high gradient-norm (importance) get more weight.
  - **RegMean:** per linear layer, W_M = (Σ_i X_i^T X_i)^{−1} Σ_i (X_i^T X_i W_i); uses inner-product (Gram) matrices of layer inputs, not raw data. Non-diagonal terms scaled by α≈0.9. Reduces to simple averaging when all Gram matrices are equal.
- **Models/datasets:** RoBERTa-base, DeBERTa-large-v3, T5-base-v1.1, DistilBERT; GLUE, emotion classification, OntoNotes NER, CoNLL/Twitter NER (OOD).
- **Key results:** RegMean > Fisher > Simple. On emotion, RegMean cut the merge-induced performance drop from 55%→12% (RoBERTa) and 85%→15% (DeBERTa). Note: simple averaging can collapse to **0.00** with different head inits — fragile merging.
- **Code:** https://github.com/bloomberg/dataless-model-merging
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Provides the **importance-weighted aggregation** model: a model whose harmful parameters have **high Fisher importance** dominates the merge — i.e. a "highly infectious" node is one that holds its (misaligned) parameters with high confidence/importance, so Fisher weight = "viral load." It also explicitly equates dataless merging with a **single FL round**, bridging the merging and FL pillars. For simulation, Fisher-weighting gives a natural per-node infectivity coefficient F_i.

---

## 5. RESTA — Safety Re-Alignment via Task Arithmetic (Bhardwaj et al., 2024) — "the cure"

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Language Models are Homer Simpson! Safety Re-Alignment of Fine-tuned Language Models through Task Arithmetic* — Rishabh Bhardwaj, Do Duc Anh, Soujanya Poria. 2024 (arXiv 2402.11746).
- **Key Contribution:** Fine-tuning erodes safety even on benign data; **RESTA restores it by adding a safety vector** back to the compromised model — the direct "immunization/cure" operation.
- **Methodology (the cure math):**
  - Models SFT compromise as: θ_SFT^o = θ_SFT^+ − λ·δ_safe (fine-tuning implicitly subtracts safety).
  - **Recovery:** θ̂_SFT^+ = θ_SFT^o + γ·δ_safe = θ_SFT^+ − (λ−γ)·δ_safe. Adding γ·δ_safe cancels the lost safety. (γ=0.5 default; safety scales monotonically with γ up to ~1.)
  - **Safety vector:** δ_safe = θ_base^+ − θ_base (aligned minus unaligned), obtained by **unalignment** (SFT on ~100 harmful QA pairs) then differencing.
  - Optional **DARE** preprocessing: drop delta params with prob p (Bernoulli mask), rescale survivors by 1/(1−p), to give the safety vector more room. (p=0.3 default.)
- **Models/datasets:** Llama-2-7B-Chat; PEFT (LoRA) & Full-FT on Chinese/Hindi/English Alpaca, Code, Math. New CATQA benchmark (550 harmful Qs, 11 categories) + HarmfulQA, AdvBench, DangerousQA.
- **Key results:** Unsafety dropped 18.6%→5.1% (PEFT) and 9.2%→1.5% (Full-FT). **Cost: only ~2.4% (PEFT) / ~0.5% (Full-FT) average task-performance drop** — performance stays near original until γ≈1.
- **Code:** https://github.com/declare-lab/resta
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** This is the **immunization model in arithmetic form**: cure = adding δ_safe with coefficient γ; infection = the latent −λ·δ_safe shift induced by fine-tuning. The single relation θ_SFT^+ − (λ−γ)·δ_safe makes "infection severity" (λ) and "treatment dose" (γ) directly comparable on the same axis — i.e. **you must immunize with γ ≥ λ to neutralize**. The low (~2%) utility cost quantifies the "cost of restoring safety."

---

## 6. SafeMERGE — Selective Layer-Wise Safe Merging (Djuhera et al., 2025)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *SafeMERGE: Preserving Safety Alignment in Fine-Tuned LLMs via Selective Layer-Wise Model Merging* — Aladin Djuhera, Swanand R. Kadhe, Farhan Ahmed, Syed Zawad, Holger Boche (TUM + IBM Research). 2025 (arXiv 2503.17239).
- **Key Contribution:** A lightweight **post-fine-tuning** defense: merge a fine-tuned LoRA layer with a safe LoRA layer **only when that layer has drifted from the safety subspace** (cosine-similarity gate), avoiding the utility cost of merging everything.
- **Methodology (merge math):**
  - Safety subspace per layer: V_i = W_aligned^i − W_unaligned^i; projection C_i = V_i V_i^T / ‖V_i‖_F.
  - Drift metric: ρ_i = cos(ΔW_f^i, C_i ΔW_f^i). Layer flagged **unsafe if ρ_i < τ**.
  - For flagged layers: **linear merge** ΔW_merge^i = α·ΔW_f^i + (1−α)·ΔW_s^i (α≈0.7).
  - Threshold τ controls selectivity; optimal τ ≈ 0.6–0.8 (0.7 Llama-2, 0.75 Llama-3.1, 0.65 Qwen-2). Only ~28–34 LoRA layers get merged.
- **Models/datasets:** Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen-2-7B, Qwen-2.5-7B; GSM8K, PubMedQA (+telecom OOD). Safety eval: DirectHarm, HexPhi via Llama-Guard-3-8B.
- **Key results:** Beat RESTA, RESTA-Instruct, SafeLoRA, SafeInstruct on safety-utility trade-off; e.g. Llama-2 GSM8K DirectHarm 27.8%→7.5% while keeping accuracy. **Found plain linear merging most robust** (TIES inconsistent, DARE comparable).
- **Code:** github.com/aladinD/SafeMERGE
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Demonstrates that **infection is layer-localized** — only a subset of layers drift toward the unsafe direction (measured by cosine angle to the safety subspace). This supports modeling misalignment transmission as occurring through specific "channels" (layers) rather than uniformly, and gives a concrete **geometric infection detector** (cosine < τ). Also empirically confirms **plain linear merging (α-weighted)** is the most robust merge operator — supporting its choice for simulation.

---

## 7. Safety Arithmetic — Test-Time Safety Steering (Hazra et al., 2024)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Safety Arithmetic: A Framework for Test-time Safety Alignment of Language Models by Steering Parameters and Activations* — Rima Hazra, Sayan Layek, Somnath Banerjee, Soujanya Poria. EMNLP 2024 (arXiv 2406.11801).
- **Key Contribution:** Training-free, two-stage safety framework working across Base / SFT / Edited models: **(1) Harm Direction Removal (HDR)** in weight space + **(2) Safe-Align** in activation space.
- **Methodology (the math):**
  - **HDR (weight-space, the "antiviral"):** harm vector τ_H = θ_H − θ_b (θ_H = model SFT'd on harmful QA). Prune to top-k magnitude entries (k≈10%), then **subtract:** θ̂_t = θ_t − λ·τ_H'. (This is exactly negated task arithmetic with TIES-style trimming.)
  - **Safe-Align (activation-space):** compute an in-context safety vector ICV (first principal direction of (h(p_safe) − h(p_unsafe)) over ~30 exemplars), then add to hidden states: (h_sf)_t^l = h_t^l + α·ICV_l, with L2-norm renormalization to preserve capability.
- **Models/datasets:** Llama-2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct; WizardMath, LlamaMath, EvolCodeAlpaca (SFT); ROME-edited models. Datasets: AdvBench, DangerousQA, HarmfulQA, NicheHazardQA, HEx-PHI; new NoIntentEdit dataset.
- **Key results:** Lowest attack-success-rate across all 5 datasets/2 models (e.g. AdvBench Llama-2 19.8%→6.2%). Top-k≈10% keeps MMLU intact while suppressing ASR.
- **Code:** https://github.com/declare-lab/safety-arithmetic
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Reinforces the **negate-the-harm-vector cure** (HDR: θ_t − λ·τ_H), and adds a complementary **activation-steering cure** that doesn't require a merge. Important nuance for the model: misalignment lives both in **weights** (transmissible by merging) and in **activations** (transmissible only at inference). For epidemic modeling, only the weight-space component (τ_H) propagates through merges/FL — the activation component is non-heritable.

---

## 8. FedAvg — Federated Averaging (McMahan et al., 2016/2017)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data* — H. Brendan McMahan, Eider Moore, Daniel Ramage, Seth Hampson, Blaise Agüera y Arcas (Google). AISTATS 2017 (arXiv 1602.05629).
- **Key Contribution:** Defines **Federated Averaging (FedAvg)** — the canonical FL aggregation algorithm: clients do local SGD, server averages weights.
- **Methodology (aggregation math), Algorithm 1:**
  - Each round t: server broadcasts w_t to a random fraction C of K clients. Each client k runs E epochs of local SGD (batch B) to get w_{t+1}^k.
  - **Server aggregation (the FL merge round):** w_{t+1} = Σ_{k∈S_t} (n_k / Σ n_k) · w_{t+1}^k — a **sample-size-weighted average of client weights**. (FedSGD is the E=1, B=∞ special case.)
  - Key precondition (Fig. 1): naive averaging works only with **shared initialization** (common starting w_t each round) — averaging from different random inits gives high loss; from shared init it gives lower loss than either parent.
- **Models/datasets:** MNIST (2NN MLP, CNN), Shakespeare LSTM, CIFAR-10; IID and pathological non-IID partitions.
- **Key results:** 10–100× fewer communication rounds vs FedSGD; robust to non-IID. Key parameters: C (client fraction), E (local epochs), B (local batch). Large E can cause divergence.
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** This is the **FL transmission operator** for the simulation. Each round, an infected client k injects its weights into the global model with **aggregation weight n_k/Σn_k** — that weight is precisely the "contact rate × viral load." The global model is then re-broadcast to all clients, so **one round = simultaneous re-infection of the entire susceptible population** from the merged centroid. The shared-init requirement = the "same-lineage susceptibility" condition for contagion.

---

## 9. Can You Really Backdoor Federated Learning? (Sun et al., 2019)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Can You Really Backdoor Federated Learning?* — Ziteng Sun, Peter Kairouz, Ananda Theertha Suresh, H. Brendan McMahan (Cornell + Google). 2019 (arXiv 1911.07963).
- **Key Contribution:** Empirically studies whether backdoors **persist/propagate** through FedAvg, and shows **norm-clipping + weak DP** largely neutralize them.
- **Methodology (propagation math):**
  - Aggregation: w_{t+1} = w_t + η Σ_{k∈S_t} (n_k/Σn_k) Δw_t^k.
  - **Model-replacement attack** (the "super-spreader"): attacker sends Δw_t^1 = β(w* − w_t) with **boost factor β = (Σ n_k)/(η n_k)** so the malicious model w* survives averaging — i.e. it scales its update up to overwhelm the aggregation weight. After aggregation the global ≈ w* (if other updates are small near convergence).
  - **Defenses:** norm-thresholding/clipping (divide each update by max(1, ‖Δ‖/M)); weak differential privacy (clip + small Gaussian noise σ).
- **Models/datasets:** EMNIST (3383 users, non-IID, writer-partitioned), 5-layer CNN, TensorFlow Federated. Backdoor = misclassify "7"s from target clients as "1"s.
- **Key results:** **Attack success depends heavily on the fraction ε of adversaries** — degrades sharply when ε < 1%. Benign clients holding correctly-labeled target samples actively "heal" the backdoor. Norm bound = 3 fully mitigates with no main-task cost; Gaussian noise σ=0.025 helps further.
- **Code:** github.com/tensorflow/federated (.../research/targeted_attack)
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** This is the **most directly epidemiological FL paper.** It establishes (a) a **critical adversary fraction (~1%)** below which infection dies out — the analog of an epidemic threshold / R₀<1; (b) benign clients with correct labels act as **immune/recovering nodes that drive backdoor accuracy back down** (a recovery rate); (c) the **boost factor β is the explicit transmissibility multiplier**; and (d) **norm-clipping = an intervention that caps per-node transmission** (caps the effective dose any one infected node can inject). Maps almost 1:1 onto an SIS/SIR model with intervention coverage.

---

## 10. Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in FL — ARU (Kim et al., 2023)

- **Title / Authors / Year:** *Adversarial Robustness Unhardening via Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning* — Taejin Kim, Jiarui Li, Nikhil Madaan, Shubhranshu Singh, Carlee Joe-Wong (CMU). NeurIPS 2023 Workshop / extended 2025 (arXiv 2310.11594).
- **Key Contribution:** Introduces **ARU**, where a small subset of clients **undoes adversarial-training robustness** of the global FL model — a backdoor that degrades robustness for *all* inputs (broader than a pixel-pattern backdoor), and that can **erode robustness gradually over multiple rounds**.
- **Methodology (propagation math):**
  - Model-replacement: R = G_t + Σ_i (1/γ_i)(U_i^{t+1} − G_t); attacker uploads U_j^{t+1} = γ_j R − (γ_j−1)G_t − Σ(1/γ_i)(U_i − G_t) ≈ **γ_j(R − G_t) + G_t** (boost by γ_j to dominate aggregation).
  - **Non-robust model extraction:** since adversaries lack a pre-made non-robust model, colluding clients overfit the global FAT model on PGD-perturbed mislabeled data to **induce catastrophic forgetting** of robustness, then replace.
  - **Iterative ARU:** scaling variable β (U_j ≈ γ_jβ(R−G_t)+G_t); smaller β → more gradual replacement that **evades trimmed-mean defenses**.
  - Key geometric finding (Fig. 4): FAT and FedAvg models are **highly similar (cosine >0.95 in most layers)**; robustness is concentrated in a few layers, and **robustness varies continuously along the interpolation path** between robust and non-robust models — so gradual erosion is feasible.
- **Models/datasets:** CIFAR-10/100, CelebA, FakeNewsNet; federated adversarial training (FAT), 40 clients. Defenses: trimmed-mean, coordinate-wise median.
- **Key results:** ARU drops adversarial accuracy from 0.427→0.004 (CIFAR-10) while keeping benign accuracy high (stealth). Single-shot ARU is blocked by trimmed-mean/median, but **iterative ARU bypasses trimmed-mean** (the privacy-preserving defense); only median (which breaks FL privacy) resists, and even that fails under non-IID data or >50% adversaries.
- **Relevance to hypothesis:** Provides the **gradual / multi-round contagion** model: because robustness (safety) degrades *continuously* along the weight-interpolation path, an infected minority can erode a property **incrementally each FL round** rather than all-at-once — a slow-burn epidemic. It also shows **robust-aggregation defenses (trimmed-mean) have a coverage threshold they can't exceed** and fail under heterogeneity, directly informing the "critical immunization coverage" part of the hypothesis. Note: this concerns adversarial *robustness*, a proxy for the safety property, but the propagation mechanics are identical to misalignment.

---

# Synthesis: The Operational Infection Mechanism and What to Implement

### The concrete transmission mechanism

Across all 10 papers, misalignment transfers between models through **one unifying operation: a weighted sum in weight space, gated on shared initialization (common lineage).** Two equivalent formulations appear:

1. **Task-vector form (merging pillar):** an infected model contributes a misalignment direction
   **τ_harm = θ_infected − θ_pre**, which transmits to a clean model as
   **θ_new = θ_clean + λ · τ_harm.**
   The scaling coefficient **λ is the single "dose / transmissibility" knob.** Empirically (Ilharco; RESTA; Safety Arithmetic) the harmful direction is **near-orthogonal** to benign task directions, so it transmits with minimal collateral — high transmissibility. The **cure is the same operation with a negative sign or a safety vector** (RESTA: +γ·δ_safe; Safety Arithmetic HDR: −λ·τ_H), so neutralization requires **γ ≥ λ.**

2. **FedAvg form (FL pillar):** in each round the global model is
   **w_{t+1} = Σ_k (n_k / Σ n_k) · w_{t+1}^k.**
   An infected client transmits with **aggregation weight n_k/Σn_k**, which an attacker amplifies by a **boost factor β** (Sun et al.: β = Σn_k/(η n_k); Kim et al.: γ_j) to dominate the average. The merged model is re-broadcast, re-infecting the whole population each round.

These are the **same operator**: Jin et al. explicitly call dataless merging "an extreme case of federated learning — a single round of synchronization." A model soup / linear merge is one FedAvg round; FL is iterated merging.

### The epidemic parameters the papers hand us directly

- **Transmission coefficient:** λ (task-vector scale) or the FedAvg aggregation weight n_k/Σn_k × boost β.
- **Per-node viral load / infectivity:** Fisher importance F_i (Jin et al.) or task-vector magnitude (TIES top-k) — high-magnitude, high-importance parameters dominate the merge.
- **Critical threshold (R₀ analog):** Sun et al. give an explicit **~1% adversary-fraction threshold** below which backdoors die out; TIES gives a **sign-election majority-by-mass threshold**; ARU gives a **robust-aggregation coverage threshold** that fails under heterogeneity / >50% adversaries.
- **Recovery / immunization:** benign clients with correct labels pull the model back (Sun et al.); explicit cures add δ_safe with dose γ (RESTA) at ~2% utility cost; norm-clipping/DP cap per-node transmission.
- **Susceptibility precondition:** shared pre-trained initialization (FedAvg Fig. 1; Soups; RegMean) — only same-lineage models are "susceptible."

### Recommended lightweight method to implement for the "infection" simulation

**Implement plain linear weight averaging with a scaling/task-vector coefficient — i.e. the Model-Soup / Task-Arithmetic operator — as the core merge step, and iterate it as FedAvg for the FL setting.** Rationale:

- It is the **minimal common denominator** of every paper here and is computationally trivial (element-wise mean / θ + λτ, CPU-only).
- **SafeMERGE empirically found plain linear merging the most robust** operator (TIES inconsistent, DARE only comparable), so the simple operator is also the realistic one.
- It exposes exactly the two knobs the hypothesis needs: the **mixing/aggregation weight (1/k or n_k/Σn_k)** = contact/transmission rate, and **λ** = dose. A single infected ingredient contaminates the average proportional to its weight (Soups: one bad ingredient drags the soup down).

Concretely, model **infection** as: per merge/FL round, global θ ← Σ_i a_i θ_i (a_i = aggregation weights), where an infected node injects θ_pre + λ·τ_harm; model **immunization** as adding −γ·τ_harm (or +γ·δ_safe) to a fraction p of nodes and showing that misalignment dies out once **p exceeds a critical coverage** (the Sun-et-al. ~1% / TIES sign-mass / ARU robust-aggregation thresholds). Optionally add a **Fisher/magnitude weight** per node to model heterogeneous infectivity, and a **norm-clip** as the capped-transmission intervention. This reproduces an SIS/SIR-with-vaccination dynamic using nothing heavier than weighted averaging.

# Literature Review Notes — Epidemic / Network-Theory Pillar
### Project: Epidemiological Modeling of Misalignment Propagation in Distributed LLM Fine-Tuning

All four papers converge on one central, testable result: **the epidemic threshold of a spreading process on a network is governed by the largest eigenvalue (spectral radius) of the network's adjacency matrix**, with the critical condition `β/μ = 1/λ_max`. This is the formula our project should test.

---

## 1. Pastor-Satorras, Castellano, Van Mieghem & Vespignani (2015) — *Epidemic Processes in Complex Networks* ⭐ (review)

- **Title / Venue:** Epidemic processes in complex networks, *Reviews of Modern Physics* 87, 925 (arXiv:1408.2701)
- **Authors:** R. Pastor-Satorras, C. Castellano, P. Van Mieghem, A. Vespignani
- **Year:** 2015
- **Key Contribution:** Canonical unifying review of epidemic spreading on networks. Lays out SIS/SIR models, the two dominant mean-field theories (heterogeneous/degree-based HMF and individual-based/quenched QMF), the spectral epidemic threshold, and immunization strategies. This is the reference text whose framework our whole pillar rests on.

- **The model (governing equations & rates):**
  - Compartments: **S** (susceptible), **I** (infected), **R** (recovered/removed). Reaction form:
    - SIS: `S + I —β→ 2I`, `I —μ→ S` (reinfection possible → endemic steady state exists)
    - SIR: `S + I —β→ 2I`, `I —μ→ R` (permanent removal → always dies out eventually)
  - **β** = infection rate per S–I contact; **μ** = recovery rate; mean infectious period = `1/μ`. Effective spreading rate **λ ≡ β/μ**.
  - Homogeneous mixing (mass-action) ODEs:  `dρ_I/dt = β ρ_I ρ_S − μ ρ_I`. Linearized early-time: `dρ_I/dt ≈ (β−μ)ρ_I ⇒ ρ_I(t) ≈ ρ_I(0)e^{(β−μ)t}`.
  - **Basic reproduction number:** `R₀ = β/μ` (well-mixed); on a homogeneous contact network with mean degree ⟨k⟩, `R₀ = ⟨k⟩β/μ`. Outbreak iff `R₀ > 1`.

- **The epidemic threshold result (the core take-away):**
  - **Classical (well-mixed):** outbreak iff `R₀ = β/μ > 1`.
  - **Heterogeneous Mean-Field (HMF / degree-based), uncorrelated network:**
    `λ_c = ⟨k⟩ / ⟨k²⟩`  (Eq. 28). For regular networks (⟨k²⟩=⟨k⟩²) this reduces to `λ_c = 1/⟨k⟩`. For scale-free networks with degree exponent 2<γ≤3, ⟨k²⟩→∞ ⇒ **threshold → 0** (epidemics always spread).
  - **Quenched / Individual-Based Mean-Field (QMF / IBMF / NIMFA)** — the result we will use. Per-node ODE:
    `dρ_i/dt = −ρ_i + λ(1−ρ_i) Σ_j a_ij ρ_j`  (Eq. 33, with μ rescaled to 1).
    Linear stability of the all-healthy state gives the threshold:
    **`λ_c = 1 / Λ₁`**  (Eq. 34), where **Λ₁ is the largest eigenvalue (spectral radius) of the adjacency matrix A**. Endemic iff `λ = β/μ ≥ 1/Λ₁`.
  - For power-law nets, `Λ₁ ≈ max{√k_max, ⟨k²⟩/⟨k⟩}`, so `λ_c` vanishes whenever k_max grows with system size.

- **Immunization / intervention results (Section VI.A):** Immunizing a fraction `g` of nodes (removing them + their links) raises the effective threshold `λ_c(g)` ("herd immunity"). Define immunization threshold `g_c(λ)`: prevalence is zero for g > g_c.
  - **Random immunization:** equivalent to rescaling `λ → λ(1−g)`; threshold `g_c(λ) = 1 − ⟨k⟩/(λ⟨k²⟩)` (Eq. 63). On heterogeneous nets g_c→1 (must immunize almost everyone — inefficient).
  - **Targeted immunization** (remove highest-degree hubs first): exponentially small threshold, `g_c(λ) ≈ exp[−2/(mλ)]` for γ=3. Far more efficient — killing hubs collapses Λ₁.
  - **Acquaintance immunization** (pick random node, immunize a random *neighbor*): local, no global degree knowledge needed, preferentially hits hubs because random edges point to high-degree nodes. Practical when network structure is only partially known.
  - **Betweenness-centrality / equal-graph-partitioning** strategies fragment the network and are even more efficient for SIR.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** This is the backbone reference. It supplies (a) the SIS↔endemic / SIR↔transient distinction we map onto "misalignment that persists vs. self-extinguishes," (b) the exact threshold `λ_c = 1/Λ₁` to test in simulation, (c) the hub-targeting immunization theory that directly motivates "which models to re-align (safety-patch) to halt propagation," and (d) the warning that heterogeneous (hub-dominated) merge networks can have near-zero thresholds → misalignment almost always becomes endemic unless hubs are protected.

---

## 2. Van Mieghem (2014) — *Exact Markovian SIR and SIS epidemics on networks and an upper bound for the epidemic threshold*

- **Title / Venue:** arXiv:1402.1731 [math.DS]
- **Authors:** P. Van Mieghem (Delft)
- **Year:** 2014
- **Key Contribution:** Derives the *exact* (non-mean-field) stochastic governing equations for SIS and SIR on an arbitrary fixed graph using indicator/Bernoulli random variables, expresses the max prevalence as a quadratic form of the graph **Laplacian Q = Δ − A**, and — crucially — proves both a **lower and an upper bound** on the true epidemic threshold. Shows the famous `1/λ₁` is *exact* only under mean-field but is a rigorous *lower bound* for the true stochastic process.

- **The model (rates):** Continuous-time Markovian. Per-link infection = Poisson rate **β**; per-node curing = Poisson rate **δ**. Effective infection rate **τ ≡ β/δ** (same role as λ above). SIS: I→S; SIR: I→R (removed). Exact equation for node infection probability:
  `d Pr[X_j=I]/dt = β Σ_k a_kj Pr[X_k=I] − δ Pr[X_j=I] − β·(pair-correlation term)`.

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **Lower bound (rigorous, any graph, SIS and SIR, directed or undirected):**
    **`τ_c ≥ 1/λ₁`**  (Eq. 6), λ₁ = largest eigenvalue of A. This `τ_c^{(1)} = 1/λ₁` is *exactly* the NIMFA/QMF mean-field threshold, but for the true stochastic model it is only a lower bound. Also proves `τ_{c,SIS} ≤ τ_{c,SIR}` (SIS spreads more easily).
  - **Upper bound (Theorem 2):** `τ_c ≤ 1 / [d_min(1 − ε_G)]`, where d_min is the minimum degree and ε_G is the maximal conditional infection probability on a link at outbreak onset (bounded by the complete-graph value, ε_G ≤ O(1/√N)). So `1/λ₁ ≤ τ_c ≤ 1/[d_min(1−ε_G)]` — the true threshold is sandwiched.
  - **Practical reading:** if you can keep `τ = β/δ ≤ 1/λ₁`, or lower λ₁ by editing the network, the system is provably safe from massive long-term infection. Notes that any *finite* network has a strictly positive threshold even if `1/λ₁ → 0` as N→∞.

- **Immunization / intervention:** No explicit immunization protocols, but the actionable lever is the same: **reduce the spectral radius λ₁** of the contact graph (by removing nodes/links) to push 1/λ₁ above the operating τ — i.e., network design as defense.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** Gives us mathematical rigor and a *bracket* (lower + upper bound) rather than just the mean-field point estimate. Tells us our simulated threshold for misalignment-SIS should land between `1/λ₁` and `1/[d_min(1−ε)]`. Also justifies treating "lower λ₁ of the merge/FL interaction graph" as a concrete defensive intervention, and warns that mean-field `1/λ₁` may *overestimate* danger (true τ_c is somewhat higher).

---

## 3. Castellano & Pastor-Satorras (2010) — *Thresholds for Epidemic Spreading in Networks*

- **Title / Venue:** *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 105, 218701 (arXiv:1010.1646)
- **Authors:** C. Castellano, R. Pastor-Satorras
- **Year:** 2010
- **Key Contribution:** The foundational result that the SIS threshold on a quenched (fixed) network is set by `λ_c = 1/Λ_N` (largest adjacency eigenvalue), **not** by ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩ as HMF claimed. Identifies the physical mechanism (a single high-degree hub acts as a self-sustained reservoir) and shows SIS and SIR behave differently.

- **The model (rates):** SIS — susceptibles infected at rate ∝ (λ × number of infected neighbors); recovery rate set to 1, so λ is the effective spreading rate (= β/μ). SIR identical but recovered nodes are permanently removed (no steady state).

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **SIS, exact on any quenched graph:** `λ_c = Λ_N^{-1} = 1/λ_max(A)` (Eq. 1, attributing it to Wang et al. 2003).
  - Combined with Chung et al.'s eigenvalue estimate `Λ_N ≈ max{√k_max, ⟨k²⟩/⟨k⟩}` gives the scaling:
    `λ_c ≈ 1/√k_max` for γ > 5/2, and `λ_c ≈ ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩` for 2 < γ < 5/2 (Eq. 3).
  - **Star-graph mechanism:** for a hub of degree k_max, the hub+leaves subgraph self-sustains infection for any `λ > 1/√k_max`, acting as a reservoir that re-seeds the rest of the network → threshold vanishes as k_max→∞ for *any* network with a growing maximum degree (even Erdős–Rényi, logarithmically).
  - **SIR is different:** because hubs can only be infected once (no reinfection), the steady-state reservoir mechanism is absent. SIR obeys HMF: `λ_c^{SIR} = ⟨k⟩/[⟨k²⟩ − ⟨k⟩]`, which stays **finite** for scale-rich networks (γ > 3). General conjecture: **models with a steady state (SIS) → vanishing threshold on heterogeneous nets; models without (SIR) → finite threshold.**

- **Immunization / intervention:** Not the focus, but the corollary is sharp — because a *single* hub can sustain SIS, controlling/removing the largest-degree node(s) is disproportionately effective (motivates targeted hub protection in our setting).

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** Directly tells us (a) the threshold to test is `1/λ_max(A)`, (b) **whether misalignment is SIS-like or SIR-like is the decisive modeling question** — if a misaligned model can be re-infected/re-corrupted repeatedly (SIS), even a single persistently-misaligned "hub" model (one heavily-merged checkpoint or a popular base model) can keep the whole population endemic; if misalignment is "use-once" SIR-like, the population can stay below threshold. This sharpens our hypothesis and gives a falsifiable distinction.

---

## 4. Sanatkar, White, Natarajan, Scoglio & Garrett (2015) — *Epidemic Threshold of an SIS Model in Dynamic Switching Networks*

- **Title / Venue:** arXiv:1501.02472 [cs.SI]
- **Authors:** M. R. Sanatkar, W. N. White, B. Natarajan, C. M. Scoglio, K. A. Garrett
- **Year:** 2015
- **Key Contribution:** Extends the spectral epidemic threshold to **time-varying / switching networks**, where the adjacency matrix changes over time (the network is drawn from a set of topologies at each step). Derives the threshold via the **joint spectral radius (JSR)** of the set of system matrices, and proves a simplified form for undirected graphs. Highly relevant because in distributed fine-tuning the model-interaction graph (who merges with whom each round) changes every training round.

- **The model (discrete-time SIS):** Node infection probability update:
  `p_i(t+1) = p_i(t)(1−δ) + Σ_{j∈N_i(t)} p_j(t) β`  (Eq. 5), where δ = recovery prob, β = per-neighbor infection prob, and the neighbor set N_i(t) (i.e. the adjacency matrix A_t) **changes with t**. Matrix form:
  `P_{t+1} = M_t P_t` with **system matrix** `M_t = (1−δ)I + β A_t`.

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **Theorem 1 (general dynamic/arbitrary switching):** Let M = {M_i = (1−δ)I + βA_i} be the set of system matrices. The epidemic dies out (origin asymptotically stable) iff the **joint spectral radius ρ̂(M) < 1**. (JSR = limiting max norm of all length-k products of matrices from the set.)
  - **Theorem 2 (undirected graphs — simplified):** For symmetric A_i, the JSR equals the **maximum spectral radius over the set**: `ρ̂(M) = max_i ρ(M_i)`. So the epidemic dies out iff `max_i ρ((1−δ)I + βA_i) < 1` — i.e., it suffices to check the single worst (densest-eigenvalue) topology in the switching set.
  - **Corollary 1 (static network):** recovers the classic result — epidemic dies out iff **`β/δ < 1/ρ(A)`** = `1/λ_max(A)`. Consistent with papers 1–3.
  - **Corollary 2 (periodic networks, period T):** dies out iff `ρ(∏_{i=1}^{T} [(1−δ)I + βA_i]) < 1` — the spectral radius of the *product* of one full period's system matrices.
  - **Corollary 3 (dynamic regular networks, degree k):** dies out iff `β/δ < 1/k` — identical to the static regular-network threshold.

- **Immunization / intervention:** No explicit protocol, but the JSR / max-spectral-radius criterion implies the lever is to **keep every per-round topology (and especially the worst one, or the product over a period) below the spectral bound** — i.e., schedule/sparsify the merge graph so no round (or cumulative period) exceeds threshold.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** This is the paper that matches our actual setting best: the merge/federated-aggregation graph is **not static** — it switches every round. It tells us the right object to compute is the **joint spectral radius of the sequence of round-wise interaction matrices** (or the spectral radius of the per-period product), not just a single λ_max. For undirected merge graphs we can use the simpler `max_i ρ(M_i) < 1` test. It also confirms periodic FL schedules reduce to a product-of-matrices criterion.

---

# Synthesis — Mapping Epidemiology onto Misalignment Propagation in Distributed LLM Fine-Tuning

The four papers give a single, consistent quantitative theory. We map each epidemic primitive onto the LLM-population setting:

| Epidemic quantity | Symbol | LLM-population analogue |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Infection rate** | **β** | Per-interaction probability that misalignment transfers when a clean model absorbs weights from a misaligned one — i.e., the *transmissibility* of misaligned parameters/behavior through a single merge, task-arithmetic addition, federated-aggregation round, or model-on-model distillation. Higher β = larger merge weight given to the misaligned model, more aligned parameter directions, stronger persona/feature entanglement. |
| **(b) Recovery / healing rate** | **μ (or δ)** | The **alignment-intervention rate**: probability per round that a misaligned model is "cured" — safety re-alignment / re-fine-tuning on safe data, safety-arithmetic / TIES-style safety preservation, safe-merge projection, or detection-and-quarantine of a corrupted checkpoint. `1/μ` = mean time a model stays misaligned before being patched. |
| **(c) Contact network** | **A (or A_t)** | The **model-interaction graph**: nodes = models/checkpoints in the population; an edge i–j means i and j share/merge weights (model soups, task vectors, FL aggregation, distillation). Degree = how many other models a given checkpoint merges with. **Hubs** = popular base models or heavily-reused checkpoints. In distributed fine-tuning this graph **switches per round** → use the time-varying matrix A_t (Paper 4). |
| **(d) Epidemic threshold** | **τ_c = λ_c** | The critical effective transmission ratio. Misalignment self-extinguishes below it, becomes endemic above it. Set by the **spectral radius of the interaction graph**. |
| **(e) Basic reproduction number** | **R₀** | Expected number of *new* models a single misaligned model corrupts before it is re-aligned. `R₀ = (β/μ)·λ_max(A)`. Endemic/epidemic iff R₀ > 1. |

### Concrete threshold formula to test in simulation

**Static / single-round merge graph (Papers 1–3, Cor. 1 of Paper 4):**

$$\boxed{\ \frac{\beta}{\mu} > \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}(A)} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\mu}\,\lambda_{\max}(A) > 1\ }$$

- Below it (`β/μ < 1/λ_max`): misalignment **self-extinguishes** — the population heals faster than corruption spreads.
- Above it: misalignment becomes **endemic** (SIS-like, persistent) or causes a full **epidemic** (SIR-like, one-time sweep).
- Rigorous bracket from Paper 2 to validate against: `1/λ_max(A) ≤ τ_c ≤ 1/[d_min(1−ε_G)]` (the mean-field value `1/λ_max` is a *lower bound* on the true stochastic threshold — i.e. somewhat conservative/pessimistic).

**Time-varying / per-round federated schedule (Paper 4 — the realistic case):** replace `λ_max(A)` with the joint spectral radius of the per-round system matrices `M_t = (1−μ)I + β A_t`:

$$\text{misalignment dies out} \iff \hat\rho(\{M_t\}) < 1,\quad M_t = (1-\mu)I + \beta A_t.$$

For undirected merge graphs this simplifies (Thm 2) to `max_t ρ((1−μ)I + β A_t) < 1` — check the single densest round. For a periodic FL schedule of period T (Cor. 2): `ρ(∏_{t=1}^{T} M_t) < 1`.

### Modeling decisions this pillar forces on us
1. **SIS vs SIR is the pivotal choice (Paper 3).** If a healed model can be re-corrupted in later rounds → **SIS** → on heterogeneous (hub-dominated) merge graphs the threshold *vanishes* and even one persistently-misaligned hub keeps the population endemic. If corruption is effectively one-shot per model → **SIR** → finite threshold, population can stay safe. Our simulations should run both and report which regime emergent misalignment empirically obeys.
2. **Heterogeneity is dangerous.** Scale-free merge graphs (a few super-popular base models) have `λ_c → ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩ → 0`: misalignment spreads for almost any β. Realistic model-sharing ecosystems are hub-dominated, so the default prediction is "endemic unless actively defended."
3. **Intervention design (Paper 1).** Random re-alignment of models is inefficient (`g_c ≈ 1`); **targeted re-alignment of hub models** (most-merged checkpoints) is exponentially more efficient (`g_c ≈ e^{−2/(mλ)}`). **Acquaintance-style** intervention (patch a random *neighbor* of a random model) is a practical, structure-light heuristic that preferentially protects hubs. The operational lever in all cases: **lower the spectral radius λ_max of the merge graph** (sparsify/quarantine high-degree corrupt models) to push `1/λ_max` above the operating `β/μ`.

### Simulation plan implied
Build the model-interaction graph (or per-round sequence A_t), estimate β (misalignment transfer per merge) and μ (re-alignment rate) empirically from the merging/FL pillar, compute `R₀ = (β/μ)λ_max(A)` (or the JSR), and verify the phase transition at `R₀ = 1`. Then test immunization: re-align a fraction g of nodes by degree-targeted vs. random vs. acquaintance selection and measure the shift in `λ_c(g)` / endemic prevalence.

---

**Files read (all under `/workspaces/epidemiological_modeling_of_mi_20260626_012502_5c899dff/papers/`):**
- `1408.2701_epidemic_processes_complex_networks_pastorsatorras2015.pdf`
- `1402.1731_exact_markovian_sir_sis_epidemic_threshold2014.pdf`
- `1010.1646_thresholds_epidemic_spreading_networks2010.pdf`
- `1501.02472_epidemic_threshold_dynamic_switching_networks2015.pdf`

(Note: PDF page-image rendering via the Read tool was unavailable — poppler not installable without root — so I extracted text with pypdf and read all relevant equation-bearing sections directly. All threshold formulas above were verified against the source text.)

## 1. Pastor-Satorras, Castellano, Van Mieghem & Vespignani (2015) — *Epidemic Processes in Complex Networks* ⭐ (review)

- **Title / Venue:** Epidemic processes in complex networks, *Reviews of Modern Physics* 87, 925 (arXiv:1408.2701)
- **Authors:** R. Pastor-Satorras, C. Castellano, P. Van Mieghem, A. Vespignani

- **Key Contribution:** Canonical unifying review of epidemic spreading on networks. Lays out SIS/SIR models, the two dominant mean-field theories (heterogeneous/degree-based HMF and individual-based/quenched QMF), the spectral epidemic threshold, and immunization strategies. This is the reference text whose framework our whole pillar rests on.

- **The model (governing equations & rates):**
  - Compartments: **S** (susceptible), **I** (infected), **R** (recovered/removed). Reaction form:
    - SIS: `S + I —β→ 2I`, `I —μ→ S` (reinfection possible → endemic steady state exists)
    - SIR: `S + I —β→ 2I`, `I —μ→ R` (permanent removal → always dies out eventually)
  - **β** = infection rate per S–I contact; **μ** = recovery rate; mean infectious period = `1/μ`. Effective spreading rate **λ ≡ β/μ**.
  - Homogeneous mixing (mass-action) ODEs:  `dρ_I/dt = β ρ_I ρ_S − μ ρ_I`. Linearized early-time: `dρ_I/dt ≈ (β−μ)ρ_I ⇒ ρ_I(t) ≈ ρ_I(0)e^{(β−μ)t}`.
  - **Basic reproduction number:** `R₀ = β/μ` (well-mixed); on a homogeneous contact network with mean degree ⟨k⟩, `R₀ = ⟨k⟩β/μ`. Outbreak iff `R₀ > 1`.

- **The epidemic threshold result (the core take-away):**
  - **Classical (well-mixed):** outbreak iff `R₀ = β/μ > 1`.
  - **Heterogeneous Mean-Field (HMF / degree-based), uncorrelated network:**
    `λ_c = ⟨k⟩ / ⟨k²⟩`  (Eq. 28). For regular networks (⟨k²⟩=⟨k⟩²) this reduces to `λ_c = 1/⟨k⟩`. For scale-free networks with degree exponent 2<γ≤3, ⟨k²⟩→∞ ⇒ **threshold → 0** (epidemics always spread).
  - **Quenched / Individual-Based Mean-Field (QMF / IBMF / NIMFA)** — the result we will use. Per-node ODE:
    `dρ_i/dt = −ρ_i + λ(1−ρ_i) Σ_j a_ij ρ_j`  (Eq. 33, with μ rescaled to 1).
    Linear stability of the all-healthy state gives the threshold:
    **`λ_c = 1 / Λ₁`**  (Eq. 34), where **Λ₁ is the largest eigenvalue (spectral radius) of the adjacency matrix A**. Endemic iff `λ = β/μ ≥ 1/Λ₁`.
  - For power-law nets, `Λ₁ ≈ max{√k_max, ⟨k²⟩/⟨k⟩}`, so `λ_c` vanishes whenever k_max grows with system size.

- **Immunization / intervention results (Section VI.A):** Immunizing a fraction `g` of nodes (removing them + their links) raises the effective threshold `λ_c(g)` ("herd immunity"). Define immunization threshold `g_c(λ)`: prevalence is zero for g > g_c.
  - **Random immunization:** equivalent to rescaling `λ → λ(1−g)`; threshold `g_c(λ) = 1 − ⟨k⟩/(λ⟨k²⟩)` (Eq. 63). On heterogeneous nets g_c→1 (must immunize almost everyone — inefficient).
  - **Targeted immunization** (remove highest-degree hubs first): exponentially small threshold, `g_c(λ) ≈ exp[−2/(mλ)]` for γ=3. Far more efficient — killing hubs collapses Λ₁.
  - **Acquaintance immunization** (pick random node, immunize a random *neighbor*): local, no global degree knowledge needed, preferentially hits hubs because random edges point to high-degree nodes. Practical when network structure is only partially known.
  - **Betweenness-centrality / equal-graph-partitioning** strategies fragment the network and are even more efficient for SIR.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** This is the backbone reference. It supplies (a) the SIS↔endemic / SIR↔transient distinction we map onto "misalignment that persists vs. self-extinguishes," (b) the exact threshold `λ_c = 1/Λ₁` to test in simulation, (c) the hub-targeting immunization theory that directly motivates "which models to re-align (safety-patch) to halt propagation," and (d) the warning that heterogeneous (hub-dominated) merge networks can have near-zero thresholds → misalignment almost always becomes endemic unless hubs are protected.

---

## 2. Van Mieghem (2014) — *Exact Markovian SIR and SIS epidemics on networks and an upper bound for the epidemic threshold*

- **Title / Venue:** arXiv:1402.1731 [math.DS]
- **Authors:** P. Van Mieghem (Delft)
- **Year:** 2014
- **Key Contribution:** Derives the *exact* (non-mean-field) stochastic governing equations for SIS and SIR on an arbitrary fixed graph using indicator/Bernoulli random variables, expresses the max prevalence as a quadratic form of the graph **Laplacian Q = Δ − A**, and — crucially — proves both a **lower and an upper bound** on the true epidemic threshold. Shows the famous `1/λ₁` is *exact* only under mean-field but is a rigorous *lower bound* for the true stochastic process.

- **The model (rates):** Continuous-time Markovian. Per-link infection = Poisson rate **β**; per-node curing = Poisson rate **δ**. Effective infection rate **τ ≡ β/δ** (same role as λ above). SIS: I→S; SIR: I→R (removed). Exact equation for node infection probability:
  `d Pr[X_j=I]/dt = β Σ_k a_kj Pr[X_k=I] − δ Pr[X_j=I] − β·(pair-correlation term)`.

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **Lower bound (rigorous, any graph, SIS and SIR, directed or undirected):**
    **`τ_c ≥ 1/λ₁`**  (Eq. 6), λ₁ = largest eigenvalue of A. This `τ_c^{(1)} = 1/λ₁` is *exactly* the NIMFA/QMF mean-field threshold, but for the true stochastic model it is only a lower bound. Also proves `τ_{c,SIS} ≤ τ_{c,SIR}` (SIS spreads more easily).
  - **Upper bound (Theorem 2):** `τ_c ≤ 1 / [d_min(1 − ε_G)]`, where d_min is the minimum degree and ε_G is the maximal conditional infection probability on a link at outbreak onset (bounded by the complete-graph value, ε_G ≤ O(1/√N)). So `1/λ₁ ≤ τ_c ≤ 1/[d_min(1−ε_G)]` — the true threshold is sandwiched.
  - **Practical reading:** if you can keep `τ = β/δ ≤ 1/λ₁`, or lower λ₁ by editing the network, the system is provably safe from massive long-term infection. Notes that any *finite* network has a strictly positive threshold even if `1/λ₁ → 0` as N→∞.

- **Immunization / intervention:** No explicit immunization protocols, but the actionable lever is the same: **reduce the spectral radius λ₁** of the contact graph (by removing nodes/links) to push 1/λ₁ above the operating τ — i.e., network design as defense.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** Gives us mathematical rigor and a *bracket* (lower + upper bound) rather than just the mean-field point estimate. Tells us our simulated threshold for misalignment-SIS should land between `1/λ₁` and `1/[d_min(1−ε)]`. Also justifies treating "lower λ₁ of the merge/FL interaction graph" as a concrete defensive intervention, and warns that mean-field `1/λ₁` may *overestimate* danger (true τ_c is somewhat higher).

---

## 3. Castellano & Pastor-Satorras (2010) — *Thresholds for Epidemic Spreading in Networks*

- **Title / Venue:** *Phys. Rev. Lett.* 105, 218701 (arXiv:1010.1646)
- **Authors:** C. Castellano, R. Pastor-Satorras
- **Year:** 2010
- **Key Contribution:** The foundational result that the SIS threshold on a quenched (fixed) network is set by `λ_c = 1/Λ_N` (largest adjacency eigenvalue), **not** by ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩ as HMF claimed. Identifies the physical mechanism (a single high-degree hub acts as a self-sustained reservoir) and shows SIS and SIR behave differently.

- **The model (rates):** SIS — susceptibles infected at rate ∝ (λ × number of infected neighbors); recovery rate set to 1, so λ is the effective spreading rate (= β/μ). SIR identical but recovered nodes are permanently removed (no steady state).

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **SIS, exact on any quenched graph:** `λ_c = Λ_N^{-1} = 1/λ_max(A)` (Eq. 1, attributing it to Wang et al. 2003).
  - Combined with Chung et al.'s eigenvalue estimate `Λ_N ≈ max{√k_max, ⟨k²⟩/⟨k⟩}` gives the scaling:
    `λ_c ≈ 1/√k_max` for γ > 5/2, and `λ_c ≈ ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩` for 2 < γ < 5/2 (Eq. 3).
  - **Star-graph mechanism:** for a hub of degree k_max, the hub+leaves subgraph self-sustains infection for any `λ > 1/√k_max`, acting as a reservoir that re-seeds the rest of the network → threshold vanishes as k_max→∞ for *any* network with a growing maximum degree (even Erdős–Rényi, logarithmically).
  - **SIR is different:** because hubs can only be infected once (no reinfection), the steady-state reservoir mechanism is absent. SIR obeys HMF: `λ_c^{SIR} = ⟨k⟩/[⟨k²⟩ − ⟨k⟩]`, which stays **finite** for scale-rich networks (γ > 3). General conjecture: **models with a steady state (SIS) → vanishing threshold on heterogeneous nets; models without (SIR) → finite threshold.**

- **Immunization / intervention:** Not the focus, but the corollary is sharp — because a *single* hub can sustain SIS, controlling/removing the largest-degree node(s) is disproportionately effective (motivates targeted hub protection in our setting).

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** Directly tells us (a) the threshold to test is `1/λ_max(A)`, (b) **whether misalignment is SIS-like or SIR-like is the decisive modeling question** — if a misaligned model can be re-infected/re-corrupted repeatedly (SIS), even a single persistently-misaligned "hub" model (one heavily-merged checkpoint or a popular base model) can keep the whole population endemic; if misalignment is "use-once" SIR-like, the population can stay below threshold. This sharpens our hypothesis and gives a falsifiable distinction.

---

## 4. Sanatkar, White, Natarajan, Scoglio & Garrett (2015) — *Epidemic Threshold of an SIS Model in Dynamic Switching Networks*

- **Title / Venue:** arXiv:1501.02472 [cs.SI]
- **Authors:** M. R. Sanatkar, W. N. White, B. Natarajan, C. M. Scoglio, K. A. Garrett
- **Year:** 2015
- **Key Contribution:** Extends the spectral epidemic threshold to **time-varying / switching networks**, where the adjacency matrix changes over time (the network is drawn from a set of topologies at each step). Derives the threshold via the **joint spectral radius (JSR)** of the set of system matrices, and proves a simplified form for undirected graphs. Highly relevant because in distributed fine-tuning the model-interaction graph (who merges with whom each round) changes every training round.

- **The model (discrete-time SIS):** Node infection probability update:
  `p_i(t+1) = p_i(t)(1−δ) + Σ_{j∈N_i(t)} p_j(t) β`  (Eq. 5), where δ = recovery prob, β = per-neighbor infection prob, and the neighbor set N_i(t) (i.e. the adjacency matrix A_t) **changes with t**. Matrix form:
  `P_{t+1} = M_t P_t` with **system matrix** `M_t = (1−δ)I + β A_t`.

- **The epidemic threshold result:**
  - **Theorem 1 (general dynamic/arbitrary switching):** Let M = {M_i = (1−δ)I + βA_i} be the set of system matrices. The epidemic dies out (origin asymptotically stable) iff the **joint spectral radius ρ̂(M) < 1**. (JSR = limiting max norm of all length-k products of matrices from the set.)
  - **Theorem 2 (undirected graphs — simplified):** For symmetric A_i, the JSR equals the **maximum spectral radius over the set**: `ρ̂(M) = max_i ρ(M_i)`. So the epidemic dies out iff `max_i ρ((1−δ)I + βA_i) < 1` — i.e., it suffices to check the single worst (densest-eigenvalue) topology in the switching set.
  - **Corollary 1 (static network):** recovers the classic result — epidemic dies out iff **`β/δ < 1/ρ(A)`** = `1/λ_max(A)`. Consistent with papers 1–3.
  - **Corollary 2 (periodic networks, period T):** dies out iff `ρ(∏_{i=1}^{T} [(1−δ)I + βA_i]) < 1` — the spectral radius of the *product* of one full period's system matrices.
  - **Corollary 3 (dynamic regular networks, degree k):** dies out iff `β/δ < 1/k` — identical to the static regular-network threshold.

- **Immunization / intervention:** No explicit protocol, but the JSR / max-spectral-radius criterion implies the lever is to **keep every per-round topology (and especially the worst one, or the product over a period) below the spectral bound** — i.e., schedule/sparsify the merge graph so no round (or cumulative period) exceeds threshold.

- **Relevance to our hypothesis:** This is the paper that matches our actual setting best: the merge/federated-aggregation graph is **not static** — it switches every round. It tells us the right object to compute is the **joint spectral radius of the sequence of round-wise interaction matrices** (or the spectral radius of the per-period product), not just a single λ_max. For undirected merge graphs we can use the simpler `max_i ρ(M_i) < 1` test. It also confirms periodic FL schedules reduce to a product-of-matrices criterion.

---

# Synthesis — Mapping Epidemiology onto Misalignment Propagation in Distributed LLM Fine-Tuning

The four papers give a single, consistent quantitative theory. We map each epidemic primitive onto the LLM-population setting:

| Epidemic quantity | Symbol | LLM-population analogue |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Infection rate** | **β** | Per-interaction probability that misalignment transfers when a clean model absorbs weights from a misaligned one — i.e., the *transmissibility* of misaligned parameters/behavior through a single merge, task-arithmetic addition, federated-aggregation round, or model-on-model distillation. Higher β = larger merge weight given to the misaligned model, more aligned parameter directions, stronger persona/feature entanglement. |
| **(b) Recovery / healing rate** | **μ (or δ)** | The **alignment-intervention rate**: probability per round that a misaligned model is "cured" — safety re-alignment / re-fine-tuning on safe data, safety-arithmetic / TIES-style safety preservation, safe-merge projection, or detection-and-quarantine of a corrupted checkpoint. `1/μ` = mean time a model stays misaligned before being patched. |
| **(c) Contact network** | **A (or A_t)** | The **model-interaction graph**: nodes = models/checkpoints in the population; an edge i–j means i and j share/merge weights (model soups, task vectors, FL aggregation, distillation). Degree = how many other models a given checkpoint merges with. **Hubs** = popular base models or heavily-reused checkpoints. In distributed fine-tuning this graph **switches per round** → use the time-varying matrix A_t (Paper 4). |
| **(d) Epidemic threshold** | **τ_c = λ_c** | The critical effective transmission ratio. Misalignment self-extinguishes below it, becomes endemic above it. Set by the **spectral radius of the interaction graph**. |
| **(e) Basic reproduction number** | **R₀** | Expected number of *new* models a single misaligned model corrupts before it is re-aligned. `R₀ = (β/μ)·λ_max(A)`. Endemic/epidemic iff R₀ > 1. |

### Concrete threshold formula to test in simulation

**Static / single-round merge graph (Papers 1–3, Cor. 1 of Paper 4):**

$$\boxed{\ \frac{\beta}{\mu} > \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}(A)} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\mu}\,\lambda_{\max}(A) > 1\ }$$

- Below it (`β/μ < 1/λ_max`): misalignment **self-extinguishes** — the population heals faster than corruption spreads.
- Above it: misalignment becomes **endemic** (SIS-like, persistent) or causes a full **epidemic** (SIR-like, one-time sweep).
- Rigorous bracket from Paper 2 to validate against: `1/λ_max(A) ≤ τ_c ≤ 1/[d_min(1−ε_G)]` (the mean-field value `1/λ_max` is a *lower bound* on the true stochastic threshold — i.e. somewhat conservative/pessimistic).

**Time-varying / per-round federated schedule (Paper 4 — the realistic case):** replace `λ_max(A)` with the joint spectral radius of the per-round system matrices `M_t = (1−μ)I + β A_t`:

$$\text{misalignment dies out} \iff \hat\rho(\{M_t\}) < 1,\quad M_t = (1-\mu)I + \beta A_t.$$

For undirected merge graphs this simplifies (Thm 2) to `max_t ρ((1−μ)I + β A_t) < 1` — check the single densest round. For a periodic FL schedule of period T (Cor. 2): `ρ(∏_{t=1}^{T} M_t) < 1`.

### Modeling decisions this pillar forces on us
1. **SIS vs SIR is the pivotal choice (Paper 3).** If a healed model can be re-corrupted in later rounds → **SIS** → on heterogeneous (hub-dominated) merge graphs the threshold *vanishes* and even one persistently-misaligned hub keeps the population endemic. If corruption is effectively one-shot per model → **SIR** → finite threshold, population can stay safe. Our simulations should run both and report which regime emergent misalignment empirically obeys.
2. **Heterogeneity is dangerous.** Scale-free merge graphs (a few super-popular base models) have `λ_c → ⟨k⟩/⟨k²⟩ → 0`: misalignment spreads for almost any β. Realistic model-sharing ecosystems are hub-dominated, so the default prediction is "endemic unless actively defended."
3. **Intervention design (Paper 1).** Random re-alignment of models is inefficient (`g_c ≈ 1`); **targeted re-alignment of hub models** (most-merged checkpoints) is exponentially more efficient (`g_c ≈ e^{−2/(mλ)}`). **Acquaintance-style** intervention (patch a random *neighbor* of a random model) is a practical, structure-light heuristic that preferentially protects hubs. The operational lever in all cases: **lower the spectral radius λ_max of the merge graph** (sparsify/quarantine high-degree corrupt models) to push `1/λ_max` above the operating `β/μ`.

### Simulation plan implied
Build the model-interaction graph (or per-round sequence A_t), estimate β (misalignment transfer per merge) and μ (re-alignment rate) empirically from the merging/FL pillar, compute `R₀ = (β/μ)λ_max(A)` (or the JSR), and verify the phase transition at `R₀ = 1`. Then test immunization: re-align a fraction g of nodes by degree-targeted vs. random vs. acquaintance selection and measure the shift in `λ_c(g)` / endemic prevalence.

---

**Files read (all under `/workspaces/epidemiological_modeling_of_mi_20260626_012502_5c899dff/papers/`):**
- `1408.2701_epidemic_processes_complex_networks_pastorsatorras2015.pdf`
- `1402.1731_exact_markovian_sir_sis_epidemic_threshold2014.pdf`
- `1010.1646_thresholds_epidemic_spreading_networks2010.pdf`
- `1501.02472_epidemic_threshold_dynamic_switching_networks2015.pdf`

(Note: PDF page-image rendering via the Read tool was unavailable — poppler not installable without root — so I extracted text with pypdf and read all relevant equation-bearing sections directly. All threshold formulas above were verified against the source text.)
I have thorough notes from all three pillars. Now let me gather code repositories and datasets. I'll clone the key lightweight repos and download the actual "infection payload" datasets in parallel.
